### 27. G89 Codes as Principal Diagnosis
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Pain Coding and Classification  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for assigning G89 codes as principal diagnoses in pain control scenarios.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `149`

> **Actual Text**
>
> - Category G89 Codes as Principal or First-Listed Diagnosis Category G89 codes are acceptable as principal diagnosis or the first-listed code:
>
> - When pain control or pain management is the reason for the admission/encounter (e.g., a patient with displaced intervertebral disc, nerve impingement and severe back pain presents for injection of steroid into the spinal canal). The underlying cause of the pain should be reported as an additional diagnosis, if known.
> - When a patient is admitted for the insertion of a neurostimulator for pain control, assign the appropriate pain code as the principal or first-listed diagnosis. When an admission or encounter is for a procedure aimed at treating the underlying condition and a neurostimulator is inserted for pain control during the same admission/encounter, a code for the underlying condition should be assigned as the principal diagnosis and the appropriate pain code should be assigned as a secondary diagnosis.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** G89 Codes as Principal Diagnosis

**Key Behaviors:**
- Category G89 codes are acceptable as principal or first-listed diagnosis when pain control or pain management is the reason for the admission/encounter.
- When a patient is admitted for the insertion of a neurostimulator for pain control, assign the appropriate pain code as the principal or first-listed diagnosis.
- When an admission is for a procedure aimed at treating the underlying condition and a neurostimulator is inserted for pain control, assign the code for the underlying condition as the principal diagnosis and the pain code as a secondary diagnosis.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The underlying cause of the pain should be reported as an additional diagnosis if known when pain control is the reason for admission.
- The procedure must be aimed at treating the underlying condition for the underlying condition to be assigned as the principal diagnosis in neurostimulator cases.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `579`

> **Actual Text**
>
> - Category G89 Codes as Principal or First-Listed Diagnosis
>
> Category G89 codes are acceptable as principal diagnosis or the first-listed code:
>
> - When pain control or pain management is the reason for the admission/encounter (e.g., a patient with displaced intervertebral disc, nerve impingement and severe back pain presents for injection of steroid into the spinal canal). The underlying cause of the pain should be reported as an additional diagnosis, if known.
> - When a patient is admitted for the insertion of a neurostimulator for pain control, assign the appropriate pain code as the principal or first-listed diagnosis. When an admission or encounter is for a procedure aimed at treating the underlying condition and a neurostimulator is inserted for pain control during the same admission/encounter, a code for the underlying condition should be assigned as the principal diagnosis and the appropriate pain code should be assigned as a secondary diagnosis.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** G89 Codes as Principal Diagnosis

**Key Behaviors:**
- G89 codes are acceptable as principal diagnosis when pain control is the reason for admission.
- Underlying cause of pain must be reported as an additional diagnosis if known.
- Assign pain code as principal when admission is for neurostimulator insertion for pain control.
- Assign underlying condition code as principal when procedure treats underlying condition and neurostimulator is inserted for pain control during same encounter.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Pain control or pain management must be the reason for the admission/encounter.
- Underlying cause of pain must be documented and known to be reported as an additional diagnosis.
- Procedure must be aimed at treating the underlying condition for the underlying code to be principal.

### 28. Code G89.3 Neoplasm Pain Sequencing
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Pain Coding and Classification  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical coding logic for G89.3 with verbatim matching behaviors and raw text excerpts.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `156`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code G89.3 is assigned to pain documented as being related, associated or due to cancer, primary or secondary malignancy, or tumor. This code is assigned regardless of whether the pain is acute or chronic.
>
> This code may be assigned as the principal or first-listed code when the stated reason for the admission/encounter is documented as pain control/pain management. The underlying neoplasm should be reported as an additional diagnosis.
>
> When the reason for the admission/encounter is management of the neoplasm and the pain associated with the neoplasm is also documented, code G89.3 may be assigned as an additional diagnosis. It is not necessary to assign an additional code for the site of the pain.
>
> See Section I.C.2. for instructions on the sequencing of neoplasms for all other stated reasons for the admission/encounter (except for pain control/pain management).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Code G89.3 Neoplasm Pain Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign G89.3 for pain related to cancer, primary or secondary malignancy, or tumor regardless of acute or chronic status.
- Assign G89.3 as principal or first-listed code when the reason for admission/encounter is pain control/pain management.
- Report the underlying neoplasm as an additional diagnosis when G89.3 is the principal code.
- Assign G89.3 as an additional diagnosis when the reason for admission is neoplasm management and pain is documented.
- Do not assign an additional code for the site of the pain when G89.3 is used.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Pain must be documented as related, associated, or due to cancer, primary or secondary malignancy, or tumor.
- Underlying neoplasm must be reported as an additional diagnosis if G89.3 is the principal code.
- Sequencing follows Section I.C.2. for all reasons other than pain control/pain management.

---

### 30. Glaucoma Seventh Character Coding Rules
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Glaucoma Coding Rules  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for distinguishing seventh character '4' (indeterminate) from '0' (unspecified) in glaucoma cases.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `162`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assignment of the seventh character '4' for 'indeterminate stage' should be based on the clinical documentation. The seventh character '4' is used for glaucomas whose stage cannot be clinically determined. This seventh character should not be confused with the seventh character '0', unspecified, which should be assigned when there is no documentation regarding the stage of the glaucoma.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Glaucoma Seventh Character Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Seventh character '4' is assigned when the glaucoma stage cannot be clinically determined based on clinical documentation.
- Seventh character '0' is assigned when there is no documentation regarding the stage of the glaucoma.
- Seventh character '4' must not be confused with or substituted for seventh character '0'.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Clinical documentation must explicitly indicate that the stage cannot be determined to justify character '4'.
- Absence of stage documentation requires the use of character '0' rather than '4'.

---

### 31. Sepsis Coding Sequencing Rules
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature of Sepsis Coding Sequencing Rules with specific behavioral differences in the 2025 update.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `67_70_72`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If severe sepsis is present on admission, and meets the definition of principal diagnosis, the underlying systemic infection should be assigned as principal diagnosis followed by the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2 as required by the sequencing rules in the Tabular List. A code from subcategory R65.2 can never be assigned as a principal diagnosis. When severe sepsis develops during an encounter (it was not present on admission), the underlying systemic infection and the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2 should be assigned as secondary diagnoses.
>
> Severe sepsis may be present on admission, but the diagnosis may not be confirmed until sometime after admission. If the documentation is not clear whether severe sepsis was present on admission, the provider should be queried.
>
> For infection-associated hemolytic-uremic syndrome with severe sepsis, see guideline I.C.1.d.9.
>
> For sepsis following a postprocedural wound (surgical site) infection, a code from T81.41 to T81.43, Infection following a procedure, T81.49, Infection following a procedure, other surgical site, or a code from O86.00 to O86.03, Infection of obstetric surgical wound, or code O86.09, Infection of obstetric surgical wound, other surgical site, that identifies the site of the infection should be sequenced first, if known. Assign an additional code for sepsis following a procedure (T81.44) or sepsis following an obstetrical procedure (O86.04). Use an additional code to identify the infectious agent. If the patient has severe sepsis, the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2 should also be assigned with the additional code(s) for any acute organ dysfunction.
>
> For infections following infusion, transfusion, therapeutic injection, or immunization, a code from subcategory T80.2, Infections following infusion, transfusion, and therapeutic injection, or code T88.0-, Infection following immunization, should be coded first, followed by the code for the specific infection. If the patient has severe sepsis, the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2 should also be assigned, with the additional codes(s) for any acute organ dysfunction.
>
> In some cases, a noninfectious process (condition) such as trauma, may lead to an infection which can result in sepsis or severe sepsis. If sepsis or severe sepsis is documented as associated with a noninfectious condition, such as a burn or serious injury, and this condition meets the definition for principal diagnosis, the code for the noninfectious condition should be sequenced first, followed by the code for the resulting infection. If severe sepsis is present, a code from subcategory R65.2 should also be assigned with any associated organ dysfunction(s) codes. It is not necessary to assign a code from subcategory R65.1, Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) of non-infectious origin, for these cases.
>
> If the infection meets the definition of principal diagnosis, it should be sequenced before the non-infectious condition. When both the associated non-infectious condition and the infection meet the definition of principal diagnosis, either may be assigned as principal diagnosis.
>
> Only one code from category R65, Symptoms and signs specifically associated with systemic inflammation and infection, should be assigned. Therefore, when a non-infectious condition leads to an infection resulting in severe sepsis, assign the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis. Do not additionally assign a code from subcategory R65.1, Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) of noninfectious origin.
>
> See Section I.C.18. SIRS due to non-infectious process

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Sepsis Coding Sequencing and R65.2 Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- Underlying systemic infection sequenced first if severe sepsis is present on admission and meets principal diagnosis definition.
- Code from subcategory R65.2 can never be assigned as a principal diagnosis.
- If severe sepsis develops during an encounter, underlying infection and R65.2 code are assigned as secondary diagnoses.
- For infection following a procedure, the procedure-related infection code is sequenced first, followed by sepsis code (T81.44) and R65.2 if severe.
- Only one code from category R65 should be assigned; do not additionally assign R65.1 for non-infectious origin cases.
- For sepsis following a postprocedural wound, assign a code from T81.41-T81.43, T81.49, O86.00-O86.03, or O86.09 to identify the specific site of infection before assigning the sepsis code.
- For infections following infusion, transfusion, therapeutic injection, or immunization, assign a code from subcategory T80.2 or T88.0- first, followed by the code for the specific infection.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Severe sepsis must meet the definition of principal diagnosis to sequence the underlying infection first.
- Provider must be queried if documentation is unclear whether severe sepsis was present on admission.
- Code from subcategory R65.2 must be assigned with additional codes for any acute organ dysfunction.
- For non-infectious conditions leading to infection, the non-infectious condition code is sequenced first if it meets principal diagnosis definition.
- If both non-infectious condition and infection meet principal diagnosis definition, either may be assigned as principal diagnosis.
- If documentation is not clear whether severe sepsis was present on admission, the provider must be queried before finalizing codes.
- When assigning codes for severe sepsis, additional codes must be assigned for any acute organ dysfunction present.

---

>

If sepsis or severe sepsis is documented as associated with a noninfectious condition, such as a burn or serious injury, and this condition meets the definition for principal diagnosis, the code for the noninfectious condition should be sequenced first, followed by the code for the resulting infection. If severe sepsis is present, a code from subcategory R65.2 should also be assigned with any associated organ dysfunction(s) codes. It is not necessary to assign a code from subcategory R65.1, Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) of non-infectious origin, for these cases.
>

>
> Only one code from category R65, Symptoms and signs specifically associated with systemic inflammation and infection, should be assigned. Therefore, when a non-infectious condition leads to an infection resulting in severe sepsis, assign the appropriate code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis. Do not additionally assign a code from subcategory R65.1, Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) of noninfectious origin.
>
> See Section I.C.18. SIRS due to non-infectious process
>
> - 7) Sepsis and septic shock complicating abortion, pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium
>
> See Section I.C.15. Sepsis and septic shock complicating abortion, pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium
>
> - 8) Newborn sepsis
>
> See Section I.C.16. f. Bacterial sepsis of Newborn
>
> - 9) Hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with sepsis
>
> If the reason for admission is hemolytic-uremic syndrome that is associated with sepsis, assign code D59.31, Infection-associated hemolytic-uremic syndrome, as the principal diagnosis. Codes for the underlying systemic infection and any other conditions (such as severe sepsis) should be assigned as secondary diagnoses .

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Sepsis Coding Sequencing and R65.2 Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- Underlying systemic infection sequenced first if severe sepsis is present on admission and meets principal diagnosis definition.
- Code from subcategory R65.2 can never be assigned as a principal diagnosis.
- If severe sepsis develops during an encounter, underlying infection and R65.2 code are assigned as secondary diagnoses.
- For infection following a procedure, the procedure-related infection code is sequenced first, followed by sepsis code and R65.2 if severe.
- Only one code from category R65 should be assigned; do not additionally assign R65.1 for non-infectious origin SIRS when severe sepsis is present.
- For infection-associated hemolytic-uremic syndrome with severe sepsis, assign code D59.31 as the principal diagnosis.
- When severe sepsis complicates abortion, pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium, refer to specific section I.C.15 guidelines.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Severe sepsis must meet the definition of principal diagnosis to sequence underlying infection first.
- Provider must be queried if documentation is unclear whether severe sepsis was present on admission.
- Code from subcategory R65.2 must be assigned with additional codes for any acute organ dysfunction.
- For non-infectious conditions leading to infection, the non-infectious condition code is sequenced first if it meets principal diagnosis definition.
- If infection meets principal diagnosis definition, it should be sequenced before the non-infectious condition.
- For postprocedural wound infections, assign the site-specific infection code (T81.41-T81.43, T81.49, O86.00-O86.03) first, followed by the sepsis following procedure code (T81.44 or O86.04).
- For infections following infusion, transfusion, therapeutic injection, or immunization, assign the code from subcategory T80.2 or T88.0- first, followed by the specific infection code.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Rule Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 guideline provided general instructions for sequencing postprocedural infections but lacked specific code references for the sepsis component in that context. The October 2025 version clarifies this by explicitly mandating the use of code T81.44 for sepsis following a procedure, whereas the older version only listed the infection codes. Additionally, the 2025 version introduces a specific principal diagnosis rule for infection-associated hemolytic-uremic syndrome (D59.31), which was absent in the older text.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): For sepsis following a postprocedural wound, assign a code from T81.41-T81.43, T81.49, O86.00-O86.03, or O86.09 to identify the specific site of infection before assigning the sepsis code. -> October 2025 (New Version): For postprocedural wound infections, assign the site-specific infection code (T81.41-T81.43, T81.49, O86.00-O86.03) first, followed by the sepsis following procedure code (T81.44 or O86.04).
- October 1, 2024 (Older): No specific principal diagnosis instruction for infection-associated hemolytic-uremic syndrome with severe sepsis. -> October 2025 (New Version): For infection-associated hemolytic-uremic syndrome with severe sepsis, assign code D59.31 as the principal diagnosis.

*Traceability: Chunk `67_70_72` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `495_498_500` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 32. Burn Injury Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same Burn Injury Coding Guidelines with identical logic for sequencing and specific coding rules for non-healing burns.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `292_294`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Sequence first the code that reflects the highest degree of burn when more than one burn is present.
>
> - a. When the reason for the admission or encounter is for treatment of external multiple burns, sequence first the code that reflects the burn of the highest degree.
> - b. When a patient has both internal and external burns, the circumstances of admission govern the selection of the principal diagnosis or first-listed diagnosis.
> - c. When a patient is admitted for burn injuries and other related conditions such as smoke inhalation and/or respiratory failure, the circumstances of admission govern the selection of the principal or first-listed diagnosis.
>
> Non-healing burns are coded as acute burns.
>
> Necrosis of burned skin should be coded as a non-healed burn.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Burn Injury Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Sequence first the code that reflects the highest degree of burn when more than one burn is present.
- When a patient has both internal and external burns, the circumstances of admission govern the selection of the principal diagnosis.
- When a patient is admitted for burn injuries and other related conditions such as smoke inhalation, the circumstances of admission govern the selection of the principal diagnosis.
- Non-healing burns are coded as acute burns.
- Necrosis of burned skin should be coded as a non-healed burn.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must indicate the highest degree of burn when multiple burns are present.
- Circumstances of admission must be documented to determine principal diagnosis for internal/external burns or related conditions.
- Condition must be documented as non-healing to be coded as acute burn.
- Necrosis of burned skin must be explicitly documented to be coded as non-healed burn.

### 33. MRSA Infection and Carrier Coding
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for MRSA infections and carrier status with no substantive changes in behavior.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `76_78`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When there is documentation of a current infection (e.g., wound infection, stitch abscess, urinary tract infection) due to MRSA, and that infection does not have a combination code that includes the causal organism, assign the appropriate code to identify the condition along with code B95.62, Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus infection as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere for the MRSA infection. Do not assign a code from subcategory Z16.11, Resistance to penicillins.
>
> If a patient is documented as having both MRSA colonization and infection during a hospital admission, code Z22.322, Carrier or suspected carrier of Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus, and a code for the MRSA infection may both be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** MRSA Infection and Carrier Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign appropriate infection code with B95.62 as the cause of disease when no combination code exists.
- Do not assign codes from subcategory Z16.11 for MRSA infections.
- Assign both Z22.322 and the MRSA infection code if both colonization and infection are documented during hospital admission.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must confirm a current infection due to MRSA.
- The infection must lack a combination code that includes the causal organism.
- Both colonization and infection must be explicitly documented during the same hospital admission to assign Z22.322 alongside the infection code.

**Superseded Guidance:**
- Assignment of codes from subcategory Z16.11 for MRSA infections.

---

> **Actual Text**
>

Do not assign a code from subcategory Z16.11, Resistance to penicillins.
>

- The specific infection must lack a combination code that includes the causal organism.

- Assignment of codes from subcategory Z16.11 for MRSA infections.

**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines

> **Actual Text**
>
> Septic shock generally refers to circulatory failure associated with severe sepsis, and therefore, it represents a type of acute organ dysfunction.
>
> For cases of septic shock, the code for the systemic infection should be sequenced first, followed by code R65.21, Severe sepsis with septic shock or code T81.12, Postprocedural septic shock. Any additional codes for the other acute organ dysfunctions should also be assigned. As noted in the sequencing instructions in the Tabular List, the code for septic shock cannot be assigned as a principal diagnosis.
>
> If a postprocedural infection has resulted in postprocedural septic shock, assign the codes indicated above for sepsis due to a postprocedural infection, followed by code T81.12-, Postprocedural septic shock. Do not assign code R65.21, Severe sepsis with septic shock. Additional code(s) should be assigned for any acute organ dysfunction.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Septic Shock Coding Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Systemic infection code must be sequenced first for septic shock cases
- Code R65.21 or T81.12 follows the infection code depending on etiology
- Additional codes for acute organ dysfunctions must be assigned
- Septic shock code cannot be assigned as a principal diagnosis
- For postprocedural septic shock, assign infection code followed by T81.12-

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must indicate circulatory failure associated with severe sepsis
- Systemic infection must be identified to sequence first
- Acute organ dysfunction must be documented to justify additional codes
- Postprocedural infection must be confirmed to use T81.12 instead of R65.21
- Principal diagnosis cannot be a septic shock code

### 35. Sequela and Late Effect Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Sequela and Late Effect Coding Guidelines with no substantive changes in logic or behavior between versions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `37_334`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A sequela is the residual effect (condition produced) after the acute phase of an illness or injury has terminated. There is no time limit on when a sequela code can be used. The residual may be apparent early, such as in cerebral infarction, or it may occur months or years later, such as that due to a previous injury. Examples of sequela include: scar formation resulting from a burn, deviated septum due to a nasal fracture, and infertility due to tubal occlusion from old tuberculosis. Coding of sequela generally requires two codes sequenced in the following order: the condition or nature of the sequela is sequenced first. The sequela code is sequenced second.
>
> An exception to the above guidelines are those instances where the code for the sequela is followed by a manifestation code identified in the Tabular List and title, or the sequela code has been expanded (at the fourth, fifth or sixth character levels) to include the manifestation(s). The code for the acute phase of an illness or injury that led to the sequela is never used with a code for the late effect.
>
> See Section I.C.9. Sequelae of cerebrovascular disease
>
> See Section I.C.15. Sequelae of complication of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium
>
> See Section I.C.19. Application of 7 th characters for Chapter 19
>
> Sequela are reported using the external cause code with the 7 th character 'S' for sequela. These codes should be used with any report of a late effect or sequela resulting from a previous injury.
>
> See Section I.B.10. Sequela (Late Effects)
>
> - Sequela external cause code with a related current injury A sequela external cause code should never be used with a related current nature of injury code.
> - Use of sequela external cause codes for subsequent visits
>
> Use a late effect external cause code for subsequent visits when a late effect of the initial injury is being treated. Do not use a late effect external cause code for subsequent visits for follow-up care (e.g., to assess healing, to receive rehabilitative therapy) of the injury when no late effect of the injury has been documented.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Sequela and Late Effect Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Sequela codes are used for residual effects after the acute phase of an illness or injury has terminated, with no time limit on usage.
- Coding generally requires two codes sequenced in the following order: the condition or nature of the sequela is sequenced first, followed by the sequela code.
- Sequela external cause codes must use the 7th character 'S' and should be used with any report of a late effect or sequela resulting from a previous injury.
- A sequela external cause code should never be used with a related current nature of injury code.
- Late effect external cause codes should be used for subsequent visits when a late effect of the initial injury is being treated.
- Sequela codes may be expanded at the fourth, fifth, or sixth character levels to include specific manifestations.
- If the sequela code is followed by a manifestation code identified in the Tabular List and title, the manifestation code takes precedence.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The acute phase of an illness or injury that led to the sequela is never used with a code for the late effect.
- If the sequela code is followed by a manifestation code identified in the Tabular List, the manifestation code takes precedence.
- Late effect external cause codes must not be used for subsequent visits for follow-up care (e.g., to assess healing, to receive rehabilitative therapy) when no late effect of the injury has been documented.
- The condition or nature of the sequela must be sequenced first before the sequela code.
- Sequela codes may be expanded at the fourth, fifth, or sixth character levels to include specific manifestations.
- Sequela external cause codes must use the 7th character 'S'.
- A sequela external cause code should never be used with a related current nature of injury code.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `469`

Examples of sequela include: scar formation resulting from a burn, deviated septum due to a nasal fracture, and infertility due to tubal occlusion from old tuberculosis. Coding of sequela generally requires two codes sequenced in the following order: the condition or nature of the sequela is sequenced first. The sequela code is sequenced second.

- Two codes must be assigned: one for the nature of the sequela and one for the sequela itself.

- The sequela code must be sequenced second in the claim.

### 36. Severe Sepsis Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles describe the Severe Sepsis Coding Guidelines, detailing the requirement for two codes and specific handling of unspecified organisms.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `64_65`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For a diagnosis of sepsis, assign the appropriate code for the underlying systemic infection. If the type of infection or causal organism is not further specified, assign code A41.9, Sepsis, unspecified organism.
>
> A code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis, should not be assigned unless severe sepsis or an associated acute organ dysfunction is documented.
>
> - (i) Negative or inconclusive blood cultures and sepsis Negative or inconclusive blood cultures do not preclude a diagnosis of sepsis in patients with clinical evidence of the condition; however, the provider should be queried.
> - (ii) Urosepsis
>
> The term urosepsis is a nonspecific term. It is not to be considered synonymous with sepsis. It has no default code in the Alphabetic Index. Should a provider use this term, he/she must be queried for clarification.
>
> - (iii) Sepsis with organ dysfunction
>
> If a patient has sepsis and associated acute organ dysfunction or multiple organ dysfunction (MOD), follow the instructions for coding severe sepsis.
>
> - (iv) Acute organ dysfunction that is not clearly associated with the sepsis
>
> If a patient has sepsis and an acute organ dysfunction, but the medical record documentation indicates that the acute organ dysfunction is related to a medical condition other than the sepsis, do not assign a code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis. An acute organ dysfunction must be associated with the sepsis in order to assign the severe sepsis code. If the documentation is not clear as to whether an acute organ dysfunction is related to the sepsis or another medical condition, query the provider.
>
> The coding of severe sepsis requires a minimum of 2 codes: first a code for the underlying systemic infection, followed by a code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis. If the causal organism is not documented, assign code A41.9, Sepsis, unspecified organism, for the infection. Additional code(s) for the associated acute organ dysfunction are also required.
>
> Due to the complex nature of severe sepsis, some cases may require querying the provider prior to assignment of the codes.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Severe Sepsis Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code for underlying systemic infection first, followed by code from subcategory R65.2 for severe sepsis.
- Do not assign severe sepsis code unless severe sepsis or associated acute organ dysfunction is explicitly documented.
- Query provider if blood cultures are negative/inconclusive but clinical evidence of sepsis exists.
- Query provider if the term 'urosepsis' is used to clarify if it is synonymous with sepsis.
- Query provider if acute organ dysfunction is not clearly associated with the sepsis.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must explicitly state severe sepsis or an associated acute organ dysfunction.
- Minimum of 2 codes required: one for underlying systemic infection and one from subcategory R65.2.
- If causal organism is not documented, assign code A41.9 for the infection.
- Additional codes for associated acute organ dysfunction are required.
- Acute organ dysfunction must be clearly associated with the sepsis to assign severe sepsis code.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `493`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The coding of severe sepsis requires a minimum of 2 codes: first a code for the underlying systemic infection, followed by a code from subcategory R65.2, Severe sepsis. If the causal organism is not documented, assign code A41.9, Sepsis, unspecified organism, for the infection. Additional code(s) for the associated acute organ dysfunction are also required.
>
> Due to the complex nature of severe sepsis, some cases may require querying the provider prior to assignment of the codes.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Severe Sepsis Coding Requirements

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign a minimum of 2 codes: one for the underlying systemic infection and one from subcategory R65.2 for severe sepsis.
- Use code A41.9 for the infection if the causal organism is not documented.
- Include additional codes for any associated acute organ dysfunction.
- Query the provider if the complex nature of the case requires clarification before code assignment.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation of an underlying systemic infection is mandatory.
- Documentation of acute organ dysfunction is required for additional coding.
- If the causal organism is unspecified, code A41.9 must be used for the infection component.

---

The older version provided specific, actionable instructions for querying providers regarding negative blood cultures and the term 'urosepsis'. The newer version generalizes these instructions into a single statement about querying for the 'complex nature of the case', removing the specific clinical criteria for when a query is mandatory.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Query provider if blood cultures are negative/inconclusive but clinical evidence of sepsis exists. -> October 2025 (New Version): Query the provider if the complex nature of the case requires clarification before code assignment.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Query provider if the term 'urosepsis' is used to clarify if it is synonymous with sepsis. -> October 2025 (New Version): No specific instruction for 'urosepsis' term; covered under general complexity query.

*Traceability: Chunk `64_65` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `493` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 39. Sepsis and Localized Infection Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical sequencing rules for sepsis and localized infection coding with no behavioral changes.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `68`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If the reason for admission is sepsis or severe sepsis and a localized infection, such as pneumonia or cellulitis, a code(s) for the underlying systemic infection should be assigned first and the code for the localized infection should be assigned as a secondary diagnosis. If the patient has severe sepsis, a code from subcategory R65.2 should also be assigned as a secondary diagnosis. If the patient is admitted with a localized infection, such as pneumonia, and sepsis/severe sepsis doesn't develop until after admission, the localized infection should be assigned first, followed by the appropriate sepsis/severe sepsis codes.
>
> For hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with sepsis, see guideline I.C.1.d.9 .

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Sepsis and Localized Infection Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code for underlying systemic infection first if admission is for sepsis/severe sepsis with localized infection
- Assign localized infection code as secondary diagnosis when sepsis is the primary reason for admission
- Assign severe sepsis code from subcategory R65.2 as secondary diagnosis if patient has severe sepsis
- Assign localized infection code first if sepsis/severe sepsis develops after admission
- Refer to guideline I.C.1.d.9 for hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with sepsis

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Determine if sepsis/severe sepsis was the reason for admission versus a condition developing after admission
- Verify presence of localized infection such as pneumonia or cellulitis
- Confirm severity level (sepsis vs severe sepsis) to determine if R65.2 code is required
- Ensure correct sequencing based on temporal relationship between infection and sepsis onset
- Check for specific guideline references for associated syndromes like hemolytic-uremic syndrome

---

If the patient has severe sepsis, a code from subcategory R65.2 should also be assigned as a secondary diagnosis. If the patient is admitted with a localized infection, such as pneumonia, and sepsis/severe sepsis doesn't develop until after admission, the localized infection should be assigned first, followed by the appropriate sepsis/severe sepsis codes.
>
> For hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with sepsis, see guideline I.C.1.d.9 .

- Assign code for localized infection as secondary diagnosis when sepsis is the primary admission reason
- Assign code from subcategory R65.2 as secondary diagnosis if patient has severe sepsis
- Assign localized infection code first if sepsis/severe sepsis develops after admission
- Refer to guideline I.C.1.d.9 for hemolytic-uremic syndrome associated with sepsis

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Underlying systemic infection must be identified to assign code first when sepsis is the admission reason
- Severe sepsis requires assignment of a code from subcategory R65.2 as a secondary diagnosis
- Localized infection must be documented as the primary condition if sepsis develops post-admission

### 41. MRSA Combination Code Assignment
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for MRSA combination codes with no substantive behavioral changes between versions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `75`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a patient is diagnosed with an infection that is due to methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), and that infection has a combination code that includes the causal organism (e.g., sepsis, pneumonia) assign the appropriate combination code for the condition (e.g., code A41.02, Sepsis due to Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus or code J15.212, Pneumonia due to Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus). Do not assign code B95.62, Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus infection as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, as an additional code, because the combination code includes the type of infection and the MRSA organism. Do not assign a code from subcategory Z16.11, Resistance to penicillins, as an additional diagnosis.
>
> See Section C.1. for instructions on coding and sequencing of sepsis and severe sepsis.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** MRSA Combination Code Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign the appropriate combination code for the condition (e.g., sepsis, pneumonia) that includes the causal organism.
- Do not assign code B95.62 (Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus infection) as an additional code because the combination code includes the organism.
- Do not assign a code from subcategory Z16.11 (Resistance to penicillins) as an additional diagnosis.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The infection must be documented as due to methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA).
- The condition must have a combination code that includes the causal organism.
- Follow Section C.1. for instructions on coding and sequencing of sepsis and severe sepsis.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `501`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a patient is diagnosed with an infection that is due to methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), and that infection has a combination code that includes the causal organism (e.g., sepsis, pneumonia) assign the appropriate combination code for the condition (e.g., code A41.02, Sepsis due to Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus or code J15.212, Pneumonia due to Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus). Do not assign code B95.62, Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus infection as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, as an additional code, because the combination code includes the type of infection and the MRSA organism. Do not assign a code from subcategory Z16.11, Resistance to penicillins, as an additional diagnosis.
>
> See Section C.1. for instructions on coding and sequencing of sepsis and severe sepsis.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** MRSA Combination Code Assignment

- Do not assign code B95.62 (Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus infection) as an additional code when a combination code is used.
- Do not assign a code from subcategory Z16.11 (Resistance to penicillins) as an additional diagnosis.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The infection must be documented as due to methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA).
- The condition must have a combination code available that includes the causal organism.

### 43. Puerperal Sepsis Coding Guidelines

*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for Puerperal Sepsis (Code O85) regarding organism assignment and exclusions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `235`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code O85, Puerperal sepsis, should be assigned with a secondary code to identify the causal organism (e.g., for a bacterial infection, assign a code from category B95-B96, Bacterial infections in conditions classified elsewhere). A code from category A40, Streptococcal sepsis, or A41, Other sepsis, should not be used for puerperal sepsis. If applicable, use additional codes to identify severe sepsis (R65.2-) and any associated acute organ dysfunction.
>
> Code O85 should not be assigned for sepsis following an obstetrical procedure (See Section I.C.1.d.5.b., Sepsis due to a postprocedural infection).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Puerperal Sepsis Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code O85 with a secondary code to identify the causal organism (e.g., B95-B96).
- Do not use codes from category A40 or A41 for puerperal sepsis.
- Use additional codes to identify severe sepsis (R65.2-) and associated acute organ dysfunction if applicable.
- Do not assign code O85 for sepsis following an obstetrical procedure.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Secondary code must identify the causal organism.
- Causal organism codes must be from category B95-B96.
- Codes from category A40 or A41 must not be used for puerperal sepsis.
- Severe sepsis and acute organ dysfunction must be coded separately if applicable.

### 44. COVID-19 Principal Diagnosis Sequencing Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical sequencing logic for U07.1 with the same specific exceptions and section references.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `81`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When COVID-19 meets the definition of principal diagnosis, code U07.1, COVID-19, should be sequenced first, followed by the appropriate codes for associated manifestations, except when another guideline requires that certain codes be sequenced first, such as obstetrics, sepsis, or transplant complications.
>
> For a COVID-19 infection that progresses to sepsis, see Section I.C.1.d. Sepsis, Severe Sepsis, and Septic Shock
>
> See Section I.C.15.s. for COVID-19 infection in pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium
>
> See Section I.C.16.h. for COVID-19 infection in newborn
>
> For a COVID-19 infection in a lung transplant patient, see Section I.C.19.g.3.a. Transplant complications other than kidney.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Principal Diagnosis Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code U07.1 (COVID-19) should be sequenced first when it meets the definition of principal diagnosis.
- Associated manifestations are sequenced after U07.1 unless other specific guidelines require different sequencing.
- Obstetrics, sepsis, and transplant complications take precedence over U07.1 sequencing when applicable.
- Refer to specific sections for COVID-19 in pregnancy, newborns, and lung transplant patients.
- Refer to Section I.C.1.d for specific sequencing rules when COVID-19 progresses to sepsis.
- Refer to Section I.C.15.s for specific sequencing rules for COVID-19 in pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium.
- Refer to Section I.C.16.h for specific sequencing rules for COVID-19 infection in newborns.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- COVID-19 must meet the definition of principal diagnosis to be sequenced first.
- Other specific guidelines (e.g., obstetrics, sepsis) must be checked before sequencing U07.1.

>

### 45. Additional Organism Code Assignment
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Sepsis and Burn Coding Guidelines

*Both profiles describe the identical logic for assigning additional organism codes (B95-B97) when primary infection codes lack an inherent agent.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `62`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Certain infections are classified in chapters other than Chapter 1 and no organism is identified as part of the infection code. In these instances, it is necessary to use an additional code from Chapter 1 to identify the organism. A code from category B95, Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and Enterococcus as the cause of diseases classified to other chapters, B96, Other bacterial agents as the cause of diseases classified to other chapters, or B97, Viral agents as the cause of diseases classified to other chapters, is to be used as an additional code to identify the organism. An instructional note will be found at the infection code advising that an additional organism code is required.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Additional Organism Code Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- Use an additional code from Chapter 1 to identify the organism when the primary infection code does not include the organism.
- Select codes from categories B95, B96, or B97 based on the specific type of bacterial or viral agent identified.
- Follow the instructional note found at the primary infection code which advises that an additional organism code is required.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The infection must be classified in a chapter other than Chapter 1.
- No organism is identified as part of the primary infection code.
- The specific organism must be documented to justify the assignment of a code from categories B95, B96, or B97.

---

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `172_173`

> **Actual Text**
>
> This diagnostic statement usually refers to an existing state of hypertension under control by therapy. Assign the appropriate code from categories I10 -I15, Hypertensive diseases.
>
> Uncontrolled hypertension may refer to untreated hypertension or hypertension not responding to current therapeutic regimen. In either case, assign the appropriate code from categories I10 -I15, Hypertensive diseases.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Hypertensive Disease Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign appropriate codes from categories I10-I15 for existing hypertension under control by therapy.
- Assign appropriate codes from categories I10-I15 for uncontrolled hypertension, whether untreated or not responding to therapy.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Diagnosis must refer to an existing state of hypertension.
- Diagnosis must be either under control by therapy or uncontrolled (untreated/not responding).
- Code selection must be from categories I10-I15.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `593_600_601`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Hypertension with heart conditions classified to I50.-, Heart failure, I51.4, Myocarditis, unspecified, I51.89, Other ill-defined heart diseases, and I51.9, Heart disease, unspecified, is assigned to a code from category I11, Hypertensive heart disease. Use additional code(s) from category I50, Heart failure, or I51, Complications and ill-defined descriptions of heart disease, to identify the heart condition.
>
> Hypertension with heart conditions classified to I51.5, Myocardial degeneration, or I51.7, Cardiomegaly, is assigned to a code from category I11, Hypertensive heart disease. No additional code is assigned to identify the specific heart condition.
>
> The same heart conditions (I50.-, I51.4-I51.7, I51.89, I51.9) with hypertension are coded separately if the provider has documented they are unrelated to the hypertension. The applicable hypertension code I10, Essential (primary) hypertension, or a code from category I15, Secondary hypertension, should be assigned. Sequence according to the circumstances of the admission/encounter.
>
> This diagnostic statement usually refers to an existing state of hypertension under control by therapy. Assign the appropriate code from categories I10 -I15, Hypertensive diseases.
>
> Uncontrolled hypertension may refer to untreated hypertension or hypertension not responding to current therapeutic regimen. In either case, assign the appropriate code from categories I10 -I15, Hypertensive diseases.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Hypertensive Disease Coding with Heart Conditions

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code from category I11 for hypertension with heart conditions classified to I50.-, I51.4, I51.89, or I51.9, using additional codes from I50 or I51 to identify the heart condition.
- Assign code from category I11 for hypertension with heart conditions classified to I51.5 or I51.7 without assigning an additional code for the specific heart condition.
- Code heart conditions (I50.-, I51.4-I51.9) and hypertension separately if the provider documents they are unrelated, sequencing based on admission circumstances.
- Assign appropriate codes from categories I10-I15 for diagnostic statements referring to existing hypertension under control by therapy.
- Assign appropriate codes from categories I10-I15 for uncontrolled hypertension, whether untreated or not responding to therapy.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider must document that heart conditions are unrelated to hypertension to justify separate coding.
- Provider must document uncertainty or specific conditions (e.g., myocardial degeneration) to determine if additional codes are required.
- Coding must follow sequence rules based on the circumstances of the admission or encounter when conditions are unrelated.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Scope Expansion   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version focused strictly on assigning I10-I15 codes for hypertension that is either controlled by therapy or uncontrolled. The newer version expands this scope by introducing specific rules for coding hypertension combined with heart conditions (I50.-, I51.4, etc.), distinguishing between cases requiring additional codes and those where the heart condition is subsumed under I11. While the core logic for simple controlled/uncontrolled hypertension remains identical in both versions, the addition of complex comorbidity rules represents a significant expansion of the guideline's application.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Guidelines only covered assigning I10-I15 for controlled or uncontrolled hypertension without addressing comorbid heart conditions. -> October 2025 (New Version): Guidelines now explicitly define rules for assigning I11 for hypertension with specific heart conditions (I50.-, I51.4, etc.) and separate coding when conditions are unrelated.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): No distinction was made regarding whether heart conditions required additional coding. -> October 2025 (New Version): Specific instructions are added to use additional codes from I50 or I51 for certain heart conditions, while omitting them for others like myocardial degeneration.

*Traceability: Chunk `172_173` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `593_600_601` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 47. Diabetes Mellitus Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Chronic Disease Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding Diabetes Mellitus coding guidelines with specific behavioral rules for code assignment.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `129_132_135`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The diabetes mellitus codes are combination codes that include the type of diabetes mellitus, the body system affected, and the complications affecting that body system. As many codes within a particular category as are necessary to describe all of the complications of the disease may be used. They should be sequenced based on the reason for a particular encounter. Assign as many codes from categories E08 - E13 as needed to identify all of the associated conditions that the patient has.
>
> If the type of diabetes mellitus is not documented in the medical record the default is E11.-, Type 2 diabetes mellitus.
>
> - 3) Diabetes mellitus and the use of insulin, oral hypoglycemics, and injectable non-insulin drugs
>
> If the documentation in a medical record does not indicate the type of diabetes but does indicate that the patient uses insulin, code E11-, Type 2 diabetes mellitus, should be assigned. Additional code(s) should be assigned from category Z79 to identify the long-term (current) use of insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs, or injectable non-insulin antidiabetic, as follows:
>
> If the patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and insulin, both code Z79.4, Long term (current) use of insulin, and code Z79.84, Long term (current) use of oral hypoglycemic drugs, should be assigned.
>
> If the patient is treated with both insulin and an injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drug, assign codes Z79.4, Long term (current) use of insulin, and Z79.85, Long-term (current) use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
>
> If the patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and an injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drug, assign codes Z79.84, Long term (current) use of oral hypoglycemic drugs, and Z79.85, Long-term (current) use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
>
> Code Z79.4 should not be assigned if insulin is given temporarily to bring a type 2 patient's blood sugar under control during an encounter.
>
> - 4) Diabetes mellitus in pregnancy and gestational diabetes
>
> See Section I.C.15. Diabetes mellitus in pregnancy. See Section I.C.15. Gestational (pregnancy induced) diabetes
>
> Codes under categories E08, Diabetes mellitus due to underlying condition, E09, Drug or chemical induced diabetes mellitus, and E13, Other specified diabetes mellitus, identify complications/manifestations associated with secondary diabetes mellitus. Secondary diabetes is always caused by another condition or event (e.g., cystic fibrosis, malignant neoplasm of pancreas, pancreatectomy, adverse effect of drug, or poisoning).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Diabetes Mellitus Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Diabetes mellitus codes are combination codes including type, body system affected, and complications.
- Codes should be sequenced based on the reason for the particular encounter.
- As many codes as necessary should be assigned to describe all complications of the disease.
- If the type of diabetes is not documented, the default is E11.-, Type 2 diabetes mellitus.
- Additional codes from category Z79 should be assigned to identify long-term use of insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs, or injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
- Codes from categories E08, E09, and E13 are used to identify complications/manifestations associated with secondary diabetes mellitus.
- Secondary diabetes mellitus is defined as diabetes always caused by another condition or event (e.g., cystic fibrosis, malignant neoplasm of pancreas, pancreatectomy, adverse effect of drug, or poisoning).

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must indicate the type of diabetes mellitus; otherwise, default to E11.-.
- Documentation must specify the use of insulin, oral hypoglycemics, or injectable non-insulin drugs to assign Z79 codes.
- If both oral hypoglycemic drugs and insulin are used, assign both Z79.4 and Z79.84.
- If insulin and injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs are used, assign Z79.4 and Z79.85.
- Code Z79.4 should not be assigned if insulin is given temporarily to bring a type 2 patient's blood sugar under control during an encounter.

**Extracted Profile:**

- Documentation must indicate the type of diabetes; if absent, default to E11.-.

- Z79.4 should not be assigned if insulin is given temporarily to control blood sugar during an encounter.

- Code Z79.4 should not be assigned if insulin is given temporarily to bring a type 2 patient's blood sugar under control during an encounter.

### 48. Insulin Pump Failure Coding Protocol
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Chronic Disease Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Insulin Pump Failure Coding Protocol with no substantive changes to the coding logic or sequence.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `133_134`

> **Actual Text**
>
> An underdose of insulin due to an insulin pump failure should be assigned to a code from subcategory T85.6, Mechanical complication of other specified internal and external prosthetic devices, implants and grafts, that specifies the type of pump malfunction, as the principal or first-listed code, followed by code T38.3X6-, Underdosing of insulin and oral hypoglycemic [antidiabetic] drugs. Additional codes for the type of diabetes mellitus and any associated complications due to the underdosing should also be assigned.
>
> The principal or first-listed code for an encounter due to an insulin pump malfunction resulting in an overdose of insulin, should also be T85.6-, Mechanical complication of other specified internal and external prosthetic devices, implants and grafts, followed by code T38.3X1-, Poisoning by insulin and oral hypoglycemic [antidiabetic] drugs, accidental (unintentional).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Insulin Pump Failure Coding Protocol

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign T85.6 code specifying pump malfunction as principal/first-listed for underdose due to pump failure
- Follow T85.6 with T38.3X6- for underdosing of insulin and oral hypoglycemic drugs
- Assign T85.6 code as principal/first-listed for overdose due to pump malfunction
- Follow T85.6 with T38.3X1- for poisoning by insulin and oral hypoglycemic drugs, accidental (unintentional)
- Assign additional codes for type of diabetes mellitus and associated complications due to underdosing

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must specify the type of pump malfunction
- Documentation must indicate underdose or overdose resulting from the pump failure
- Documentation must specify the type of diabetes mellitus
- Documentation must identify associated complications due to underdosing
- Documentation must confirm accidental (unintentional) nature for overdose poisoning codes

---

Additional codes for the type of diabetes mellitus and any associated complications due to the underdosing should also be assigned.

### 49. Resistant Hypertension Coding Guidelines
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Chronic Disease Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for resistant hypertension, including the specific definition, required documentation terms, and sequencing rules.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `170_176`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Secondary hypertension is due to an underlying condition. Two codes are required: one to identify the underlying etiology and one from category I15 to identify the hypertension. Sequencing of codes is determined by the reason for admission/encounter.
>
> Resistant hypertension refers to blood pressure of a patient with hypertension that remains above goal in spite of the use of antihypertensive medications. Assign code I1A.0, Resistant hypertension, as an additional code when apparent treatment resistant hypertension, treatment resistant hypertension, or true resistant hypertension is documented by the provider. A code for the specific type of existing hypertension is sequenced first, if known.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Resistant Hypertension Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code I10.0 as an additional code when apparent treatment resistant hypertension, treatment resistant hypertension, or true resistant hypertension is documented by the provider.
- Sequence a code for the specific type of existing hypertension first if known, followed by the resistant hypertension code.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider must explicitly document apparent treatment resistant hypertension, treatment resistant hypertension, or true resistant hypertension.
- A code for the specific type of existing hypertension must be sequenced first if known.

### 50. I13 Hypertensive Heart and Kidney Disease Coding
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Chronic Disease Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same coding logic for I13 and I12 hypertensive kidney disease with identical behavioral instructions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `166_167`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assign codes from category I12, Hypertensive chronic kidney disease, when both hypertension and a condition classifiable to category N18, Chronic kidney disease (CKD), are present. CKD should not be coded as hypertensive if the provider indicates the CKD is not related to the hypertension.
>
> The appropriate code from category N18 should be used as a secondary code with a code from category I12 to identify the stage of chronic kidney disease.
>
> See Section I.C.14. Chronic kidney disease.
>
> If a patient has hypertensive chronic kidney disease and acute renal failure, the acute renal failure should also be coded. Sequence according to the circumstances of the admission/encounter.
>
> Assign codes from combination category I13, Hypertensive heart and chronic kidney disease, when there is hypertension with both heart and kidney involvement. If heart failure is present, assign an additional code from category I50 to identify the type of heart failure.
>
> The appropriate code from category N18, Chronic kidney disease, should be used as a secondary code with a code from category I13 to identify the stage of chronic kidney disease.
>
> See Section I.C.14. Chronic kidney disease.
>
> The codes in category I13, Hypertensive heart and chronic kidney disease, are combination codes that include hypertension, heart disease and chronic kidney disease. The Includes note at I13 specifies that the conditions included at I11 and I12 are included together in I13. If a patient has hypertension, heart disease and chronic kidney disease, then a code from I13 should be used, not individual codes for hypertension, heart disease and chronic kidney disease, or codes from I11 or I12.
>
> For patients with both acute renal failure and chronic kidney disease, the acute renal failure should also be coded. Sequence according to the circumstances of the admission/encounter.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** I13 Hypertensive Heart and Kidney Disease Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes from category I12 when both hypertension and chronic kidney disease (N18) are present.
- Assign codes from combination category I13 when there is hypertension with both heart and kidney involvement.
- Use the appropriate code from category N18 as a secondary code with I12 or I13 to identify the stage of chronic kidney disease.
- If heart failure is present with I13, assign an additional code from category I50 to identify the type of heart failure.
- Code acute renal failure in addition to hypertensive chronic kidney disease if present, sequencing according to admission circumstances.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Both hypertension and a condition classifiable to category N18 must be present to assign I12.
- The provider must indicate that CKD is related to hypertension; if not related, do not code as hypertensive.
- For I13, there must be hypertension with both heart and kidney involvement.
- The Includes note at I13 specifies that conditions included at I11 and I12 are included together in I13.
- If a patient has hypertension, heart disease, and chronic kidney disease, use a code from I13 instead of individual codes for each condition.

---

### 55. Hypertension Comorbidity Coding Rules
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Chronic Disease Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for hypertension comorbidities regarding Alphabetic Index presumptions and documentation requirements.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `164`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The classification presumes a causal relationship between hypertension and heart involvement and between hypertension and kidney involvement, as the two conditions are linked by the term 'with' in the Alphabetic Index. These conditions should be coded as related even in the absence of provider documentation explicitly linking them, unless the documentation clearly states the conditions are unrelated.
>
> For hypertension and conditions not specifically linked by relational terms such as 'with,' 'associated with' or 'due to' in the classification, provider documentation must link the conditions in order to code them as related.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Hypertension Comorbidity Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Presume a causal relationship between hypertension and heart/kidney involvement if linked by the term 'with' in the Alphabetic Index, even without explicit provider documentation.
- Code hypertension and related conditions as related unless documentation explicitly states they are unrelated.
- Require explicit provider documentation linking conditions to code them as related when no relational terms (e.g., 'with', 'associated with') are present in the classification.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must explicitly link conditions if the classification does not use relational terms like 'with' or 'due to'.
- Absence of explicit provider documentation does not preclude coding related conditions if the Alphabetic Index links them via 'with'.
- Explicit documentation stating conditions are unrelated overrides the presumption of a causal relationship.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `592`

- Sequencing must prioritize the reason for the encounter unless the condition is an adverse effect of drugs.

### 58. Cerebrovascular Sequelae Coding (I69)
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Chronic Disease Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding Cerebrovascular Sequelae Coding (I69) and its specific rules for side assignment and code combination.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `179`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Category I69 is used to indicate conditions classifiable to categories I60I67 as the causes of sequela (neurologic deficits), themselves classified elsewhere. These 'late effects' include neurologic deficits that persist after initial onset of conditions classifiable to categories I60-I67. The neurologic deficits caused by cerebrovascular disease may be present from the onset or may arise at any time after the onset of the condition classifiable to categories I60-I67.
>
> Codes from category I69, Sequelae of cerebrovascular disease, that specify hemiplegia, hemiparesis and monoplegia identify whether the dominant or nondominant side is affected. Should the affected side be documented, but not specified as dominant or nondominant, and the classification system does not indicate a default, code selection is as follows:
>
> - For ambidextrous patients, the default should be dominant.
> - If the left side is affected, the default is non-dominant.
> - If the right side is affected, the default is dominant.
> - 2) Codes from category I69 with codes from I60-I67
>
> Codes from category I69 may be assigned on a health care record with codes from I60-I67, if the patient has a current cerebrovascular disease and deficits from an old cerebrovascular disease.
>
> - 3) Codes from category I69 and Personal history of transient ischemic attack (TIA) and cerebral infarction (Z86.73)
>
> Codes from category I69 should not be assigned if the patient does not have neurologic deficits.
>
> See Section I.C.21.4. History (of) for use of personal history codes

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Cerebrovascular Sequelae Coding (I69)

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes I69 indicate conditions classifiable to I60-I67 as causes of sequela (neurologic deficits) classified elsewhere.
- Codes specify whether the dominant or nondominant side is affected for hemiplegia, hemiparesis, and monoplegia.
- Default side assignment rules apply if affected side is documented but not specified as dominant or nondominant.
- Codes I69 may be assigned with codes I60-I67 if the patient has current cerebrovascular disease and deficits from an old cerebrovascular disease.
- Codes I69 should not be assigned if the patient does not have neurologic deficits.
- For ambidextrous patients where the affected side is unspecified, the default assignment is the dominant side.
- If the left side is affected but not specified as dominant or nondominant, the default assignment is non-dominant.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Neurologic deficits must be present from the onset or arise at any time after the onset of the condition classifiable to categories I60-I67.
- Affected side must be documented as dominant or nondominant; if not specified, defaults apply based on patient ambidexterity or side affected.
- Patient must have a current cerebrovascular disease and deficits from an old cerebrovascular disease to assign I69 with I60-I67.
- Patient must have neurologic deficits to assign codes from category I69.
- Personal history codes (Z86.73) may be used in conjunction with I69 codes per Section I.C.21.4.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `607`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Category I69 is used to indicate conditions classifiable to categories I60I67 as the causes of sequela (neurologic deficits), themselves classified elsewhere. These 'late effects' include neurologic deficits that persist after initial onset of conditions classifiable to categories I60-I67. The neurologic deficits caused by cerebrovascular disease may be present from the onset or may arise at any time after the onset of the condition classifiable to categories I60-I67.
>
> Codes from category I69, Sequelae of cerebrovascular disease, that specify hemiplegia, hemiparesis and monoplegia identify whether the dominant or nondominant side is affected. Should the affected side be documented, but not specified as dominant or nondominant, and the classification system does not indicate a default, code selection is as follows:
>
> - For ambidextrous patients, the default should be dominant.
> - If the left side is affected, the default is non-dominant.
> - If the right side is affected, the default is dominant.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Cerebrovascular Sequelae Coding (I69)

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes from category I69 are used to indicate conditions classifiable to categories I60-I67 as the causes of sequela (neurologic deficits).
- These 'late effects' include neurologic deficits that persist after initial onset of conditions classifiable to categories I60-I67.
- The neurologic deficits caused by cerebrovascular disease may be present from the onset or may arise at any time after the onset of the condition classifiable to categories I60-I67.
- Codes specifying hemiplegia, hemiparesis, and monoplegia identify whether the dominant or nondominant side is affected.
- If the affected side is documented but not specified as dominant or nondominant, default selection rules apply based on patient handedness and side affected.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The condition must be classifiable to categories I60-I67 as the cause of the sequela.
- The neurologic deficits must persist after the initial onset of the cerebrovascular condition.
- For ambidextrous patients with unspecified dominant/nondominant side, the default should be dominant.
- If the left side is affected and the dominant/nondominant status is unspecified, the default is non-dominant.
- If the right side is affected and the dominant/nondominant status is unspecified, the default is dominant.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 2025 version is a direct restatement of the October 2024 guidelines with identical logic for assigning I69 codes based on dominant/nondominant sides and the requirement for current cerebrovascular disease. The specific default rules for ambidextrous patients and left/right side assignments remain exactly the same in both versions. There are no substantive changes to the coding criteria, only minor phrasing adjustments in the extracted profile summary.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes I69 may be assigned with codes I60-I67 if the patient has current cerebrovascular disease and deficits from an old cerebrovascular disease. -> October 2025 (New Version): The condition must be classifiable to categories I60-I67 as the cause of the sequela, with no change to the requirement for current disease.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Patient must have neurologic deficits to assign codes from category I69. -> October 2025 (New Version): The neurologic deficits must persist after the initial onset of the cerebrovascular condition.

*Traceability: Chunk `179` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `607` (October 2025 (New Version))*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `83_85`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For a patient with pneumonia confirmed as due to COVID-19, assign codes U07.1, COVID-19, and J12.82, Pneumonia due to coronavirus disease 2019.
>
> If the COVID-19 is documented as being associated with a lower respiratory infection, not otherwise specified (NOS), or an acute respiratory infection, NOS, codes U07.1 and J22, Unspecified acute lower respiratory infection, should be assigned.
>
> If the COVID-19 is documented as being associated with a respiratory infection, NOS, codes U07.1 and J98.8, Other specified respiratory disorders, should be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Respiratory Infection Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes U07.1, COVID-19, and J12.82 for pneumonia confirmed as due to COVID-19.
- Assign codes U07.1 and J22 for COVID-19 associated with lower respiratory infection NOS or acute respiratory infection NOS.
- Assign codes U07.1 and J98.8 for COVID-19 associated with respiratory infection NOS.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Pneumonia must be confirmed as due to COVID-19 to assign J12.82.
- COVID-19 must be documented as associated with a specific respiratory infection type to determine the secondary code (J22 or J98.8).
- Code U07.1 must be assigned in all scenarios where COVID-19 is the underlying cause of the respiratory infection.

> For a patient with pneumonia confirmed as due to COVID-19, assign codes U07.1, COVID-19, and J12.82, Pneumonia due to coronavirus disease 2019.
>
> If the COVID-19 is documented as being associated with a lower respiratory infection, not otherwise specified (NOS), or an acute respiratory infection, NOS, codes U07.1 and J22, Unspecified acute lower respiratory infection, should be assigned.
>
> If the COVID-19 is documented as being associated with a respiratory infection, NOS, codes U07.1 and J98.8, Other specified respiratory disorders, should be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Respiratory Infection Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes U07.1, COVID-19, and J12.82 for pneumonia confirmed as due to COVID-19.
- Assign codes U07.1 and J22 for COVID-19 associated with lower respiratory infection NOS or acute respiratory infection NOS.
- Assign codes U07.1 and J98.8 for COVID-19 associated with respiratory infection NOS.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Pneumonia must be confirmed as due to COVID-19 to assign J12.82.
- COVID-19 must be documented as associated with a lower respiratory infection NOS or acute respiratory infection NOS to assign J22.
- COVID-19 must be documented as associated with a respiratory infection NOS to assign J98.8.

---

There are no new conditions, reversed guidance, or clarified ambiguities introduced in the newer version.

>
> A code from subcategory J96.0, Acute respiratory failure, or subcategory J96.2, Acute and chronic respiratory failure, may be assigned as a principal diagnosis when it is the condition established after study to be chiefly responsible for occasioning the admission to the hospital, and the selection is supported by the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List. However, chapter- specific coding guidelines (such as obstetrics, poisoning, HIV, newborn) that provide sequencing direction take precedence.

- An acute exacerbation is not equivalent to an infection superimposed on a chronic condition, though an exacerbation may be triggered by an infection.
- Codes from subcategory J96.0 or J96.2 may be assigned as a principal diagnosis when the condition is chiefly responsible for the admission.
- Selection of principal diagnosis is supported by the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List, subject to chapter-specific sequencing precedence.

- Chapter-specific coding guidelines (e.g., obstetrics, poisoning, HIV, newborn) that provide sequencing direction take precedence over general guidelines.
