### 115. Glaucoma Coding Assignment
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Edge Cases & Standalone Topics  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding Glaucoma Coding Assignment rules for H40 codes across versions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `158_159_160`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assign as many codes from category H40, Glaucoma, as needed to identify the type of glaucoma, the affected eye, and the glaucoma stage.
>
> When a patient has bilateral glaucoma and both eyes are documented as being the same type and stage, and there is a code for bilateral glaucoma, report only the code for the type of glaucoma, bilateral, with the seventh character for the stage.
>
> When a patient has bilateral glaucoma and both eyes are documented as being the same type and stage, and the classification does not provide a code for bilateral glaucoma (i.e. subcategories H40.10, and H40.20) report only one code for the type of glaucoma with the appropriate seventh character for the stage.
>
> When a patient has bilateral glaucoma and each eye is documented as having a different type or stage, and the classification distinguishes laterality, assign the appropriate code for each eye rather than the code for bilateral glaucoma.
>
> When a patient has bilateral glaucoma and each eye is documented as having a different type, and the classification does not distinguish laterality (i.e., subcategories H40.10, and H40.20), assign one code for each type of glaucoma with the appropriate seventh character for the stage.
>
> When a patient has bilateral glaucoma and each eye is documented as having the same type, but different stage, and the classification does not distinguish laterality (i.e., subcategories H40.10 and H40.20), assign a code for the type of glaucoma for each eye with the seventh character for the specific glaucoma stage documented for each eye.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Glaucoma Coding Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign as many codes from category H40 as needed to identify the type of glaucoma, the affected eye, and the glaucoma stage.
- Report only the code for bilateral glaucoma with the seventh character for the stage when both eyes are the same type and stage and a bilateral code exists.
- Report only one code for the type of glaucoma with the appropriate seventh character when both eyes are the same type and stage but no bilateral code exists.
- Assign the appropriate code for each eye rather than the code for bilateral glaucoma when each eye has a different type or stage and laterality is distinguished.
- Assign one code for each type of glaucoma with the appropriate seventh character when each eye has a different type and laterality is not distinguished.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must identify the type of glaucoma, the affected eye, and the glaucoma stage.
- If bilateral glaucoma is present, the specific relationship between eye types and stages determines whether a single bilateral code or multiple eye-specific codes are assigned.
- When laterality is not distinguished in the classification (e.g., H40.10, H40.20), codes must be assigned for each eye individually if types or stages differ.
- The seventh character must be used to indicate the glaucoma stage for each assigned code.
- A code for bilateral glaucoma is only reported if the classification explicitly provides one for the documented type and stage.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `586_587_588`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assign as many codes from category H40, Glaucoma, as needed to identify the type of glaucoma, the affected eye, and the glaucoma stage.
>
> When a patient has bilateral glaucoma and both eyes are documented as being the same type and stage, and there is a code for bilateral glaucoma, report only the code for the type of glaucoma, bilateral, with the seventh character for the stage.
>
> When a patient has bilateral glaucoma and both eyes are documented as being the same type and stage, and the classification does not provide a code for bilateral glaucoma (i.e. subcategories H40.10, and H40.20)
>
> report only one code for the type of glaucoma with the appropriate seventh character for the stage.
>
> When a patient has bilateral glaucoma and each eye is documented as having a different type or stage, and the classification distinguishes laterality, assign the appropriate code for each eye rather than the code for bilateral glaucoma.
>
> When a patient has bilateral glaucoma and each eye is documented as having a different type, and the classification does not distinguish laterality (i.e., subcategories H40.10, and H40.20), assign one code for each type of glaucoma with the appropriate seventh character for the stage.
>
> When a patient has bilateral glaucoma and each eye is documented as having the same type, but different stage, and the classification does not distinguish laterality (i.e., subcategories H40.10 and H40.20), assign a code for the type of glaucoma for each eye with the seventh character for the specific glaucoma stage documented for each eye.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Glaucoma Coding Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign as many codes from category H40 as needed to identify the type of glaucoma, the affected eye, and the glaucoma stage.
- Report only the code for bilateral glaucoma with the seventh character for the stage when both eyes are the same type and stage and a bilateral code exists.
- Report only one code for the type of glaucoma with the appropriate seventh character for the stage when both eyes are the same type and stage but no bilateral code exists.
- Assign the appropriate code for each eye rather than the code for bilateral glaucoma when each eye has a different type or stage and laterality is distinguished.
- Assign one code for each type of glaucoma with the appropriate seventh character for the stage when each eye has a different type and laterality is not distinguished.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must identify the type of glaucoma, the affected eye, and the glaucoma stage.
- If bilateral glaucoma is present, determine if both eyes share the same type and stage to decide between a single bilateral code or separate eye codes.
- If laterality is not distinguished in the classification (e.g., subcategories H40.10, H40.20), assign separate codes for each eye if types or stages differ.
- Use the seventh character to indicate the glaucoma stage when applicable.
- Do not report a code for bilateral glaucoma if the eyes have different types or stages.
- When laterality is not distinguished in the classification (e.g., subcategories H40.10, H40.20), assign separate codes for each eye if the types or stages differ.

---

The specific rules for bilateral cases—whether to use a single code or separate codes based on laterality and classification—are identical in both texts. While the 2025 version includes minor phrasing adjustments in the requirements section, the core coding behavior and clinical criteria remain unchanged.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign one code for each type of glaucoma with the appropriate seventh character when each eye has a different type and laterality is not distinguished. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign one code for each type of glaucoma with the appropriate seventh character for the stage when each eye has a different type and laterality is not distinguished.

- Assign only a single code representing the highest degree when multiple degrees are present at the same site

### 118. Multiple Code Sequencing Rules
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Edge Cases & Standalone Topics  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature regarding Multiple Code Sequencing Rules with identical behavioral definitions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `34`

> **Actual Text**
>
> In addition to the etiology/manifestation convention that requires two codes to fully describe a single condition that affects multiple body systems, there are other single conditions that also require more than one code. 'Use additional code' notes are found in the Tabular List at codes that are not part of an etiology/manifestation pair where a secondary code is useful to fully describe a condition. The sequencing rule is the same as the etiology/manifestation pair, 'use additional code' indicates that a secondary code should be added , if known.
>
> For example, for bacterial infections that are not included in chapter 1, a secondary code from category B95, Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and Enterococcus, as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, or B96, Other bacterial agents as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, may be required to identify the bacterial organism causing the infection. A 'use additional code' note will normally be found at the infectious disease code, indicating a need for the organism code to be added as a secondary code.
>
> 'Code first' notes are also under certain codes that are not specifically manifestation codes but may be due to an underlying cause. When there is a 'code first' note and an underlying condition is present, the underlying condition should be sequenced first, if known.
>
> 'Code, if applicable, any causal condition first' notes indicate that this code may be assigned as a principal diagnosis when the causal condition is unknown or not applicable. If a causal condition is known, then the code for that condition should be sequenced as the principal or first-listed diagnosis.
>
> Multiple codes may be needed for sequela, complication codes and obstetric codes to more fully describe a condition. See the specific guidelines for these conditions for further instruction.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Multiple Code Sequencing Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- 'Use additional code' notes indicate a secondary code should be added if known to fully describe a condition.
- 'Code first' notes require the underlying condition to be sequenced first if present.
- 'Code, if applicable, any causal condition first' notes allow the current code as principal diagnosis only if the causal condition is unknown or not applicable.
- Secondary codes from categories B95 or B96 may be required to identify bacterial organisms causing infections classified elsewhere.
- Multiple codes are required for sequela, complication codes, and obstetric codes to fully describe a condition.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The underlying condition must be known and present for 'code first' notes to apply.
- The causal condition must be unknown or not applicable for 'code, if applicable, any causal condition first' notes to allow the current code as principal.
- The secondary code must be known to be added when 'use additional code' notes are present.
- The condition must affect multiple body systems or require more than one code to be fully described.
- Specific guidelines must be consulted for sequela, complication, and obstetric codes.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `466`

> **Actual Text**
>
> In addition to the etiology/manifestation convention that requires two codes to fully describe a single condition that affects multiple body systems, there are other single conditions that also require more than one code. 'Use additional code' notes are found in the Tabular List at codes that are not part of an etiology/manifestation pair where a secondary code is useful to fully describe a condition. The sequencing rule is the same as the etiology/manifestation pair, 'use additional code' indicates that a secondary code should be added , if known.
>
> For example, for bacterial infections that are not included in chapter 1, a secondary code from category B95, Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and Enterococcus, as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, or B96, Other bacterial agents as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, may be required to identify the bacterial organism causing the infection. A 'use additional code' note will normally be found at the infectious disease code, indicating a need for the organism code to be added as a secondary code.
>
> 'Code first' notes are also under certain codes that are not specifically manifestation codes but may be due to an underlying cause. When there is a 'code first' note and an underlying condition is present, the underlying condition should be sequenced first, if known.
>
> 'Code, if applicable, any causal condition first' notes indicate that this code may be assigned as a principal diagnosis when the causal condition is unknown or not applicable. If a causal condition is known, then the code for that condition should be sequenced as the principal or first-listed diagnosis.
>
> Multiple codes may be needed for sequela, complication codes and obstetric codes to more fully describe a condition. See the specific guidelines for these conditions for further instruction.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Multiple Code Sequencing Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- 'Use additional code' notes indicate a secondary code should be added if known to fully describe a condition.
- 'Code first' notes require the underlying condition to be sequenced first if present.
- 'Code, if applicable, any causal condition first' notes allow the current code as principal diagnosis only if the causal condition is unknown or not applicable.
- Secondary codes from categories B95 or B96 may be required to identify bacterial organisms causing infections classified elsewhere.
- Multiple codes are required for sequela, complication codes, and obstetric codes to fully describe a condition.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The underlying condition must be known and present for 'code first' notes to apply.
- The causal condition must be known to sequence the specific condition code as principal; otherwise, the causal condition code is sequenced first.
- The secondary code must be known to be added when a 'use additional code' note is present.
- The infectious disease code must be present to identify the need for an organism code from categories B95 or B96.
- The condition must affect multiple body systems or require more than one code to be fully described.

---

Consequently, the operational guidance for coders remains functionally identical between the two versions.

> **Actual Text**

> When the admission/encounter is for management of dehydration due to the malignancy and only the dehydration is being treated (intravenous rehydration), the dehydration is sequenced first, followed by the code(s) for the malignancy.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Dehydration and Malignancy Coding Sequence

**Key Behaviors:**
- When admission is for management of dehydration due to malignancy and only dehydration is treated, sequence dehydration first
- Follow the dehydration code with code(s) for the malignancy

- Admission/encounter must be for management of dehydration due to malignancy
- Only dehydration must be treated (e.g., intravenous rehydration)

> **Actual Text**

> A code from category Y90, Evidence of alcohol involvement determined by blood alcohol level, may be assigned when this information is documented and the patient's provider has documented a condition classifiable to category F10, Alcohol related disorders. The blood alcohol level does not need to be documented by the patient's provider in order for it to be coded.
>
> See Section I.B.14. for blood alcohol level documentation by clinicians other than patient's provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Y90 Blood Alcohol Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign Y90 code when blood alcohol level is documented and provider documents a condition classifiable to category F10
- Y90 code may be assigned even if the blood alcohol level is not documented by the patient's provider
- Refer to Section I.B.14 for documentation by clinicians other than the patient's provider

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Information must be documented regarding blood alcohol level
- Patient's provider must document a condition classifiable to category F10 (Alcohol related disorders)

### 121. UHDDS Other Diagnoses Reporting Definition
**Location:** ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines → Edge Cases & Standalone Topics  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical UHDDS Other Diagnoses Reporting Definition with no substantive changes in criteria or scope.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `394`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For reporting purposes, the definition for 'other diagnoses' is interpreted as additional clinically significant conditions that affect patient care in terms of requiring:
>
> clinical evaluation; or therapeutic treatment; or diagnostic procedures; or extended length of hospital stay; or increased nursing care and/or monitoring.
>
> The UHDDS item #11-b defines Other Diagnoses as 'all conditions that coexist at the time of admission, that develop subsequently, or that affect the treatment received and/or the length of stay. Diagnoses that relate to an earlier episode which have no bearing on the current hospital stay are to be excluded.' UHDDS definitions apply to inpatients in acute care, short-term, long term care and psychiatric hospital setting. The UHDDS definitions are used by acute care short-term hospitals to report inpatient data elements in a standardized manner. These data elements and their definitions can be found in the July 31, 1985, Federal Register (Vol. 50, No, 147), pp. 31038-40.
>
> Since that time, the application of the UHDDS definitions has been expanded to include all non-outpatient settings (acute care, short term, long term care and psychiatric hospitals; home health agencies; rehab facilities; nursing homes, etc.). The UHDDS definitions also apply to hospice services (all levels of care).
>
> The following guidelines are to be applied in designating 'other diagnoses' when neither the Alphabetic Index nor the Tabular List in ICD-10-CM provide direction. The listing of the diagnoses in the patient record is the responsibility of the provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** UHDDS Other Diagnoses Reporting Definition

**Key Behaviors:**
- Report additional clinically significant conditions affecting patient care requiring evaluation, treatment, procedures, extended stay, or increased nursing care.
- Include all conditions coexisting at admission, developing subsequently, or affecting treatment/length of stay.
- Exclude diagnoses relating to earlier episodes with no bearing on the current hospital stay.
- Apply definitions to inpatients in acute care, short-term, long-term care, psychiatric, home health, rehab, nursing homes, and hospice settings.
- Provider is responsible for listing diagnoses in the patient record when ICD-10-CM Index/Tabular List provide no direction.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must require clinical evaluation, therapeutic treatment, diagnostic procedures, extended length of stay, or increased nursing care/monitoring.
- Condition must coexist at time of admission, develop subsequently, or affect treatment received and/or length of stay.
- Condition must have a bearing on the current hospital stay (exclude earlier episode diagnoses with no bearing).
- Application applies to all non-outpatient settings including acute care, short-term, long-term care, psychiatric hospitals, home health, rehab, nursing homes, and hospice.
- Use ICD-10-CM Alphabetic Index and Tabular List for direction; if none provided, rely on provider's listing of diagnoses.

**Superseded Guidance:**
- The July 31, 1985 Federal Register (Vol. 50, No. 147), pp. 31038-40 is cited as the original source for UHDDS data elements and definitions.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `827`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For reporting purposes, the definition for 'other diagnoses' is interpreted as additional clinically significant conditions that affect patient care in terms of requiring:
>
> clinical evaluation; or therapeutic treatment; or diagnostic procedures; or extended length of hospital stay; or increased nursing care and/or monitoring.
>
> The UHDDS item #11-b defines Other Diagnoses as 'all conditions that coexist at the time of admission, that develop subsequently, or that affect the treatment received and/or the length of stay. Diagnoses that relate to an earlier episode which have no bearing on the current hospital stay are to be excluded.' UHDDS definitions apply to inpatients in acute care, short-term, long term care and psychiatric hospital setting. The UHDDS definitions are used by acute care short-term hospitals to report inpatient data elements in a standardized manner. These data elements and their definitions can be found in the July 31, 1985, Federal Register (Vol. 50, No, 147), pp. 31038-40.
>
> Since that time, the application of the UHDDS definitions has been expanded to include all non-outpatient settings (acute care, short term, long term care and psychiatric hospitals; home health agencies; rehab facilities; nursing homes, etc.). The UHDDS definitions also apply to hospice services (all levels of care).
>
> The following guidelines are to be applied in designating 'other diagnoses' when neither the Alphabetic Index nor the Tabular List in ICD-10-CM provide direction. The listing of the diagnoses in the patient record is the responsibility of the provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** UHDDS Other Diagnoses Reporting Definition

**Key Behaviors:**
- Report additional clinically significant conditions affecting patient care requiring evaluation, treatment, procedures, extended stay, or increased nursing care.
- Include all conditions coexisting at admission or developing subsequently that affect treatment or length of stay.
- Exclude diagnoses relating to earlier episodes with no bearing on the current hospital stay.
- Apply definitions to inpatients in acute care, short-term, long-term care, psychiatric, home health, rehab, nursing homes, and hospice settings.
- Provider is responsible for listing diagnoses in the patient record when ICD-10-CM Index or Tabular List provide no direction.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must require clinical evaluation, therapeutic treatment, or diagnostic procedures.
- Condition must require extended length of hospital stay.
- Condition must require increased nursing care and/or monitoring.
- Condition must coexist at time of admission or develop subsequently.
- Condition must affect treatment received and/or length of stay.

**Superseded Guidance:**
- UHDDS definitions originally applied only to inpatients in acute care, short-term, long-term care, and psychiatric hospital settings before being expanded to include home health agencies, rehab facilities, nursing homes, and hospice services.

---

Both versions explicitly cite UHDDS item #11-b and include the same list of applicable settings (acute care, short-term, long-term, psychiatric, home health, rehab, nursing homes, and hospice). The text excerpts provided for both versions are identical, indicating that the guideline content has remained static between these two time points.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Definition includes conditions requiring evaluation, treatment, procedures, extended stay, or increased nursing care -> October 2025 (New Version): Definition includes conditions requiring evaluation, treatment, procedures, extended stay, or increased nursing care
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Application applies to inpatients in acute care, short-term, long-term care, psychiatric, home health, rehab, nursing homes, and hospice settings -> October 2025 (New Version): Application applies to inpatients in acute care, short-term, long-term care, psychiatric, home health, rehab, nursing homes, and hospice settings

### 122. ICD-10-CM Burn and Corrosion Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical ICD-10-CM Burn and Corrosion Coding Guidelines feature with matching definitions and behaviors.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `291_296_297_299`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The ICD-10-CM makes a distinction between burns and corrosions. The burn codes are for thermal burns, except sunburns, that come from a heat source, such as a fire or hot appliance. The burn codes are also for burns resulting from electricity and radiation. Corrosions are burns due to chemicals. The guidelines are the same for burns and corrosions.
>
> Current burns (T20-T25) are classified by depth, extent and by agent (X code). Burns are classified by depth as first degree (erythema), second degree
>
> (blistering), and third degree (full-thickness involvement). Burns of the eye and internal organs (T26-T28) are classified by site, but not by degree.
>
> When coding burns, assign separate codes for each burn site. Category T30, Burn and corrosion, body region unspecified is extremely vague and should rarely be used.
>
> Codes for burns of "multiple sites" should only be assigned when the medical record documentation does not specify the individual sites.
>
> Assign codes from category T31, Burns classified according to extent of body surface involved, or T32, Corrosions classified according to extent of body surface involved, for acute burns or corrosions when the site of the burn or corrosion is not specified or when there is a need for additional data. It is advisable to use category T31 as additional coding when needed to provide data for evaluating burn mortality, such as that needed by burn units. It is also advisable to use category T31 as an additional code for reporting purposes when there is mention of a thirddegree burn involving 20 percent or more of the body surface. Codes from categories T31 and T32 should not be used for sequelae of burns or corrosions.
>
> Categories T31 and T32 are based on the classic 'rule of nines' in estimating body surface involved: head and neck are assigned nine percent, each arm nine percent, each leg 18 percent, the anterior trunk 18 percent, posterior trunk 18 percent, and genitalia one percent. Providers may change these percentage assignments where necessary to accommodate infants and children who have proportionately larger heads than adults, and patients who have large buttocks, thighs, or abdomen that involve burns.
>
> When appropriate, both a code for a current burn or corrosion with 7 th character 'A' or 'D' and a burn or corrosion code with 7 th character 'S' may be assigned on the same record (when both a current burn and sequelae of an old burn exist). Burns and corrosions do not heal at the same rate and a current healing wound may still exist with sequela of a healed burn or corrosion.
>
> See Section I.B.10. Sequela (Late Effects)

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Burn and Corrosion Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Distinguish between thermal/electric/radiation burns and chemical corrosions, applying the same coding guidelines to both.
- Classify burns by depth (first, second, third degree) and site, while classifying eye and internal organ burns by site only.
- Assign separate codes for each burn site unless the medical record documentation does not specify individual sites.
- Use categories T31 or T32 for acute burns/corrosions when the specific site is not specified or additional data is needed for mortality evaluation.
- Apply the 'rule of nines' for estimating body surface area, adjusting percentages for infants, children, or patients with atypical body proportions.
- Assign codes from categories T31 or T32 specifically when the site of the burn/corrosion is not specified OR when there is a need for additional data.
- It is advisable to use category T31 as an additional code when there is mention of a third-degree burn involving 20 percent or more of the body surface.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Codes for 'multiple sites' require that the medical record documentation does not specify the individual sites.
- Categories T31 and T32 must not be used for sequelae of burns or corrosions.
- Categories T31 and T32 are based on the classic 'rule of nines' for estimating body surface involved.
- A code for a current burn/corrosion (7th character 'A' or 'D') and a sequelae code (7th character 'S') may be assigned on the same record if both conditions exist.
- Category T30 (Burn and corrosion, body region unspecified) is extremely vague and should rarely be used.
- Codes for burns of 'multiple sites' should only be assigned when the medical record documentation does not specify the individual sites.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `718_723_724_726`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The ICD-10-CM makes a distinction between burns and corrosions. The burn codes are for thermal burns, except sunburns, that come from a heat source, such as a fire or hot appliance. The burn codes are also for burns resulting from electricity and radiation. Corrosions are burns due to chemicals. The guidelines are the same for burns and corrosions.
>
> Current burns (T20-T25) are classified by depth, extent and by agent (X code). Burns are classified by depth as first degree (erythema), second degree (blistering), and third degree (full-thickness involvement). Burns of the eye and internal organs (T26-T28) are classified by site, but not by degree.
>
> When coding burns, assign separate codes for each burn site. Category T30, Burn and corrosion, body region unspecified is extremely vague and should rarely be used.
>
> Codes for burns of "multiple sites" should only be assigned when the medical record documentation does not specify the individual sites.
>
> Assign codes from category T31, Burns classified according to extent of body surface involved, or T32, Corrosions classified according to extent of body surface involved, for acute burns or corrosions when the site of the burn or corrosion is not specified or when there is a need for additional data. It is advisable to use category T31 as additional coding when needed to provide data for evaluating burn mortality, such as that needed by burn units. It is also advisable to use category T31 as an additional code for reporting purposes when there is mention of a thirddegree burn involving 20 percent or more of the body surface. Codes from categories T31 and T32 should not be used for sequelae of burns or corrosions.
>
> Categories T31 and T32 are based on the classic 'rule of nines' in estimating body surface involved: head and neck are assigned nine percent, each arm nine percent, each leg 18 percent, the anterior trunk 18 percent, posterior trunk 18 percent, and genitalia one percent. Providers may change these percentage assignments where necessary to accommodate infants and children who have proportionately larger heads than adults, and patients who have large buttocks, thighs, or abdomen that involve burns.
>
> When appropriate, both a code for a current burn or corrosion with 7 th character 'A' or 'D' and a burn or corrosion code with 7 th character 'S' may be assigned on the same record (when both a current burn and sequelae of an old burn exist). Burns and corrosions do not heal at the same rate and a current healing wound may still exist with sequela of a healed burn or corrosion.
>
> See Section I.B.10. Sequela (Late Effects)

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Burn and Corrosion Coding Distinctions and Sequela Reporting

**Key Behaviors:**
- Distinguish between thermal/electric/radiation burns and chemical corrosions, applying the same guidelines to both.
- Classify burns by depth (first, second, third degree) and site, while classifying eye and internal organ burns by site only.
- Assign separate codes for each burn site unless documentation is unspecified, in which case use 'multiple sites' codes.
- Use extent-based categories (T31/T32) when site is unspecified or for additional data on mortality reporting (e.g., third-degree burns involving 20%+ BSA).
- Allow simultaneous assignment of current burn codes (7th char 'A' or 'D') and sequelae codes (7th char 'S') when both conditions exist.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Burns must be classified by depth (erythema, blistering, full-thickness) and extent using the 'rule of nines' adjusted for age/anatomy.
- Codes from categories T31 and T32 must not be used for sequelae of burns or corrosions.
- Category T30 (unspecified body region) should rarely be used due to vagueness.
- Percentage assignments for body surface area may be modified for infants and children to accommodate anatomical differences.
- Documentation must specify individual sites to avoid defaulting to 'multiple sites' codes.
- Codes from categories T31 and T32 should not be used for sequelae of burns or corrosions.
- Category T30 (Burn and corrosion, body region unspecified) should rarely be used due to vagueness.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older and newer versions present the exact same coding logic regarding the distinction between thermal/electrical burns and chemical corrosions, the classification of current burns by depth and site, and the specific usage rules for categories T30, T31, and T32. The text excerpts provided for both versions are virtually identical, indicating that the guidelines have not been updated or revised between these periods. There are no new clinical criteria, reversed guidance, or expanded scopes introduced in the 2025 version compared to the 2024 version.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Guidelines define burn classification by depth and site with T30 restricted for unspecified regions -> October 2025 (New Version): Guidelines define burn classification by depth and site with T30 restricted for unspecified regions.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): T31/T32 used for unspecified sites or mortality data -> October 2025 (New Version): T31/T32 used for unspecified sites or mortality data.

*Traceability: Chunk `291_296_297_299` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `718_723_724_726` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 123. ICD-10 Pain Code Differentiation
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the differentiation of specific pain syndromes and device-related pain coding rules.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `154_157_312`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Postoperative pain associated with a specific postoperative complication (such as painful wire sutures) is assigned to the appropriate code(s) found in Chapter 19, Injury, poisoning, and certain other consequences of external causes. If appropriate, use additional code(s) from category G89 to identify acute or chronic pain (G89.18 or G89.28).
>
> Central pain syndrome (G89.0) and chronic pain syndrome (G89.4) are different than the term 'chronic pain,' and therefore codes should only be used when the provider has specifically documented this condition.
>
> See Section I.C.5. Pain disorders related to psychological factors
>
> Pain associated with devices, implants or grafts left in a surgical site (for example painful hip prosthesis) is assigned to the appropriate code(s) found in Chapter 19, Injury, poisoning, and certain other consequences of external causes. Specific codes for pain due to medical devices are found in the T code section of the ICD-10-CM. Use additional code(s) from category G89 to identify acute or chronic pain due to presence of the device, implant or graft (G89.18 or G89.28).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Pain Code Differentiation

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign postoperative pain associated with specific complications to Chapter 19 codes, using G89.18 or G89.28 only if appropriate.
- Reserve Central pain syndrome (G89.0) and chronic pain syndrome (G89.4) for cases where the provider specifically documents these exact conditions.
- Assign pain associated with devices, implants, or grafts to Chapter 19 codes, using specific T codes for device-related pain.
- Use additional codes from category G89 (G89.18 or G89.28) to identify acute or chronic pain due to the presence of a device, implant, or graft.
- Differentiate between general 'chronic pain' terminology and the specific diagnosis of 'chronic pain syndrome' to ensure correct code selection.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider must specifically document 'Central pain syndrome' or 'chronic pain syndrome' to use codes G89.0 or G89.4 respectively.
- Pain must be associated with a specific postoperative complication or a device/implant/graft to be assigned to Chapter 19 or T codes.
- Codes G89.18 or G89.28 should be used as additional codes to identify the acute or chronic nature of the pain when applicable.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `585_740`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Central pain syndrome (G89.0) and chronic pain syndrome (G89.4) are different than the term 'chronic pain,' and therefore codes should only be used when the provider has specifically documented this condition.
>
> See Section I.C.5. Pain disorders related to psychological factors
>
> Pain associated with devices, implants or grafts left in a surgical site (for example painful hip prosthesis) is assigned to the appropriate code(s) found in Chapter 19, Injury, poisoning, and certain other consequences of external causes. Specific codes for pain due to medical devices are found in the T code section of the ICD-10-CM. Use additional code(s) from category G89 to identify acute or chronic pain due to presence of the device, implant or graft (G89.18 or G89.28).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Pain Code Differentiation

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes G89.0 and G89.4 must only be used when the provider specifically documents 'central pain syndrome' or 'chronic pain syndrome' respectively, distinguishing them from general 'chronic pain' documentation.
- Pain associated with devices, implants, or grafts is assigned to appropriate Chapter 19 codes (Injury, poisoning, and certain other consequences of external causes) rather than general pain codes.
- Additional codes from category G89 (specifically G89.18 or G89.28) are used to identify acute or chronic pain due to the presence of a device, implant, or graft.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider must specifically document 'central pain syndrome' to assign code G89.0.
- Provider must specifically document 'chronic pain syndrome' to assign code G89.4.
- Pain due to devices/implants/grafts requires assignment of a Chapter 19 code first, followed by an additional code from G89.18 or G89.28.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 guideline explicitly instructs coders to assign postoperative pain to Chapter 19 and reserve G89.0/G89.4 for specifically documented syndromes. The October 2025 version retains these exact instructions, including the requirement to use Chapter 19 codes for device-related pain and the specific usage of G89.18 or G89.28 as additional codes. There are no substantive alterations to the coding logic, definitions, or behavioral requirements between the two versions.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Assigns postoperative pain to Chapter 19 and reserves G89.0/G89.4 for specific syndromes -> October 2025 (New Version): Assigns postoperative pain to Chapter 19 and reserves G89.0/G89.4 for specific syndromes
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Uses Chapter 19 and T codes for device pain with G89.18/28 as additional codes -> October 2025 (New Version): Uses Chapter 19 and T codes for device pain with G89.18/28 as additional codes

*Traceability: Chunk `154_157_312` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `585_740` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 124. ICD-10 Combination Code Implementation
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles address the implementation of ICD-10 combination codes, with the newer version expanding the scope to include specific hypertensive heart and kidney disease categories alongside the existing poison/toxic effect rules.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `275_301`

> **Actual Text**
>
> ICD-10-CM contains a number of combination codes that identify both the definitive diagnosis and common symptoms of that diagnosis. When using one of these combination codes, an additional code should not be assigned for the symptom.
>
> Codes in categories T36-T65 are combination codes that include the substance that was taken as well as the intent. No additional external cause code is required for poisonings, toxic effects, adverse effects and underdosing codes.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Combination Code Implementation

**Key Behaviors:**
- Do not assign an additional code for symptoms when a combination code identifies both the definitive diagnosis and the symptom.
- Do not assign an additional external cause code for poisonings, toxic effects, adverse effects, and underdosing codes found in categories T36-T65.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The combination code must explicitly identify both the definitive diagnosis and the common symptom.
- The code must be from categories T36-T65 to avoid requiring an additional external cause code for intent-related substance effects.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `595_702_728`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The codes in category I13, Hypertensive heart and chronic kidney disease, are combination codes that include hypertension, heart disease and chronic kidney disease. Assign codes from combination category I13, Hypertensive heart and chronic kidney disease, when there is hypertension with both heart and chronic kidney disease . If heart failure is present, assign an additional code from category I50 to identify the type of heart failure.
>
> The appropriate code from category N18, Chronic kidney disease, should be used as a secondary code with a code from category I13 to identify the stage of chronic kidney disease.
>
> See Section I.C.14. Chronic kidney disease.
>
> The Includes note at I13 specifies that the conditions included at I11 and I12 are included together in I13. If a patient has hypertension, heart disease and chronic kidney disease, then a code from I13 should be used, not codes from I11 or I12,
>
> For patients with both acute renal failure and chronic kidney disease, the acute renal failure should also be coded. Sequence according to the circumstances of the admission/encounter.
>
> ICD-10-CM contains a number of combination codes that identify both the definitive diagnosis and common symptoms of that diagnosis. When using one of these combination codes, an additional code should not be assigned for the symptom.
>
> Codes in categories T36-T65 are combination codes that include the substance that was taken as well as the intent. No additional external cause code is required for poisonings, toxic effects, adverse effects and underdosing codes.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Combination Code Implementation

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes from combination category I13 when hypertension is present with both heart and chronic kidney disease.
- Do not assign codes from categories I11 or I12 if a code from I13 is applicable for the same conditions.
- Do not assign an additional code for symptoms when a combination code already identifies the definitive diagnosis and common symptoms.
- No additional external cause code is required for poisonings, toxic effects, adverse effects, and underdosing codes in categories T36-T65.
- Assign an additional code from category I50 to identify the type of heart failure if present alongside I13 codes.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Hypertension must be documented with both heart disease and chronic kidney disease to use I13.
- The appropriate code from category N18 must be used as a secondary code with a code from category I13 to identify the stage of chronic kidney disease.
- For patients with both acute renal failure and chronic kidney disease, acute renal failure must also be coded.
- Acute renal failure and chronic kidney disease codes must be sequenced according to the circumstances of the admission or encounter.
- The Includes note at I13 must be referenced to confirm that conditions included at I11 and I12 are included together in I13.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Scope Expansion   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older guideline focused exclusively on prohibiting additional codes for symptoms in general combination codes and specifically for poisonings in categories T36-T65. The newer version retains these rules but significantly expands the scope by introducing mandatory assignment rules for category I13 (Hypertensive heart and chronic kidney disease), requiring specific sequencing with categories I50 and N18.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Rules limited to general symptom combination codes and categories T36-T65 -> October 2025 (New Version): Added mandatory rules for category I13 assignment when hypertension coexists with heart and chronic kidney disease.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): No mention of sequencing requirements for heart failure or kidney disease stages -> October 2025 (New Version): Requires additional code from category I50 for heart failure and secondary code from category N18 for chronic kidney disease stages.

*Traceability: Chunk `275_301` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `595_702_728` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 125. ICD-10 Category M81 Osteoporosis Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for ICD-10 Category M81, including the exclusion of site codes and the requirement for status code Z87.310.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `208_209`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Osteoporosis is a systemic condition, meaning that all bones of the musculoskeletal system are affected. Therefore, site is not a component of the codes under category M81, Osteoporosis without current pathological fracture. The site codes under category M80, Osteoporosis with current pathological fracture, identify the site of the fracture, not the osteoporosis.
>
> Category M81, Osteoporosis without current pathological fracture, is for use for patients with osteoporosis who do not currently have a pathologic fracture due to the osteoporosis, even if they have had a fracture in the past. For patients with a history of osteoporosis fractures, status code Z87.310, Personal history of (healed) osteoporosis fracture, should follow the code from M81.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Category M81 Osteoporosis Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Site is not a component of codes under category M81 for osteoporosis without current pathological fracture.
- Site codes under category M80 identify the fracture site, not the osteoporosis itself.
- Category M81 is used for patients with osteoporosis who do not currently have a pathologic fracture due to the condition, even if they have had a fracture in the past.
- Status code Z87.310 (Personal history of osteoporosis fracture) should follow the M81 code for patients with a history of osteoporosis fractures.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Confirm the patient does not currently have a pathologic fracture due to osteoporosis before assigning category M81.
- If a history of osteoporosis fracture exists, append status code Z87.310 after the primary M81 code.
- Do not assign site-specific codes to the osteoporosis diagnosis itself under category M81.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `637_638`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Osteoporosis is a systemic condition, meaning that all bones of the musculoskeletal system are affected. Therefore, site is not a component of the codes under category M81, Osteoporosis without current pathological fracture. The site codes under category M80, Osteoporosis with current pathological fracture, identify the site of the fracture, not the osteoporosis.
>

For patients with a history of osteoporosis fractures, status code Z87.310, Personal history of (healed) osteoporosis fracture, should follow the code from M81.

- Category M81 is used for patients with osteoporosis who do not currently have a pathologic fracture due to the osteoporosis, even if they have had a fracture in the past.
- Status code Z87.310 (Personal history of osteoporosis fracture) should follow the M81 code for patients with a history of osteoporosis fractures.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be osteoporosis without current pathological fracture due to the osteoporosis.
- If a history of osteoporosis fracture exists, status code Z87.310 must follow the primary M81 code.
- Codes under M81 do not include site specificity.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older and newer versions contain verbatim identical text regarding the systemic nature of osteoporosis and the exclusion of site components in Category M81. Both guidelines explicitly state that site codes belong to Category M80 and mandate the use of status code Z87.310 for patients with a history of healed fractures. There are no functional differences in the coding rules, definitions, or requirements between the two versions.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Site is not a component of codes under category M81 for osteoporosis without current pathological fracture. -> October 2025 (New Version): Site is not a component of codes under category M81 for osteoporosis without current pathological fracture.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Status code Z87.310 should follow the M81 code for patients with a history of osteoporosis fractures. -> October 2025 (New Version): Status code Z87.310 should follow the M81 code for patients with a history of osteoporosis fractures.

### 126. ICD-10 Category O10 Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe identical coding rules for ICD-10 categories O10 and P36 with no substantive changes in logic or requirements.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `227_268`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Category O10, Pre-existing hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, includes codes for hypertensive heart and hypertensive chronic kidney disease. When assigning one of the O10 codes that includes hypertensive heart disease or hypertensive chronic kidney disease, it is necessary to add a secondary code from the appropriate hypertension category to specify the type of heart failure or chronic kidney disease.
>
> See Section I.C.9. Hypertension.
>
> Category P36, Bacterial sepsis of newborn, includes congenital sepsis. If a perinate is documented as having sepsis without documentation of congenital or community acquired, the default is congenital and a code from category P36 should be assigned. If the P36 code includes the causal organism, an additional code from category B95, Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and Enterococcus as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, or B96, Other bacterial agents as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, should not be assigned. If the P36 code does not include the causal organism, assign an additional code from category B96. If applicable, use additional codes to identify severe sepsis (R65.2-) and any associated acute organ dysfunction.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Category O10 and P36 Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Category O10 includes codes for hypertensive heart and hypertensive chronic kidney disease.
- When assigning an O10 code with heart or kidney disease, a secondary code from the hypertension category must be added to specify the type of failure or disease.
- Category P36 includes congenital sepsis.
- If sepsis is documented without specifying congenital or community-acquired, the default is congenital (Category P36).
- If the P36 code does not include the causal organism, an additional code from category B96 must be assigned.
- If the P36 code includes the causal organism, an additional code from category B95 or B96 should not be assigned.
- The default assignment for unspecified sepsis origin in perinates is congenital sepsis (Category P36).

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Secondary code from the appropriate hypertension category is required when O10 includes hypertensive heart disease or hypertensive chronic kidney disease.
- Additional code from category B96 is required if the P36 code does not include the causal organism.
- Additional codes for severe sepsis (R65.2-) and acute organ dysfunction should be used if applicable.
- A secondary code from the appropriate hypertension category is required when assigning an O10 code that includes hypertensive heart disease or hypertensive chronic kidney disease.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `656_695`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Category O10, Pre-existing hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, includes codes for hypertensive heart and hypertensive chronic kidney disease. If the P36 code does not include the causal organism, assign an additional code from category B96. If applicable, use additional codes to identify severe sepsis (R65.2-) and any associated acute organ dysfunction.

- If a P36 code includes the causal organism, additional codes from categories B95 or B96 should not be assigned.

#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older and newer versions present the exact same clinical definitions and coding instructions for hypertensive heart/kidney disease in O10 and bacterial sepsis in P36. The only differences are minor formatting adjustments, such as the removal of specific examples (Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, Enterococcus) in the new version's behavior list and a slight wording shift regarding severe sepsis codes from 'should be used' to 'may be assigned'. These changes do not alter the underlying coding logic or compliance requirements.

**Key Differences:**

- October 1, 2024 (Older): If the P36 code includes the causal organism, an additional code from category B95, Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and Enterococcus... or B96... should not be assigned. -> October 2025 (New Version): If a P36 code includes the causal organism, additional codes from categories B95 or B96 should not be assigned.

### 127. Factitious Disorder Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Factitious Disorder Coding Guidelines with verbatim matching behaviors and raw text excerpts.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `145`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Factitious disorder imposed on self or Munchausen's syndrome is a disorder in which a person falsely reports or causes his or her own physical or psychological signs or symptoms. For patients with documented factitious disorder on self or Munchausen's syndrome, assign the appropriate code from subcategory F68.1-, Factitious disorder imposed on self.
>
> Munchausen's syndrome by proxy (MSBP) is a disorder in which a caregiver (perpetrator) falsely reports or causes an illness or injury in another person (victim) under his or her care, such as a child, an elderly adult, or a person who has a disability. The condition is also referred to as 'factitious disorder imposed on another' or 'factitious disorder by proxy.' The perpetrator, not the victim, receives this diagnosis. Assign code F68.A, Factitious disorder imposed on another, to the perpetrator's record. For the victim of a patient suffering from MSBP, assign the appropriate code from categories T74, Adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment, confirmed, or T76, Adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment, suspected.
>
> See Section I.C.19.f. Adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Factitious Disorder Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code F68.1- for patients with documented factitious disorder imposed on self or Munchausen's syndrome.
- Assign code F68.A to the perpetrator's record for Munchausen's syndrome by proxy (MSBP) or factitious disorder imposed on another.
- Assign codes from categories T74 or T76 to the victim of MSBP based on whether the abuse is confirmed or suspected.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Diagnosis must be documented as factitious disorder imposed on self or Munchausen's syndrome for F68.1- assignment.
- Diagnosis must be documented as factitious disorder imposed on another for F68.A assignment to the perpetrator.
- Abuse status (confirmed or suspected) must be determined to select between T74 and T76 for the victim.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `575`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Factitious disorder imposed on self or Munchausen's syndrome is a disorder in which a person falsely reports or causes his or her own physical or psychological signs or symptoms. For patients with documented factitious disorder on self or Munchausen's syndrome, assign the appropriate code from subcategory F68.1-, Factitious disorder imposed on self.
>
> Munchausen's syndrome by proxy (MSBP) is a disorder in which a caregiver (perpetrator) falsely reports or causes an illness or injury in another person (victim) under his or her care, such as a child, an elderly adult, or a person who has a disability. The condition is also referred to as 'factitious disorder imposed on another' or 'factitious disorder by proxy.' The perpetrator, not the victim, receives this diagnosis. Assign code F68.A, Factitious disorder imposed on another, to the perpetrator's record. For the victim of a patient suffering from MSBP, assign the appropriate code from categories T74, Adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment, confirmed, or T76, Adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment, suspected.
>
> See Section I.C.19.f. Adult and child abuse, neglect and other maltreatment

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Factitious Disorder Coding Guidelines

- Assign code F68.A to the perpetrator's record for Munchausen's syndrome by proxy (MSBP) or factitious disorder imposed on another.
- Assign codes from categories T74 or T76 to the victim of MSBP based on whether the abuse is confirmed or suspected.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Diagnosis must be documented as factitious disorder imposed on self or Munchausen's syndrome for F68.1- assignment.
- Diagnosis must be documented as factitious disorder imposed on another for F68.A assignment to the perpetrator.
- Abuse status (confirmed or suspected) must be determined to select between T74 and T76 categories for the victim.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older and newer versions contain identical instructions for assigning ICD-10 codes F68.1- and F68.A for factitious disorder cases. The guidelines for coding victims using T74 or T76 based on confirmed or suspected abuse status are also exactly the same in both texts. There are no logical, clinical, or procedural differences between the two versions; they appear to be duplicates.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code F68.1- for self-imposed cases and F68.A for perpetrator cases -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign code F68.1- for self-imposed cases and F68.A for perpetrator cases
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign T74/T76 to victims based on confirmed/suspected abuse -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign T74/T76 to victims based on confirmed/suspected abuse

*Traceability: Chunk `145` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `575` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 128. Substance Use Disorder Remission Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `140`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Selection of codes describing 'in remission' for categories F10-F19, Mental and behavioral disorders due to psychoactive substance use (categories F10-F19 with -.11, -.21, -.91) requires the provider's clinical judgment and are assigned only on the basis of provider documentation (as defined in the Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting), unless otherwise instructed by the classification.
>
> Mild substance use disorders in early or sustained remission are classified to the appropriate codes for substance abuse in remission, and moderate or severe substance use disorders in early or sustained remission are classified to the appropriate codes for substance dependence in remission.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Substance Use Disorder Remission Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes describing 'in remission' for categories F10-F19 require provider's clinical judgment and are assigned only on the basis of provider documentation.
- Mild substance use disorders in early or sustained remission are classified to the appropriate codes for substance abuse in remission.
- Moderate or severe substance use disorders in early or sustained remission are classified to the appropriate codes for substance dependence in remission.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider must document the condition as being in remission.
- Provider must exercise clinical judgment to determine the severity level (mild, moderate, or severe).
- Codes must be selected from categories F10-F19 with specific extensions (e.g., -.11, -.21, -.91).

### 129. ICD-10 History Z Code Implementation
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the implementation rules for ICD-10 History Z codes across versions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `350_351`

> **Actual Text**
>
> There are two types of history Z codes, personal and family. Personal history codes explain a patient's past medical condition that no longer exists and is not receiving any treatment, but that has the potential for recurrence, and therefore may require continued monitoring.
>
> Family history codes are for use when a patient has a family member(s) who has had a particular disease that causes the patient to be at higher risk of also contracting the disease.
>
> Personal history codes may be used in conjunction with follow-up codes and family history codes may be used in conjunction with screening codes to explain the need for a test or procedure. History codes are also acceptable on any medical record regardless of the reason for visit. A history of an illness, even if no longer present, is important information that may alter the type of treatment ordered.
>
> The reason for the encounter (for example, screening or counseling) should be sequenced first and the appropriate personal and/or family history code(s) should be assigned as additional diagnos(es).
>
> The history Z code categories are:
>
> {"Z80": "Z81", "Family history of primary malignant neoplasm": "Family history of mental and behavioral disorders"}
> {"Z80": "Z82", "Family history of primary malignant neoplasm": "Family history of certain disabilities and chronic diseases (leading to disablement)"}
> {"Z80": "Z83", "Family history of primary malignant neoplasm": "Family history of other specific disorders"}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 History Z Code Implementation

**Key Behaviors:**
- Personal history codes explain past medical conditions no longer present but with potential for recurrence requiring monitoring.
- Family history codes are used when a patient has a family member with a disease causing higher risk of contracting it.
- History codes are acceptable on any medical record regardless of the reason for visit.
- The reason for the encounter (e.g., screening) should be sequenced first, with history codes assigned as additional diagnoses.
- History codes may be used in conjunction with follow-up or screening codes to explain the need for a test or procedure.
- Personal history codes are used when a past medical condition no longer exists and is not receiving any treatment.
- Family history codes are used when a family member has had a particular disease causing higher risk for the patient.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be documented as a past medical history for personal history codes.
- Risk must be linked to a family member's disease for family history codes.
- Encounter reason (e.g., screening) must be sequenced before history codes.
- Condition must not be currently receiving treatment for personal history codes.
- History codes must be assigned as additional diagnoses, not primary, when a specific encounter reason exists.
- The past medical condition must no longer exist and not be receiving treatment for personal history codes to be valid.
- The family member must have had the disease (past tense) for family history codes to be applicable.

---

- History codes are acceptable on any medical record regardless of the reason for visit.
- The reason for the encounter (e.g., screening or counseling) should be sequenced first, with history codes assigned as additional diagnoses.
- History codes may be used in conjunction with follow-up or screening codes to explain the need for a test or procedure.
- Personal history codes apply to conditions that no longer exist and are not receiving any treatment.
- Family history codes apply when a family member has had a particular disease causing higher risk.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be documented as a past medical history for personal history codes.
- Risk must be established via family member disease for family history codes.
- Reason for encounter must be sequenced before history codes.
- History codes must be assigned as additional diagnoses, not primary, when a reason for encounter exists.
- Codes must reflect that the condition is no longer present (personal) or represents risk (family).
- Personal history codes require the condition to be documented as no longer existing and not receiving treatment.
- Family history codes require documentation that a family member has had a particular disease.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present identical logic for sequencing encounter reasons before history codes and defining the criteria for personal versus family history codes. The text excerpts provided for both versions are verbatim identical, indicating no substantive update to the coding rules. Minor variations in the extracted profile summaries (e.g., 'Risk must be linked' vs 'Risk must be established') do not reflect a change in the underlying guideline behavior.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): History codes are acceptable on any medical record regardless of the reason for visit. -> October 2025 (New Version): History codes are acceptable on any medical record regardless of the reason for visit.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): The reason for the encounter (e.g., screening) should be sequenced first. -> October 2025 (New Version): The reason for the encounter (e.g., screening or counseling) should be sequenced first.

*Traceability: Chunk `350_351` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `783_784` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 130. ICD-10 Coding for Retained Products of Conception
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for retained products of conception, including specific code assignments and complication handling.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `249`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Subsequent encounters for retained products of conception following a spontaneous abortion or elective termination of pregnancy , without complications are assigned O03.4, Incomplete spontaneous abortion without complication, or code O07.4, Failed attempted termination of pregnancy without complication. This advice is appropriate even when the patient was discharged previously with a discharge diagnosis of complete abortion. If the patient has a specific complication associated with the spontaneous abortion or elective termination of pregnancy in addition to retained products of conception, assign the appropriate complication code (e.g., O03.-, O04.-, O07.-) instead of code O03.4 or O07.4.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Coding for Retained Products of Conception

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign O03.4 for subsequent encounters of incomplete spontaneous abortion without complication.
- Assign O07.4 for subsequent encounters of failed attempted termination of pregnancy without complication.
- Apply these codes even if the patient was previously discharged with a diagnosis of complete abortion.
- Assign specific complication codes (O03.-, O04.-, O07.-) instead of O03.4 or O07.4 if complications are present.
- Use these codes for subsequent encounters following spontaneous abortion or elective termination.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be retained products of conception following spontaneous abortion or elective termination.
- Encounter must be subsequent to the initial event.
- No complications must be present to use O03.4 or O07.4.
- If complications exist, the appropriate complication code must be assigned instead.

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `255`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Codes in this chapter are never for use on the maternal record. Codes from Chapter 15, the obstetric chapter, are never permitted on the newborn record. Chapter 16 codes may be used throughout the life of the patient if the condition is still present.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Chapter Usage Restrictions

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes in this chapter are never for use on the maternal record.
- Codes from Chapter 15 (obstetric) are never permitted on the newborn record.
- Chapter 16 codes may be used throughout the life of the patient if the condition is still present.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be present to use Chapter 16 codes.
- Maternal record must not contain codes from this chapter.
- Newborn record must not contain codes from Chapter 15.

### 134. ICD-10 Z-Code Exposure Classification
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding ICD-10 Z-Code Exposure Classification with nearly identical behavioral definitions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `342`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Category Z20 indicates contact with, and suspected exposure to, communicable diseases. These codes are for patients who are suspected to have been exposed to a disease by close personal contact with an infected individual or are in an area where a disease is epidemic.
>
> Category Z77, Other contact with and (suspected) exposures hazardous to health, indicates contact with and suspected exposures hazardous to health.
>
> Contact/exposure codes may be used as a first-listed code to explain an encounter for testing, or, more commonly, as a secondary code to identify a potential risk.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Z-Code Exposure Classification

**Key Behaviors:**
- Category Z20 codes are used for suspected exposure to communicable diseases via close personal contact or in epidemic areas.
- Category Z77 codes are used for contact with and suspected exposures hazardous to health.
- Contact/exposure codes may be assigned as a first-listed code to explain an encounter for testing.
- Contact/exposure codes are commonly assigned as secondary codes to identify a potential risk.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must indicate suspected exposure to a communicable disease or hazardous health condition.
- Exposure must be via close personal contact with an infected individual or in an area where a disease is epidemic (for Z20).
- Exposure must be hazardous to health (for Z77).

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `772`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Category Z20 indicates contact with, and suspected exposure to, communicable diseases. These codes are for patients who are suspected to have been exposed to a disease by close personal contact with an infected individual or are in an area where a disease is epidemic.
>
> Category Z77, Other contact with and (suspected) exposures hazardous to health, indicates contact with and suspected exposures hazardous to health.
>
> Contact/exposure codes may be used as a first-listed code to explain an encounter for testing, or, more commonly, as a secondary code to identify a potential risk.

- Contact/exposure codes may be assigned as a first-listed code to explain an encounter for testing.
- Contact/exposure codes are commonly assigned as secondary codes to identify a potential risk.

### 135. ICD-10 T86 Transplant Complication Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for ICD-10 T86 transplant complications with no behavioral differences.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `313`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Codes under category T86, Complications of transplanted organs and tissues, are for use for both complications and rejection of transplanted organs. A transplant complication code is only assigned if the complication affects the function of the transplanted organ. Two codes are required to fully describe a transplant complication : the appropriate code from category T86 and a secondary code that identifies the complication.
>
> Pre-existing conditions or conditions that develop after the transplant are not coded as complications unless they affect the function of the transplanted organs.
>
> See I.C.21. for transplant organ removal status
>
> See I.C.2. for malignant neoplasm associated with transplanted organ.
>
> See I.C.1.d.4. for sequencing of sepsis due to infection in transplanted organ

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 T86 Transplant Complication Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes under category T86 are used for both complications and rejection of transplanted organs.
- A transplant complication code is only assigned if the complication affects the function of the transplanted organ.
- Two codes are required to fully describe a transplant complication: the appropriate code from category T86 and a secondary code that identifies the complication.
- Pre-existing conditions or conditions that develop after the transplant are not coded as complications unless they affect the function of the transplanted organs.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The complication must affect the function of the transplanted organ.
- Two codes must be assigned: one from category T86 and a secondary code identifying the complication.
- Refer to I.C.21 for transplant organ removal status.
- Refer to I.C.2 for malignant neoplasm associated with transplanted organ.
- Refer to I.C.1.d.4 for sequencing of sepsis due to infection in transplanted organ.
