### 136. ICD-10 Status Code Definitions
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles describe the ICD-10 Status Code Definitions and their application rules, specifically regarding carrier status, sequelae, and sequencing logic.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `344_345_346`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Status codes indicate that a patient is either a carrier of a disease or has the sequelae or residual of a past disease or condition. This includes such things as the presence of prosthetic or mechanical devices resulting from past treatment. A status code is informative, because the status may affect the course of treatment and its outcome. A status code is distinct from a history code. The history code indicates that the patient no longer has the condition.
>
> A status code should not be used with a diagnosis code from one of the body system chapters, if the diagnosis code includes the information provided by the status code. For example, code Z94.1, Heart transplant status, should not be used with a code from subcategory T86.2, Complications of heart transplant. The status code does not provide additional information. The complication code indicates that the patient is a heart transplant patient.
>
> For encounters for weaning from a mechanical ventilator, assign a code from subcategory J96.1, Chronic respiratory failure, followed by code Z99.11, Dependence on respirator [ventilator] status.
>
> The status Z codes/categories are:
>
> Z14
>
> Z15
>
> Z16
>
> Z17
>
> Z18
>
> Z19
>
> Genetic carrier
>
> Genetic carrier status indicates that a person carries a gene, associated with a particular disease, which may be passed to offspring who may develop that disease. The person does not have the disease and is not at risk of developing the disease.
>
> Genetic susceptibility to disease
>
> Genetic susceptibility indicates that a person has a gene that increases the risk of that person developing the disease.
>
> Codes from category Z15 should not be used as principal or first-listed codes. If the patient has the condition to which he/she is susceptible, and that condition is the reason for the encounter, the code for the current condition should be sequenced first. If the patient is being seen for follow-up after completed treatment for this condition, and the condition no longer exists, a follow-up code should be sequenced first, followed by the appropriate personal history and genetic susceptibility codes. If the purpose of the encounter is genetic counseling associated with procreative management, code Z31.5, Encounter for genetic counseling, should be assigned as the first-listed code, followed by a code from category Z15. Additional codes should be assigned for any applicable family or personal history.
>
> Resistance to antimicrobial drugs
>
> This code indicates that a patient has a condition that is resistant to antimicrobial drug treatment. Sequence the infection code first.
>
> Estrogen , and other hormones and factors receptor status
>
> Retained foreign body fragments
>
> Hormone sensitivity malignancy status
>
> - Z21 Asymptomatic HIV infection status This code indicates that a patient has tested positive for HIV but has manifested no signs or symptoms of the disease.
> - Z22 Carrier of infectious disease Carrier status indicates that a person harbors the specific organisms of a disease without manifest symptoms and is capable of transmitting the infection.
> - Z28.3 Underimmunization status
>
> See Section I.B.14. for underimmunization documentation by clinicians other than the patient's provider .
>
> - Z33.1 Pregnant state, incidental This code is a secondary code only for use when the pregnancy is in no way complicating the reason for visit. Otherwise, a code from the obstetric chapter is required.
> - Z66 Do not resuscitate This code may be used when it is documented by the provider that a patient is on do not resuscitate status at any time during the stay.
> - Z67 Blood type Z68 Body mass index (BMI) BMI codes should only be assigned when there is an associated, reportable diagnosis (such as obesity). Do not assign BMI codes during pregnancy.
>
> See Section I.B.14. for BMI documentation by clinicians other than the patient's provider .
>
> {"Z74.01": "Z76.82 Z78", "Bed confinement status": "Awaiting organ transplant status Other specified health status Code Z78.1, Physical restraint status, may be used when it is documented by the provider that a patient has been put in restraints during the current encounter. Please note that this code should not be reported when it is documented by the provider that a patient is temporarily restrained during a procedure."}
> {"Z74.01": "Z79", "Bed confinement status": "Long-term (current) drug therapy Codes from this category indicate a patient's continuous use of a prescribed drug (including such things as aspirin therapy) for the long-term treatment of a condition or for prophylactic use. It is not for use for patients who have addictions to drugs. This subcategory is not for use of medications for detoxification or maintenance programs to prevent withdrawal symptoms (e.g., methadone maintenance for opiate dependence). Assign the appropriate code for the drug use, abuse, or dependence instead."}
>
> {"": "Z88", "Assign a code from Z79 if the patient is receiving a medication for an extended period as a prophylactic measure (such as for the prevention of deep vein thrombosis) or as treatment of a chronic condition (such as arthritis) or a disease requiring a lengthy course of treatment (such as cancer). Do not assign a code from category Z79 for medication being administered for a brief period of time to treat an acute illness or injury (such as a course of antibiotics to treat acute bronchitis).": "Allergy status to drugs, medicaments and biological substances"}
> {"": "Z89", "Assign a code from Z79 if the patient is receiving a medication for an extended period as a prophylactic measure (such as for the prevention of deep vein thrombosis) or as treatment of a chronic condition (such as arthritis) or a disease requiring a lengthy course of treatment (such as cancer). Do not assign a code from category Z79 for medication being administered for a brief period of time to treat an acute illness or injury (such as a course of antibiotics to treat acute bronchitis).": "Acquired absence of limb"}
> {"": "Z90", "Assign a code from Z79 if the patient is receiving a medication for an extended period as a prophylactic measure (such as for the prevention of deep vein thrombosis) or as treatment of a chronic condition (such as arthritis) or a disease requiring a lengthy course of treatment (such as cancer). Do not assign a code from category Z79 for medication being administered for a brief period of time to treat an acute illness or injury (such as a course of antibiotics to treat acute bronchitis).": "Acquired absence of organs, not elsewhere classified"}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Status Code Definitions and Application

**Key Behaviors:**
- Status codes indicate a patient is a carrier of a disease, has sequelae of a past disease, or has prosthetic/mechanical devices resulting from past treatment.
- Status codes are distinct from history codes; history codes indicate the patient no longer has the condition.
- Status codes should not be used with diagnosis codes from body system chapters if the diagnosis code already includes the information provided by the status code.
- For encounters for weaning from a mechanical ventilator, assign code J96.1 followed by Z99.11.
- BMI codes should only be assigned when there is an associated, reportable diagnosis and must not be assigned during pregnancy.
- Genetic carrier status indicates the person does not have the disease and is not at risk of developing it.
- Genetic susceptibility indicates the person has a gene that increases the risk of developing the disease.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Codes from category Z15 should not be used as principal or first-listed codes unless the patient has the condition and it is the reason for the encounter.
- For genetic counseling associated with procreative management, code Z31.5 should be assigned as the first-listed code followed by a code from category Z15.
- For resistance to antimicrobial drugs, sequence the infection code first.
- Code Z66 (Do not resuscitate) may be used when documented by the provider that a patient is on do not resuscitate status at any time during the stay.
- BMI codes must not be assigned during pregnancy.
- For genetic counseling associated with procreative management, code Z31.5 must be assigned as the first-listed code followed by a code from category Z15.
- For resistance to antimicrobial drugs, the infection code must be sequenced first.

The history code indicates that the patient no longer has the condition.
>
> A status code should not be used with a diagnosis code from one of the body system chapters, if the diagnosis code includes the information provided by the status code. For example, code Z94.1, Heart transplant status, should not be used with a code from subcategory T86.2, Complications of heart transplant. The status code does not provide additional information. The complication code indicates that the patient is a heart transplant patient.
>
> For encounters for weaning from a mechanical ventilator, assign a code from subcategory J96.1, Chronic respiratory failure, followed by code Z99.11, Dependence on respirator [ventilator] status.
>
> {"The status Z codes/categories are:": "Z14 Genetic carrier Genetic carrier status indicates that a person carries a gene, associated with a particular disease, which may be passed to offspring who may develop that disease. The person does not"}
> {"The status Z codes/categories are:": "Z15 Genetic susceptibility to disease Genetic susceptibility indicates that a person has a gene that increases the risk of that person developing the disease."}
> {"The status Z codes/categories are:": "Z16 Codes from category Z15 should generally not be used as principal or first-listed codes. If the patient has the condition to which he/she is susceptible, and that condition is the reason for the encounter, the code for the current condition should sequenced first. If the patient is being seen for follow-up after completed treatment for this condition, and the condition no longer exists, a follow-up code should be sequenced first, followed by the appropriate personal history and genetic susceptibility codes. If the purpose of the encounter is genetic counseling associated with procreative management, code Z31.5, Encounter for genetic counseling, should be assigned as the first-listed code, followed by a code from category Z15. Additional codes should be assigned for any applicable family or personal history."}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Status Code Definitions and Application

**Key Behaviors:**
- Status codes indicate a patient is a carrier of a disease or has sequelae/residuals of a past condition, including prosthetic devices.
- Status codes are distinct from history codes, which indicate the patient no longer has the condition.
- Status codes should not be used with diagnosis codes from body system chapters if the diagnosis code already includes the status information.
- For encounters involving weaning from a mechanical ventilator, assign code J96.1 followed by Z99.11.
- Genetic susceptibility codes (Z15) should generally not be used as principal or first-listed codes unless the susceptibility is the reason for the encounter.
- Status codes include the presence of prosthetic or mechanical devices resulting from past treatment.
- Genetic carrier status (Z14) indicates a person carries a gene associated with a disease that may be passed to offspring, but the person does not currently have the disease.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The status must affect the course of treatment or its outcome to be informative.
- If a diagnosis code includes the information provided by the status code, the status code should not be used.
- For genetic susceptibility, if the patient has the condition, code the current condition first; if the condition no longer exists, code follow-up first.
- For genetic counseling encounters, code Z31.5 as the first-listed code followed by category Z15 codes.
- Status codes must be assigned when the patient is a carrier or has sequelae, even if the condition is not currently active.
- For genetic susceptibility (Z15), if the patient has the condition, code the current condition first; if the condition no longer exists, code follow-up first.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Rule Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version broadly stated that genetic carrier status indicates a person does not have the disease, while the newer version clarifies the specific definition for Z14 as carrying a gene that may be passed to offspring. The newer version also introduces a specific sequencing rule for genetic susceptibility (Z15) based on whether the patient currently has the condition or not, which was not explicitly detailed in the older text. Both versions maintain the core rule that status codes should not be combined with diagnosis codes that already include the status information.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Genetic carrier status indicates the person does not have the disease and is not at risk of developing it. -> October 2025 (New Version): Genetic carrier status (Z14) indicates a person carries a gene associated with a disease that may be passed to offspring, but the person does not currently have the disease.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): General instruction on sequencing infection codes for resistance. -> October 2025 (New Version): Specific sequencing rule for genetic susceptibility (Z15) where the current condition is coded first if present, or follow-up is coded first if the condition no longer exists.

*Traceability: Chunk `344_345_346` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `774_775` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 137. ICD-10 Poisoning Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same ICD-10 Poisoning Coding Guidelines feature with identical logic and behavioral requirements.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `307`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When coding a poisoning or reaction to the improper use of a medication (e.g., overdose, wrong substance given or taken in error, wrong route of administration), first assign the appropriate code from categories T36-T50. The poisoning codes have an associated intent as their 5 th or 6 th character (accidental, intentional self-harm, assault and undetermined). If the intent of the poisoning is unknown or unspecified, code the intent as accidental intent. The undetermined intent is only for use if the documentation in the record specifies that the intent cannot be determined. Use additional code(s) for all manifestations of poisonings.
>
> If there is also a diagnosis of abuse or dependence of the substance, the abuse or dependence is assigned as an additional code.
>
> Examples of poisoning include:
>
> - (i)
> - Error was made in drug prescription
>
> Errors made in drug prescription or in the administration of the drug by provider, nurse, patient, or other person.
>
> - (ii) Overdose of a drug intentionally taken administered and resulted in drug toxicity, it would be
>
> If an overdose of a drug was intentionally taken or coded as a poisoning.
>
> - (iii)Nonprescribed drug taken with correctly prescribed and properly administered drug
>
> If a nonprescribed drug or medicinal agent was taken in combination with a correctly prescribed and properly administered drug, any drug toxicity or other reaction resulting from the interaction of the two drugs would be classified as a poisoning.
>
> - (iv)
> - Interaction of drug(s) and alcohol When a reaction results from the interaction of a drug(s)
>
> and alcohol, this would be classified as poisoning.
>
> See Section I.C.4. if poisoning is the result of insulin pump malfunctions.
>
> For Sequela (Late Effects) see Section I.B.10.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Poisoning Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign appropriate code from categories T36-T50 for poisoning or reaction to improper use of medication.
- Code intent as accidental if unknown or unspecified, and only use undetermined intent if documentation explicitly states it cannot be determined.
- Use additional codes for all manifestations of poisonings.
- Assign abuse or dependence as an additional code if a diagnosis of abuse or dependence of the substance exists.
- Classify drug interactions (including with alcohol) and prescription errors resulting in toxicity as poisoning.
- Classify errors made in drug prescription or administration by any provider, nurse, patient, or other person as poisoning.
- Classify reactions resulting from the interaction of a drug and alcohol as poisoning.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must specify the intent (accidental, intentional self-harm, assault, or undetermined) or default to accidental.
- All manifestations of the poisoning must be coded using additional codes.
- Abuse or dependence diagnoses must be documented to be assigned as additional codes.
- Reaction must result from improper use, overdose, wrong substance, wrong route, or interaction.
- Insulin pump malfunction poisonings must follow Section I.C.4 guidelines.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `734`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When coding a poisoning or reaction to the improper use of a medication (e.g., overdose, wrong substance given or taken in error, wrong route of administration), first assign the appropriate code from categories T36-T50. The poisoning codes have an associated intent as their 5 th or 6 th character (accidental, intentional self-harm, assault and undetermined). If the intent of the poisoning is unknown or unspecified, code the intent as accidental intent. The undetermined intent is only for use if the documentation in the record specifies that the intent cannot be determined. Use additional code(s) for all manifestations of poisonings.
>
> If there is also a diagnosis of abuse or dependence of the substance, the abuse or dependence is assigned as an additional code.
>
> Examples of poisoning include:
>
> - (i) Error was made in drug prescription Errors made in drug prescription or in the administration of the drug by provider, nurse, patient, or other person.
> - (ii) Overdose of a drug intentionally taken If an overdose of a drug was intentionally taken or administered and resulted in drug toxicity, it would be coded as a poisoning.
>
> - (iii)Nonprescribed drug taken with correctly prescribed and properly administered drug
>
> If a nonprescribed drug or medicinal agent was taken in combination with a correctly prescribed and properly administered drug, any drug toxicity or other reaction resulting from the interaction of the two drugs would be classified as a poisoning.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Poisoning Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign appropriate code from categories T36-T50 for poisoning or reaction to improper use of medication.
- Code intent as accidental if unknown or unspecified, and only use undetermined intent if documentation explicitly states it cannot be determined.
- Use additional codes for all manifestations of poisonings.
- Assign abuse or dependence as an additional code if a diagnosis of abuse or dependence of the substance exists.
- Classify drug toxicity or reactions resulting from interactions between nonprescribed and correctly prescribed drugs as poisoning.
- Include errors made in drug prescription or administration by providers, nurses, patients, or other persons as examples of poisoning.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Intent must be documented to determine if it is accidental, intentional self-harm, assault, or undetermined.
- Additional codes are required for all manifestations of the poisoning.
- Abuse or dependence must be documented as a separate diagnosis to be assigned as an additional code.
- Documentation must specify that intent cannot be determined to use the undetermined intent code.
- The reaction must result from improper use, overdose, wrong substance, wrong route, or drug interactions.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions present identical coding protocols for categories T36-T50, including the rule to default to accidental intent when unspecified. Both versions mandate the use of additional codes for all manifestations and require separate documentation for abuse or dependence diagnoses. The text excerpts confirm that the specific examples and classification criteria for drug interactions and prescription errors remain unchanged between the two versions.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Code intent as accidental if unknown or unspecified, and only use undetermined intent if documentation explicitly states it cannot be determined. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code intent as accidental if unknown or unspecified, and only use undetermined intent if documentation explicitly states it cannot be determined.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Classify drug interactions (including with alcohol) and prescription errors resulting in toxicity as poisoning. -> October 2025 (New Version): Classify drug toxicity or reactions resulting from interactions between nonprescribed and correctly prescribed drugs as poisoning.

*Traceability: Chunk `307` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `734` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 138. ICD-10-CM Prophylactic Surgery Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same coding logic for prophylactic surgery, with the new version explicitly adding Z40.8 to the scope of principal codes.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `371`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For encounters specifically for prophylactic removal of an organ (such as prophylactic removal of breasts due to a genetic susceptibility to cancer or a family history of cancer), the principal or first-listed code should be a code from category Z40, Encounter for prophylactic surgery, followed by the appropriate codes to identify the associated risk factor (such as genetic susceptibility or family history).
>
> If the patient has a malignancy of one site and is having prophylactic removal at another site to prevent either a new primary malignancy or metastatic disease, a code for the malignancy should also be assigned in addition to a code from subcategory Z40.0, Encounter for prophylactic surgery for risk factors related to malignant neoplasms. A Z40.0 code should not be assigned if the patient is having organ removal for treatment of a malignancy, such as the removal of the testes for the treatment of prostate cancer.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Prophylactic Surgery Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign category Z40 as the principal or first-listed code for encounters specifically for prophylactic organ removal.
- Follow the Z40 code with appropriate codes to identify associated risk factors such as genetic susceptibility or family history.
- Assign a code for an existing malignancy in addition to a Z40.0 code when prophylactic removal is performed at a different site to prevent new primary or metastatic disease.
- Do not assign a Z40.0 code if the organ removal is for the treatment of an existing malignancy (e.g., testis removal for prostate cancer treatment).

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The encounter must be specifically for prophylactic removal of an organ.
- Risk factors (genetic susceptibility, family history) must be documented to support the Z40 code selection.
- If treating an existing malignancy at a different site, both the malignancy code and the Z40.0 code must be assigned.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `801`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For encounters specifically for prophylactic removal of an organ (such as prophylactic removal of breasts due to a genetic susceptibility to cancer or a family history of cancer), the principal or first-listed code should be a code from subcategory Z40.0, Encounter for prophylactic surgery for risk factors related to malignant neoplasms, or subcategory Z40.8, Encounter for other prophylactic surgery. If applicable, assign additional code(s) to identify any associated risk factor (such as genetic susceptibility or family history).
>
> If the patient has a malignancy of one site and is having prophylactic removal at another site to prevent either a new primary malignancy or metastatic disease, a code for the malignancy should also be assigned in addition to a code from subcategory Z40.0, Encounter for prophylactic surgery for risk factors related to malignant neoplasms. A Z40.0 code should not be assigned if the patient is having organ removal for treatment of a malignancy, such as the removal of the testes for the treatment of prostate cancer.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Prophylactic Surgery Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign principal code from subcategory Z40.0 or Z40.8 for encounters specifically for prophylactic organ removal due to risk factors.
- Assign additional codes to identify associated risk factors such as genetic susceptibility or family history.
- Assign a code for the existing malignancy in addition to a Z40.0 code when prophylactic removal is performed at a different site to prevent new primary or metastatic disease.
- Do not assign a Z40.0 code if the organ removal is for the treatment of an existing malignancy (e.g., testis removal for prostate cancer).

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Encounter must be specifically for prophylactic removal of an organ.
- Removal must be due to risk factors related to malignant neoplasms (e.g., genetic susceptibility, family history).
- If applicable, associated risk factors must be documented to justify additional coding.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Scope Expansion   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older guideline restricted the principal code assignment to Category Z40 generally, while the newer version explicitly expands the scope to include subcategory Z40.8 for encounters involving other prophylactic surgeries. Both versions maintain identical logic regarding the requirement to code concurrent malignancies at different sites and the prohibition against coding Z40.0 for therapeutic removal of existing cancer. The primary delta is the explicit inclusion of Z40.8 in the principal code selection criteria in the 2025 version.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Principal code should be from Category Z40 generally -> October 2025 (New Version): Principal code should be from subcategory Z40.0 or Z40.8 specifically.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Followed by appropriate codes to identify risk factors -> October 2025 (New Version): Explicitly mandates assigning additional codes for associated risk factors if applicable.

*Traceability: Chunk `371` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `801` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 139. ICD-10-CM Pressure Ulcer Classification

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `194`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Codes in category L89, Pressure ulcer, identify the site and stage of the pressure ulcer.
>
> The ICD-10-CM classifies pressure ulcer stages based on severity, which is designated by stages 1-4, deep tissue pressure injury , unspecified stage, and unstageable.
>
> Assign as many codes from category L89 as needed to identify all the pressure ulcers the patient has, if applicable.
>
> See Section I.B.14. for pressure ulcer stage documentation by clinicians other than patient's provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Pressure Ulcer Classification

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes in category L89 identify the site and stage of the pressure ulcer.
- Classification is based on severity designated by stages 1-4, deep tissue pressure injury, unspecified stage, and unstageable.
- Assign as many codes from category L89 as needed to identify all pressure ulcers the patient has, if applicable.
- Refer to Section I.B.14 for pressure ulcer stage documentation by clinicians other than the patient's provider.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must identify the specific site of the pressure ulcer.
- Documentation must identify the stage of the pressure ulcer (1-4, deep tissue pressure injury, unspecified, or unstageable).
- Multiple codes from category L89 may be assigned if the patient has multiple pressure ulcers.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM P07 Category Implementation

**Key Behaviors:**

- Codes from category P07 are located in Section I.C.21, Factors influencing health status and contact with health services, Status.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The patient must have been premature or had a low birth weight as a newborn.
- The condition must be affecting the patient's current health status.
- The patient must be a child or adult (not a newborn at the time of coding).

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `220`

> **Actual Text**
>
> In ICD-10-CM, 'completed' weeks of gestation refers to full weeks. For example, if the provider documents gestation at 39 weeks and 6 days, the code for 39 weeks of gestation should be assigned, as the patient has not yet reached 40 completed weeks.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Gestation Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code based on full completed weeks of gestation only.
- Do not round up to the next week if days are present but the next week is not completed.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider must document specific weeks and days of gestation.
- Code assignment is limited to the number of full weeks elapsed.

### 143. ICD-10-CM Z Code Reporting Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding Z code assignment guidelines with identical behavioral logic and specific code examples.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `49`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Z codes (other reasons for healthcare encounters) may be assigned as appropriate to further explain the reasons for presenting for healthcare services, including transfers between healthcare facilities, or provide additional information relevant to a patient encounter. The ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting identify which codes maybe assigned as principal or first-listed diagnosis only, secondary diagnosis only, or principal/first-listed or secondary (depending on the circumstances). Possible applicable Z codes include:
>
> - Z59.0-, Homelessness
> - Z59.1, Inadequate housing
> - Z59.5, Extreme poverty
> - Z75.1, Person awaiting admission to adequate facility elsewhere
> - Z75.3, Unavailability and inaccessibility of health-care facilities
> - Z75.4, Unavailability and inaccessibility of other helping agencies
> - Z76.2, Encounter for health supervision and care of other healthy infant and child
> - Z99.12, Encounter for respirator [ventilator] dependence during power failure
>
> The external cause of morbidity codes and the Z codes listed above are not an all-inclusive list. Other codes may be applicable to the encounter based upon the documentation. Assign as many codes as necessary to fully explain each healthcare encounter. Since patient history information may be very limited, use any available documentation to assign the appropriate external cause of morbidity and Z codes.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Z Code and External Cause Reporting Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Z codes may be assigned as appropriate to further explain reasons for healthcare encounters, including transfers between facilities.
- Z codes and external cause codes are used to provide additional information relevant to a patient encounter.
- Assign as many codes as necessary to fully explain each healthcare encounter.
- Use any available documentation to assign appropriate external cause of morbidity and Z codes when patient history is limited.
- Z codes and external cause codes are not an all-inclusive list; other codes may be applicable based on documentation.
- Z codes may be assigned as principal/first-listed diagnosis only, secondary diagnosis only, or both depending on circumstances.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Codes must be assigned based on the specific circumstances to determine if they are principal/first-listed or secondary.
- Documentation must support the assignment of specific Z codes (e.g., homelessness, inadequate housing, ventilator dependence).
- Specific Z codes (e.g., Z59.0-, Z59.1, Z59.5, Z75.1, Z75.3, Z75.4, Z76.2, Z99.12) must be assigned based on specific circumstances.
- Assignment of codes must be supported by available documentation, even if patient history is limited.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `481`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Z codes (other reasons for healthcare encounters) may be assigned as appropriate to further explain the reasons for presenting for healthcare services, including transfers between healthcare facilities, or provide additional information relevant to a patient encounter. The ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting identify which codes maybe assigned as principal or first-listed diagnosis only, secondary diagnosis only, or principal/first-listed or secondary (depending on the circumstances). Possible applicable Z codes include:
>
> - Z59.0-, Homelessness
> - Z59.1, Inadequate housing
> - Z59.5, Extreme poverty
> - Z75.1, Person awaiting admission to adequate facility elsewhere
> - Z75.3, Unavailability and inaccessibility of health-care facilities
> - Z75.4, Unavailability and inaccessibility of other helping agencies
> - Z76.2, Encounter for health supervision and care of other healthy infant and child
> - Z99.12, Encounter for respirator [ventilator] dependence during power failure
>

Assign as many codes as necessary to fully explain each healthcare encounter. Since patient history information may be very limited, use any available documentation to assign the appropriate external cause of morbidity and Z codes.

- Use any available documentation to assign appropriate external cause of morbidity and Z codes when patient history is limited.
- Z codes may be assigned to explain reasons for presenting for healthcare services, including transfers between healthcare facilities.
- The guidelines explicitly list specific Z codes (e.g., Z59.0, Z59.1, Z59.5, Z75.1, Z75.3, Z75.4, Z76.2, Z99.12) as examples of applicable codes.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Codes must be assigned based on the specific circumstances to determine if they are principal/first-listed or secondary.
- Documentation must support the assignment of the specific Z code or external cause code.
- Codes must be assigned as principal/first-listed diagnosis only, secondary diagnosis only, or principal/first-listed or secondary depending on the specific circumstances.

### 146. Unqualified Visual Loss Coding Logic
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for unqualified visual loss using the exact same criteria and code assignments.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `163`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If 'blindness' or 'low vision' of both eyes is documented but the visual impairment category is not documented, assign code H54.3, Unqualified visual loss, both eyes. If 'blindness' or 'low vision' in one eye is documented but the visual impairment category is not documented, assign a code from H54.6-, Unqualified visual loss, one eye. If 'blindness' or 'visual loss' is documented without any information about whether one or both eyes are affected, assign code H54.7, Unspecified visual loss.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Unqualified Visual Loss Coding Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code H54.3 if blindness or low vision of both eyes is documented without a specific visual impairment category.
- Assign a code from H54.6- if blindness or low vision in one eye is documented without a specific visual impairment category.
- Assign code H54.7 if visual loss is documented without any information regarding whether one or both eyes are affected.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must specify 'blindness' or 'low vision' for H54.3 or H54.6- assignments.
- Documentation must lack a specific visual impairment category for H54.3 or H54.6- assignments.
- Documentation must lack information about the number of eyes affected for H54.7 assignment.

### 147. Fracture Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same Fracture Coding Guidelines feature with identical logic and requirements.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `287_288_290`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The principles of multiple coding of injuries should be followed in coding fractures. Fractures of specified sites are coded individually by site in accordance with both the provisions within categories S02, S12, S22, S32, S42, S49, S52, S59, S62, S72, S79, S82, S89, S92 and the level of detail furnished by medical record content.
>
> A fracture not indicated as open or closed should be coded to closed. A fracture not indicated whether displaced or not displaced should be coded to displaced.
>
> More specific guidelines are as follows:
>
> Traumatic fractures are coded using the appropriate 7 th character for initial encounter (A, B, C) for each encounter where the patient is receiving active treatment for the fracture. The appropriate 7 th character for initial encounter should also be assigned for a patient who delayed seeking treatment for the fracture or nonunion.
>
> Fractures are coded using the appropriate 7 th character for subsequent care for encounters after the patient has completed active treatment of the fracture and is receiving routine care for the fracture during the healing or recovery phase.
>
> Care for complications of surgical treatment for fracture repairs during the healing or recovery phase should be coded with the appropriate complication codes.
>
> Care of complications of fractures, such as malunion and nonunion, should be reported with the appropriate 7 th character for subsequent care with nonunion (K, M, N,) or subsequent care with malunion (P, Q, R).
>
> Malunion/nonunion: The appropriate 7 th character for initial encounter should also be assigned for a patient who delayed seeking treatment for the fracture or nonunion.
>
> The open fracture designations in the assignment of the 7th character for fractures of the forearm, femur and lower leg, including ankle are based on the Gustilo open fracture classification. When the Gustilo classification type is not specified for an open fracture, the 7th character for open fracture type I or II should be assigned (B, E, H, M, Q).
>
> A code from category M80, not a traumatic fracture code, should be used for any patient with known osteoporosis who suffers a fracture, even if the patient had a minor fall or trauma, if that fall or trauma would not usually break a normal, healthy bone.
>
> See Section I.C.13. Osteoporosis.
>
> The aftercare Z codes should not be used for aftercare for traumatic fractures. For aftercare of a traumatic fracture, assign the acute fracture code with the appropriate 7 th character.
>
> For physeal fractures, assign only the code identifying the type of physeal fracture. Do not assign a separate code to identify the specific bone that is fractured.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Fracture Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Fractures of specified sites are coded individually by site in accordance with category provisions and medical record detail.
- Fractures not indicated as open or closed should be coded to closed.
- Fractures not indicated whether displaced or not displaced should be coded to displaced.
- Traumatic fractures are coded using the appropriate 7th character for initial encounter (A, B, C) for each encounter where the patient is receiving active treatment.
- Care for complications of surgical treatment for fracture repairs during the healing or recovery phase should be coded with the appropriate complication codes.
- Fractures of specified sites are coded individually by site in accordance with both the provisions within categories and the level of detail furnished by medical record content.
- Fractures are coded using the appropriate 7th character for subsequent care for encounters after the patient has completed active treatment of the fracture and is receiving routine care for the fracture during the healing or recovery phase.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Fractures must be coded individually by site in accordance with provisions within categories S02, S12, S22, S32, S42, S49, S52, S59, S62, S72, S79, S82, S89, S92.
- A fracture not indicated as open or closed should be coded to closed.
- A fracture not indicated whether displaced or not displaced should be coded to displaced.
- Traumatic fractures must use the appropriate 7th character for initial encounter (A, B, C) for each encounter where the patient is receiving active treatment.
- Aftercare Z codes should not be used for aftercare for traumatic fractures; instead, assign the acute fracture code with the appropriate 7th character.
- A code from category M80, not a traumatic fracture code, should be used for any patient with known osteoporosis who suffers a fracture, even if the patient had a minor fall or trauma, if that fall or trauma would not usually break a normal, healthy bone.

>

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Fracture Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Fractures of specified sites are coded individually by site in accordance with category provisions and medical record detail.
- Fractures not indicated as open or closed should be coded to closed.
- Fractures not indicated whether displaced or not displaced should be coded to displaced.
- Traumatic fractures are coded using the appropriate 7th character for initial encounter (A, B, C) for each encounter where the patient is receiving active treatment.
- Care for complications of surgical treatment for fracture repairs during the healing or recovery phase should be coded with the appropriate complication codes.
- Fractures of specified sites are coded individually by site in accordance with both the provisions within categories and the level of detail furnished by medical record content.
- Fractures are coded using the appropriate 7th character for subsequent care for encounters after the patient has completed active treatment of the fracture and is receiving routine care for the fracture during the healing or recovery phase.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Fractures must be coded individually by site in accordance with provisions within categories S02, S12, S22, S32, S42, S49, S52, S59, S62, S72, S79, S82, S89, S92.
- A fracture not indicated as open or closed should be coded to closed.
- A fracture not indicated whether displaced or not displaced should be coded to displaced.
- Traumatic fractures must use the appropriate 7th character for initial encounter (A, B, C) for each encounter where the patient is receiving active treatment.
- Aftercare Z codes should not be used for aftercare for traumatic fractures; instead, assign the acute fracture code with the appropriate 7th character.
- A code from category M80 must be used instead of a traumatic fracture code for patients with known osteoporosis who suffer a fracture from minor trauma that would not typically break a healthy bone.
- For physeal fractures, only the code identifying the type of physeal fracture is required; a separate code for the specific bone is prohibited.

### 148. ICD-10-CM CKD Classification Logic
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical ICD-10-CM CKD classification logic, including stage definitions and ESRD precedence rules.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `22_211`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The word 'and' should be interpreted to mean either 'and' or 'or' when it appears in a title.
>
> For example, cases of 'tuberculosis of bones', 'tuberculosis of joints' and 'tuberculosis of bones and joints' are classified to subcategory A18.0, Tuberculosis of bones and joints.
>
> The ICD-10-CM classifies CKD based on severity. The severity of CKD is designated by stages 1-5. Stage 2, code N18.2, equates to mild CKD; stage 3, codes N18.30-N18.32, equate to moderate CKD; and stage 4, code N18.4, equates to severe CKD. Code N18.6, End stage renal disease (ESRD), is assigned when the provider has documented endstage renal disease (ESRD).
>
> If both a stage of CKD and ESRD are documented, assign code N18.6 only.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM CKD Classification Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- The word 'and' in titles is interpreted to mean either 'and' or 'or' for classification purposes.
- CKD is classified based on severity stages 1-5, with specific codes for mild (Stage 2), moderate (Stage 3), and severe (Stage 4) CKD.
- Code N18.6 (End stage renal disease) is assigned when ESRD is documented.
- If both a stage of CKD and ESRD are documented, assign code N18.6 only.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider must document the specific stage of CKD (1-5) to assign the corresponding N18.2, N18.3x, or N18.4 code.
- Provider must explicitly document end-stage renal disease (ESRD) to assign code N18.6.
- When both CKD stage and ESRD are present, N18.6 takes precedence over the stage-specific code.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `454_640`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The word 'and' should be interpreted to mean either 'and' or 'or' when it appears in a title.
>

Code N18.6, End stage renal disease (ESRD), is assigned when the provider has documented endstage renal disease (ESRD).

### 149. ICD-10-CM Myocardial Infarction Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles describe the same ICD-10-CM Myocardial Infarction Coding Guidelines feature, specifically focusing on the assignment rules for Type 1, Type 2, and other AMI types.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `183_184`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A code from category I22, Subsequent ST elevation (STEMI) and non -ST elevation (NSTEMI) myocardial infarction, is to be used when a patient who has suffered a type 1 or unspecified AMI has a new AMI within the 4 week time frame of the initial AMI. A code from category I22 must be used in conjunction with a code from category I21. The sequencing of the I22 and I21 codes depends on the circumstances of the encounter.
>
> Do not assign code I22 for subsequent myocardial infarctions other than type 1 or unspecified. For subsequent type 2 AMI assign only code I21.A1. For subsequent type 4 or type 5 AMI, assign only code I21.A9.
>
> If a subsequent myocardial infarction of one type occurs within 4 weeks of a myocardial infarction of a different type, assign the appropriate codes from category I21 to identify each type. Do not assign a code from I22. Codes from category I22 should only be assigned if both the initial and subsequent myocardial infarctions are type 1 or unspecified.
>
> The ICD-10-CM provides codes for different types of myocardial infarction. Type 1 myocardial infarctions are assigned to codes I21.0I21.4.
>
> Type 2 myocardial infarction ( myocardial infarction due to demand ischemia or secondary to ischemic imbalance ) is assigned to code I21.A1, Myocardial infarction type 2 with the underlying cause coded first , if applicable . Do not assign code I24.8 9 , Other forms of acute ischemic heart disease , for the demand ischemia. If a type 2 AMI is described as NSTEMI or STEMI, only assign code I21.A1. Codes I21.01-I21.4 should only be assigned for type 1 AMIs.
>
> Acute myocardial infarctions type 3, 4a, 4b, 4c and 5 are assigned to code I21.A9, Other myocardial infarction type.
>
> The "Code also" and "Code first" notes should be followed related to complications, and for coding of postprocedural myocardial infarctions during or following cardiac surgery.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Myocardial Infarction Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Use category I22 for subsequent type 1 or unspecified AMI within 4 weeks of an initial AMI, sequenced with I21 based on encounter circumstances.
- Assign only code I21.A1 for subsequent type 2 AMI and only code I21.A9 for subsequent type 4 or 5 AMI, avoiding I22 codes.
- Assign appropriate I21 codes for subsequent myocardial infarctions of different types occurring within 4 weeks of each other, rather than using I22.
- Assign codes I21.0-I21.4 exclusively for type 1 AMIs and I21.A9 for types 3, 4a, 4b, 4c, and 5.
- Follow 'Code also' and 'Code first' notes for complications and postprocedural myocardial infarctions during or following cardiac surgery.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Subsequent AMI must be type 1 or unspecified to use category I22.
- Subsequent type 2 AMI must be coded as I21.A1 with underlying cause coded first if applicable.
- Subsequent type 4 or 5 AMI must be coded as I21.A9.
- Initial and subsequent AMIs must both be type 1 or unspecified to justify the use of category I22.
- Codes I21.0-I21.4 should only be assigned for type 1 AMIs.
- Code I21.A1 must be sequenced with the underlying cause coded first if applicable.
- Code I24.8 must not be assigned for demand ischemia.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `614`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The ICD-10-CM provides codes for different types of myocardial infarction. Type 1 myocardial infarctions are assigned to codes I21.0I21.4.
>
> Type 2 myocardial infarction ( myocardial infarction due to demand ischemia or secondary to ischemic imbalance ) is assigned to code I21.A1, Myocardial infarction type 2 with the underlying cause coded first , if applicable. Do not assign code I24.89, Other forms of acute ischemic heart disease , for the demand ischemia. If a type 2 AMI is described as NSTEMI or STEMI, only assign code I21.A1. Codes I21.01-I21.4 should only be assigned for type 1 AMIs.
>
> Acute myocardial infarctions type 3, 4a, 4b, 4c and 5 are assigned to code I21.A9, Other myocardial infarction type.
>
> The "Code also" and "Code first" notes should be followed related to complications, and for coding of postprocedural myocardial infarctions during or following cardiac surgery.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Myocardial Infarction Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Type 1 myocardial infarctions are assigned to codes I21.0-I21.4.
- Type 2 myocardial infarction is assigned to code I21.A1 with the underlying cause coded first if applicable.
- Code I24.89 must not be assigned for demand ischemia in Type 2 AMI.
- If a Type 2 AMI is described as NSTEMI or STEMI, only assign code I21.A1.
- Acute myocardial infarctions types 3, 4a, 4b, 4c, and 5 are assigned to code I21.A9.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- For Type 2 MI, the underlying cause must be coded first if applicable.
- Codes I21.0-I21.4 should only be assigned for Type 1 AMIs.
- The 'Code also' and 'Code first' notes must be followed for complications and postprocedural MI.
- Type 2 AMI documentation must specify if it is NSTEMI or STEMI to ensure correct coding to I21.A1.
- Postprocedural myocardial infarctions during or following cardiac surgery require adherence to specific coding notes.

The older version explicitly restricted the use of category I22 to subsequent Type 1 or unspecified AMIs, while the newer version clarifies that Type 2 AMIs must always be coded as I21.A1 regardless of whether they are described as NSTEMI or STEMI. The newer version also adds a specific prohibition against using code I24.89 for demand ischemia in Type 2 AMIs, whereas the older version only mentioned avoiding I24.8 generally. Both versions maintain the rule that subsequent Type 4 or 5 AMIs are coded as I21.A9, but the newer text provides more granular instruction on the clinical documentation required for Type 2 classification.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Subsequent type 2 AMI must be coded as I21.A1 with underlying cause coded first if applicable. -> October 2025 (New Version): If a Type 2 AMI is described as NSTEMI or STEMI, only assign code I21.A1.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Code I24.8 must not be assigned for demand ischemia. -> October 2025 (New Version): Do not assign code I24.89, Other forms of acute ischemic heart disease, for the demand ischemia.

### 150. ICD-10-CM Code Specificity Requirements
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding ICD-10-CM code specificity and the prohibition of using categories or subcategories for reporting.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `29`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Diagnosis codes are to be used and reported at their highest number of characters available and to the highest level of specificity documented in the medical record.
>
> ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes are composed of codes with 3, 4, 5, 6 or 7 characters. Codes with three characters are included in ICD-10-CM as the heading of a category of codes that may be further subdivided by the use of fourth and/or fifth characters and/or sixth characters, which provide greater detail.
>
> A three-character code is to be used only if it is not further subdivided. A code is invalid if it has not been coded to the full number of characters required for that code, including the 7 th character, if applicable.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Code Specificity Requirements

**Key Behaviors:**
- Diagnosis codes must be used and reported at their highest number of characters available.
- Codes must be assigned to the highest level of specificity documented in the medical record.
- A three-character code is to be used only if it is not further subdivided by additional characters.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Codes must include the full number of characters required for that specific code.
- The 7th character must be included if applicable for the specific code.
- Codes with 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7 characters must be utilized to provide greater detail where available.

#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `442_461`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For reporting purposes only codes are permissible, not categories or subcategories, and any applicable 7 th character is required.
>
> Diagnosis codes are to be used and reported at their highest number of characters available and to the highest level of specificity documented in the medical record.
>
> ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes are composed of codes with 3, 4, 5, 6 or 7 characters. Codes with three characters are included in ICD-10-CM as the heading of a category of codes that may be further subdivided by the use of fourth and/or fifth characters and/or sixth characters, which provide greater detail.
>
> A three-character code is to be used only if it is not further subdivided. A code is invalid if it has not been coded to the full number of characters required for that code, including the 7 th character, if applicable.

**Extracted Profile:**

- Only codes are permissible for reporting, not categories or subcategories.
- Diagnosis codes must be used and reported at their highest number of characters available.

- A three-character code is to be used only if it is not further subdivided.
- A code is invalid if it has not been coded to the full number of characters required, including the 7th character if applicable.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Any applicable 7th character is required for the code to be valid.
- Codes must be composed of 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7 characters.
- Fourth, fifth, and sixth characters must be used to provide greater detail if the category allows subdivision.
- The code assigned must match the highest level of specificity documented in the medical record.
- Reporting must use specific codes rather than categories or subcategories.

---

The older version established the general rule that codes must be used at the highest level of specificity and that three-character codes are invalid if subdivided. The newer version retains these core rules but adds a critical clarification that only specific codes are permissible for reporting, explicitly excluding categories and subcategories from being used as standalone codes.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Mandates use of codes at highest specificity and invalidates codes not fully expanded -> October 2025 (New Version): Adds explicit prohibition on using categories or subcategories for reporting, requiring only specific codes.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): States a three-character code is invalid if not subdivided -> October 2025 (New Version): Reiterates the invalidity of three-character codes if subdivided while reinforcing the requirement for fourth, fifth, and sixth characters if the category allows subdivision.

*Traceability: Chunk `29` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `442_461` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 151. Type 1 STEMI/NSTEMI Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Type 1 STEMI/NSTEMI coding guidelines with verbatim matching behaviors and requirements.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `180`

> **Actual Text**
>
> - 1) Type 1 ST elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) and nonST elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI)
>
> The ICD-10-CM codes for type 1 acute myocardial infarction (AMI) identify the site, such as anterolateral wall or true posterior wall. Subcategories I21.0-I21.2 and code I21.3 are used for type 1 ST elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI). Code I21.4, Non-ST elevation (NSTEMI) myocardial infarction, is used for type 1 non-ST elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI) and nontransmural MIs.
>
> If a type 1 NSTEMI evolves to STEMI, assign the STEMI code. If a type 1 STEMI converts to NSTEMI due to thrombolytic therapy, it is still coded as STEMI.
>
> For encounters occurring while the myocardial infarction is equal to, or less than, four weeks old, including transfers to another acute setting or a postacute setting, and the myocardial infarction meets the definition for 'other diagnoses' (see Section III, Reporting Additional Diagnoses), codes from category I21 may continue to be reported. For encounters after the 4-week time frame and the patient is still receiving care related to the myocardial infarction, the appropriate aftercare code should be assigned, rather than a code from category I21. For old or healed myocardial infarctions not requiring further care, code I25.2, Old myocardial infarction, may be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Type 1 STEMI/NSTEMI Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Subcategories I21.0-I21.2 and I21.3 are used for type 1 ST elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI).
- Code I21.4 is used for type 1 non-ST elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI) and nontransmural MIs.
- If a type 1 NSTEMI evolves to STEMI, assign the STEMI code.
- If a type 1 STEMI converts to NSTEMI due to thrombolytic therapy, it is still coded as STEMI.
- Codes from category I21 are reported for encounters within four weeks of the myocardial infarction.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Codes must identify the site of the infarction (e.g., anterolateral wall or true posterior wall).
- The myocardial infarction must meet the definition for 'other diagnoses' to be reported for encounters occurring while equal to or less than four weeks old.
- Aftercare codes should be assigned rather than category I21 codes for encounters after the 4-week time frame if the patient is still receiving care related to the myocardial infarction.
- Code I25.2 may be assigned for old or healed myocardial infarctions not requiring further care.
- The encounter timing relative to the 4-week mark determines whether category I21 or aftercare codes are appropriate.
- Codes from category I21 may only be reported if the myocardial infarction meets the definition for 'other diagnoses'.

---

The 4-week reporting window and the requirement for the 'other diagnoses' definition are also unchanged between the two texts.

### 152. ICD-10 Excludes1 Note Logic
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical ICD-10 Excludes1 Note Logic, including the specific F45.8 and G47.63 example.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `19`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A type 1 Excludes note is a pure excludes note. It means 'NOT CODED HERE!' An Excludes1 note indicates that the code excluded should never be used at the same time as the code above the Excludes1 note. An Excludes1 is used when two conditions cannot occur together, such as a congenital form versus an acquired form of the same condition.
>
> An exception to the Excludes1 definition is the circumstance when the two conditions are unrelated to each other. If it is not clear whether the two conditions involving an Excludes1 note are related or not, query the provider. For example, code F45.8, Other somatoform disorders, has an Excludes1 note for "sleep related teeth grinding (G47.63)," because "teeth grinding" is an inclusion term under F45.8. Only one of these two codes should be assigned for teeth grinding. However psychogenic dysmenorrhea is also an inclusion term under F45.8, and a patient could have both this condition and sleep related teeth grinding. In this case, the two conditions are clearly unrelated to each other, and so it would be appropriate to report F45.8 and G47.63 together.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Excludes1 Note Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- An Excludes1 note indicates that the excluded code should never be used at the same time as the code above the note.
- Excludes1 is used when two conditions cannot occur together, such as congenital versus acquired forms.
- If conditions involving an Excludes1 note are unrelated, both codes may be assigned together.
- If it is unclear whether two conditions involving an Excludes1 note are related, the provider must be queried.
- An Excludes1 note is defined as a 'pure excludes note' meaning 'NOT CODED HERE!'
- The strict exclusion applies specifically when two conditions represent mutually exclusive states of the same disease, such as congenital versus acquired forms.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Verify if the two conditions are related or unrelated before assigning codes.
- Query the provider if the relationship between conditions is unclear.
- Ensure congenital and acquired forms of the same condition are not coded together.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `451`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A type 1 Excludes note is a pure excludes note. It means 'NOT CODED HERE!' An Excludes1 note indicates that the code excluded should never be used at the same time as the code above the Excludes1 note. An Excludes1 is used when two conditions cannot occur together, such as a congenital form versus an acquired form of the same condition.
>
> An exception to the Excludes1 definition is the circumstance when the two conditions are unrelated to each other. If it is not clear whether the two conditions involving an Excludes1 note are related or not, query the provider. For example, code F45.8, Other somatoform disorders, has an Excludes1 note for "sleep related teeth grinding (G47.63)," because "teeth grinding" is an inclusion term under F45.8. Only one of these two codes should be assigned for teeth grinding. However psychogenic dysmenorrhea is also an inclusion term under F45.8, and a patient could have both this condition and sleep related teeth grinding. In this case, the two conditions are clearly unrelated to each other, and so it would be appropriate to report F45.8 and G47.63 together.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Excludes1 Note Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- An Excludes1 note indicates that the excluded code should never be used at the same time as the code above the note.
- Excludes1 is used when two conditions cannot occur together, such as congenital versus acquired forms.
- If conditions involving an Excludes1 note are unrelated, both codes may be assigned together.
- If it is unclear whether two conditions involving an Excludes1 note are related, the provider must be queried.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Verify if the two conditions are related or unrelated before assigning codes.
- Query the provider if the relationship between conditions is unclear.
- Ensure congenital and acquired forms of the same condition are not coded together.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions contain identical text regarding the definition of Excludes1 notes as 'pure excludes' and the rule that excluded codes cannot be used simultaneously with the primary code. Both versions explicitly detail the exception for unrelated conditions and provide the exact same clinical example involving code F45.8 and G47.63 to illustrate when unrelated conditions can be coded together. The logic for querying providers when the relationship between conditions is unclear remains unchanged between the two versions.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Identical logic and example text -> October 2025 (New Version): Identical logic and example text

### 154. ICD-10 Code Inclusion and Index Assignment
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding ICD-10 code inclusion terms and index assignment logic.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `17`

> **Actual Text**
>
> List of terms is included under some codes. These terms are the conditions for which that code is to be used. The terms may be synonyms of the code title, or, in the case of 'other specified' codes, the terms are a list of the various conditions assigned to that code. The inclusion terms are not necessarily exhaustive. Additional terms found only in the Alphabetic Index may also be assigned to a code.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Code Inclusion and Index Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- List of terms included under some codes are the conditions for which that code is to be used.
- Inclusion terms may be synonyms of the code title.
- In the case of 'other specified' codes, terms are a list of various conditions assigned to that code.
- Inclusion terms are not necessarily exhaustive.
- Additional terms found only in the Alphabetic Index may also be assigned to a code.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Terms must be included under a code to be used for that condition.
- For 'other specified' codes, terms must match the list of conditions assigned to that code.
- Assignment is permitted for additional terms found only in the Alphabetic Index.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `449`

> **Actual Text**
>
> List of terms is included under some codes. These terms are the conditions for which that code is to be used. The terms may be synonyms of the code title, or, in the case of 'other specified' codes, the terms are a list of the various conditions assigned to that code. The inclusion terms are not necessarily exhaustive. Additional terms found only in the Alphabetic Index may also be assigned to a code.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Code Inclusion and Index Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- List of terms included under some codes serve as conditions for which that code is to be used.
- Inclusion terms may be synonyms of the code title.
- For 'other specified' codes, inclusion terms list the various conditions assigned to that code.
- Inclusion terms are not necessarily exhaustive.
- Additional terms found only in the Alphabetic Index may also be assigned to a code.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Terms must be explicitly listed under a code to be considered inclusion terms.
- Codes may be used for synonyms of the code title.
- 'Other specified' codes must utilize the listed terms for assigned conditions.
- Assignment is permitted for terms found exclusively in the Alphabetic Index.
- Exhaustiveness of inclusion terms is not required for valid assignment.

### 155. ICD-10 Chapter 19 Character Requirements
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding ICD-10 Chapter 19 7th character requirements with a specific, meaningful difference in sequencing rules.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `282`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Most categories in chapter 19 have a 7 th character requirement for each applicable code. Most categories in this chapter have three 7 th character values (with the exception of fractures): A, initial encounter, D, subsequent encounter and S, sequela. Categories for traumatic fractures have additional 7 th character values. While the patient may be seen by a new or different provider over the course of treatment for an injury, assignment of the 7 th character is based on whether the patient is undergoing active treatment and not whether the provider is seeing the patient for the first time.
>
> For complication codes, active treatment refers to treatment for the condition described by the code, even though it may be related to an earlier precipitating problem. For example, code T84.50XA, Infection and inflammatory reaction due to unspecified internal joint prosthesis, initial encounter, is used when active treatment is provided for the infection, even though the condition relates to the prosthetic device, implant or graft that was placed at a previous encounter.
>
> 7 th character 'A', initial encounter is used for each encounter where the patient is receiving active treatment for the condition.
>
> 7 th character 'D' subsequent encounter is used for encounters after the patient has completed active treatment of the condition and is receiving routine care for the condition during the healing or recovery phase.
>
> The aftercare Z codes should not be used for aftercare for conditions such as injuries or poisonings, where 7 th characters are provided to identify subsequent care. For example, for aftercare of an injury, assign the acute injury code with the 7 th character 'D' (subsequent encounter).
>
> 7 th character 'S', sequela, is for use for complications or conditions that arise as a direct result of a condition, such as scar formation after a burn. The scars are sequelae of the burn. When using 7 th character 'S', it is necessary to use both the injury code that precipitated the sequela and the code for the sequela itself. The 'S' is added only to the injury code, not the sequela code. The 7 th character 'S' identifies the injury responsible for the sequela. The specific type of sequela (e.g. scar) is sequenced first, followed by the injury code.
>
> See Section I.B.10. Sequelae, (Late Effects)

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Chapter 19 7th Character Requirements

**Key Behaviors:**
- Most categories require a 7th character with values A (initial), D (subsequent), or S (sequela).
- Assignment of the 7th character is based on whether the patient is undergoing active treatment, not whether the provider is seeing the patient for the first time.
- 7th character 'A' is used when the patient is receiving active treatment for the condition.
- 7th character 'D' is used for routine care during the healing or recovery phase after active treatment is completed.
- 7th character 'S' identifies the injury responsible for a sequela and is added only to the injury code, not the sequela code.
- For complication codes, active treatment refers to treatment for the condition described by the code, even if related to an earlier precipitating problem.
- When using 7th character 'S', the specific type of sequela (e.g., scar) is sequenced first, followed by the injury code.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Active treatment must be provided for the condition described by the code to assign 7th character 'A'.
- Routine care during the healing or recovery phase is required to assign 7th character 'D'.
- Both the injury code and the sequela code must be used when assigning 7th character 'S'.
- The 7th character 'S' must be added only to the injury code, not the sequela code.
- Aftercare Z codes should not be used for injuries or poisonings where 7th characters are provided.
- When using 7th character 'S', it is necessary to use both the injury code that precipitated the sequela and the code for the sequela itself.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `709`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Most categories in chapter 19 have a 7 th character requirement for each applicable code. Most categories in this chapter have three 7 th character values (with the exception of fractures): A, initial encounter, D, subsequent encounter and S, sequela. Categories for traumatic fractures have additional 7 th character values. While the patient may be seen by a new or different provider over the course of treatment for an injury, assignment of the 7 th character is based on whether the patient is undergoing active treatment and not whether the provider is seeing the patient for the first time.
>
> For complication codes, active treatment refers to treatment for the condition described by the code, even though it may be related to an earlier precipitating problem. For example, code T84.50XA, Infection and inflammatory reaction due to unspecified internal joint prosthesis, initial encounter, is used when active treatment is provided for the infection, even though the condition relates to the prosthetic device, implant or graft that was placed at a previous encounter.
>
> 7 th character 'A', initial encounter is used for each encounter where the patient is receiving active treatment for the condition.
>
> 7 th character 'D' subsequent encounter is used for encounters after the patient has completed active treatment of the condition and is receiving routine care for the condition during the healing or recovery phase.
>
> The aftercare Z codes should not be used for aftercare for conditions such as injuries or poisonings, where 7 th characters are provided to identify subsequent care. For example, for aftercare of an injury, assign the acute injury code with the 7 th character 'D' (subsequent encounter).
>
> 7 th character 'S', sequela, is for use for complications or conditions that arise as a direct result of a condition, such as scar formation after a burn. The scars are sequelae of the burn. When using 7 th character 'S', it is necessary to use both the injury code that precipitated the sequela and the code for the sequela itself. The 'S' is added only to the injury code, not the sequela code. The 7 th character 'S' identifies the injury responsible for the sequela. The specific type of sequela (e.g. scar) is sequenced first, followed by the injury code.
>
> See Section I.B.10. Sequelae, (Late Effects)

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Chapter 19 7th Character Requirements

**Key Behaviors:**
- Most categories require a 7th character with values A (initial), D (subsequent), or S (sequela).
- Assignment of the 7th character is based on whether the patient is undergoing active treatment, not whether the provider is seeing the patient for the first time.
- 7th character 'A' is used when the patient is receiving active treatment for the condition.
- 7th character 'D' is used for routine care during the healing or recovery phase after active treatment is completed.
- 7th character 'S' identifies the injury responsible for a sequela and is added only to the injury code, not the sequela code.
- For complication codes, active treatment refers to treatment for the condition described by the code, even if related to an earlier precipitating problem.
- When using 7th character 'S', the specific type of sequela is sequenced first, followed by the injury code.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Active treatment must be provided for the condition described by the code to assign 7th character 'A'.
- Routine care during the healing or recovery phase is required to assign 7th character 'D'.
- Both the injury code and the sequela code must be used when assigning 7th character 'S'.
- The 7th character 'S' must be added only to the injury code, not the sequela code.
- Aftercare Z codes should not be used for aftercare of injuries or poisonings where 7th characters are provided.
- For traumatic fractures, additional 7th character values beyond A, D, and S are required.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Rule Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version explicitly stated that the 7th character 'S' is added only to the injury code, not the sequela code, while the newer version clarifies the sequencing order by stating the specific type of sequela is sequenced first, followed by the injury code. This represents a shift from a restriction on code assignment to a directive on code sequencing logic. Both versions maintain the core rule that active treatment status, rather than provider history, determines the character assignment.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): 7th character 'S' identifies the injury responsible for a sequela and is added only to the injury code, not the sequela code. -> October 2025 (New Version): 7th character 'S' identifies the injury responsible for a sequela and is added only to the injury code, not the sequela code.

*Traceability: Chunk `282` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `709` (October 2025 (New Version))*

These Alphabetic Index entries represent specific disease entities for which no specific code exists, so the term is included within an 'other' code.
