### 158. ICD-10-CM Combination Code Sequencing
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical ICD-10-CM combination code sequencing rules for atherosclerotic heart disease and angina pectoris with verbatim matching text.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `177`

> **Actual Text**
>
> ICD-10-CM has combination codes for atherosclerotic heart disease with angina pectoris. The subcategories for these codes are I25.11, Atherosclerotic heart disease of native coronary artery with angina pectoris and I25.7, Atherosclerosis of coronary artery bypass graft(s) and coronary artery of transplanted heart with angina pectoris.
>
> When using one of these combination codes it is not necessary to use an additional code for angina pectoris. A causal relationship can be assumed in a patient with both atherosclerosis and angina pectoris, unless the documentation indicates the angina is due to something other than the atherosclerosis.
>
> If a patient with coronary artery disease is admitted due to an acute myocardial infarction (AMI), the AMI should be sequenced before the coronary artery disease.
>
> See Section I.C.9. Acute myocardial infarction (AMI)

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Combination Code Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Combination codes for atherosclerotic heart disease with angina pectoris include both conditions, making an additional code for angina unnecessary.
- A causal relationship between atherosclerosis and angina pectoris is assumed unless documentation specifies a different cause for the angina.
- In cases of acute myocardial infarction (AMI) admission, the AMI must be sequenced before the underlying coronary artery disease.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must indicate that angina is not due to a cause other than atherosclerosis to assume the causal relationship.
- Admission status must be due to acute myocardial infarction (AMI) to trigger the sequencing rule placing AMI first.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `605`

> **Actual Text**
>

A causal relationship can be assumed in a patient with both atherosclerosis and angina pectoris, unless the documentation indicates the angina is due to something other than the atherosclerosis.
>

### 159. ICD-10-CM Coding Syntax Definitions
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature regarding ICD-10-CM syntax definitions with nearly identical behavioral descriptions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `13`

> **Actual Text**
>
> - [ ] Brackets are used in the Tabular List to enclose synonyms, alternative wording or explanatory phrases. Brackets are used in the Alphabetic Index to identify manifestation codes.
> - ( ) Parentheses are used in both the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List to enclose supplementary words that may be present or absent in the statement of a disease or procedure without affecting the code number to which it is assigned. The terms within the parentheses are referred to as nonessential modifiers. The nonessential modifiers in the Alphabetic Index to Diseases apply to subterms following a main term except when a nonessential modifier and a subentry are mutually exclusive, the subentry takes precedence. For example, in the ICD-10-CM Alphabetic Index under the main term Enteritis, 'acute' is a nonessential modifier and 'chronic' is a subentry. In this case, the nonessential modifier 'acute' does not apply to the subentry 'chronic'.
>
> - : Colons are used in the Tabular List after an incomplete term which needs one or more of the modifiers following the colon to make it assignable to a given category.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Coding Syntax Definitions

**Key Behaviors:**
- Brackets enclose synonyms, alternative wording, or explanatory phrases in the Tabular List and identify manifestation codes in the Alphabetic Index.
- Parentheses enclose nonessential modifiers that may be present or absent without affecting the code number.
- Nonessential modifiers in the Alphabetic Index apply to subterms following a main term unless mutually exclusive with a subentry, in which case the subentry takes precedence.
- Colons in the Tabular List follow incomplete terms requiring additional modifiers to make them assignable to a category.
- Subentries take precedence over nonessential modifiers when the two are mutually exclusive.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Brackets must be used to identify manifestation codes in the Alphabetic Index.
- Nonessential modifiers within parentheses do not affect the code number assignment.
- Subentries override nonessential modifiers when they are mutually exclusive.
- Incomplete terms in the Tabular List must be completed with modifiers following a colon to be assignable.
- Synonyms and alternative wording are enclosed in brackets in the Tabular List.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `445`

> **Actual Text**
>
> - [ ] Brackets are used in the Tabular List to enclose synonyms, alternative wording or explanatory phrases. Brackets are used in the Alphabetic Index to identify manifestation codes.
> - ( ) Parentheses are used in both the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List to enclose supplementary words that may be present or absent in the statement of a disease or procedure without affecting the code number to which it is assigned. The terms within the parentheses are referred to as nonessential modifiers. The nonessential modifiers in the Alphabetic Index to Diseases apply to subterms following a main term except when a nonessential modifier and a subentry are mutually exclusive, the subentry takes precedence. For example, in the ICD-10-CM Alphabetic Index under the main term Enteritis, 'acute' is a nonessential modifier and 'chronic' is a subentry. In this case, the nonessential modifier 'acute' does not apply to the subentry 'chronic'.
> - : Colons are used in the Tabular List after an incomplete term which needs one or more of the modifiers following the colon to make it assignable to a given category.
>
> 'Not elsewhere classifiable'
>
> This abbreviation in the Tabular List represents 'other specified'. When a specific code is not available for a condition, the Tabular List includes an NEC entry under a code to identify the code as the 'other specified' code.
>
> 'Not otherwise specified'
>
> This abbreviation is the equivalent of unspecified.
>
> - , Commas are used in the Alphabetic Index and have different meanings based on the context of the Index entry, including alternate verbiage, modifier (essential and nonessential), or alternative for 'and/or.'

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Coding Syntax Definitions

**Key Behaviors:**
- Brackets enclose synonyms, alternative wording, or explanatory phrases in the Tabular List and identify manifestation codes in the Alphabetic Index.
- Parentheses enclose nonessential modifiers that may be present or absent without affecting the code number, except when mutually exclusive with subentries.
- Colons indicate incomplete terms in the Tabular List requiring additional modifiers to become assignable.
- 'Not elsewhere classifiable' represents 'other specified' for conditions without a specific code.
- 'Not otherwise specified' is the equivalent of unspecified.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Subentries take precedence over nonessential modifiers when the two are mutually exclusive.
- Incomplete terms following a colon must be completed with appropriate modifiers to be assignable.
- Commas in the Alphabetic Index denote alternate verbiage, modifiers, or alternatives for 'and/or' based on context.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The core definitions for brackets, parentheses, and colons remain identical between the October 1, 2024 version and the October 2025 version. While the 2025 version includes additional notes on 'Not elsewhere classifiable' and commas, the primary coding logic for syntax symbols has not been altered. The text excerpts provided for both versions confirm that the rules for nonessential modifiers and subentry precedence are unchanged.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Includes detailed examples of nonessential modifiers vs subentries in the Enteritis case. -> October 2025 (New Version): Retains the exact same Enteritis example but adds definitions for 'Not elsewhere classifiable' and 'Not otherwise specified'.

*Traceability: Chunk `13` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `445` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 160. ICD-10-CM Code Structure and Subdivision
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `406`

> **Actual Text**
>
> ICD-10-CM is composed of codes with 3, 4, 5, 6 or 7 characters. Codes with three characters are included in ICD-10-CM as the heading of a category of codes that may be further subdivided by the use of fourth , fifth, sixth or seventh characters to provide greater specificity.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Code Structure and Subdivision

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes are composed of 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7 characters.
- Three-character codes serve as the heading of a category.
- Categories may be further subdivided using fourth, fifth, sixth, or seventh characters.
- Subdivision provides greater specificity for the diagnosis.
- The structure allows for hierarchical coding from broad categories to specific instances.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- A three-character code must be assigned as the initial category heading.
- Additional characters (4th through 7th) must be used to achieve the required level of specificity.
- The full code length must be determined by the specific category rules.

### 161. ICD-10-CM Code Selection Workflow
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical ICD-10-CM code selection workflow with no substantive changes in logic or requirements.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `28`

> **Actual Text**
>
> To select a code in the classification that corresponds to a diagnosis or reason for visit documented in a medical record, first locate the term in the Alphabetic Index, and then verify the code in the Tabular List. Read and be guided by instructional notations that appear in both the Alphabetic Index and the Tabular List.
>
> It is essential to use both the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List when locating and assigning a code. The Alphabetic Index does not always provide the full code. Selection of the full code, including laterality and any applicable 7 th character can only be done in the Tabular List. A dash (-) at the end of an Alphabetic Index entry indicates that additional characters are required. Even if a dash is not included at the Alphabetic Index entry, it is necessary to refer to the Tabular List to verify that no 7 th character is required.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Code Selection Workflow

**Key Behaviors:**
- Locate the diagnosis term in the Alphabetic Index first, then verify the code in the Tabular List.
- Read and follow instructional notations found in both the Alphabetic Index and the Tabular List.
- Use both the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List for all code assignments; the Alphabetic Index does not always provide the full code.
- Select the full code including laterality and applicable 7th characters only from the Tabular List.
- Interpret a dash (-) at the end of an Alphabetic Index entry as an indicator that additional characters are required.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Verification of the code in the Tabular List is mandatory after locating the term in the Alphabetic Index.
- Instructional notations in both the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List must be read and followed.
- Full code selection (including laterality and 7th characters) must be completed in the Tabular List.
- A dash (-) in the Alphabetic Index entry necessitates checking for additional required characters.
- Even if a dash is absent in the Alphabetic Index, the Tabular List must be consulted to verify if a 7th character is required.

### 162. ICD-10-CM Causal Relationship Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical ICD-10-CM Causal Relationship Guidelines regarding the interpretation of 'with' and 'in' and the presumption of causality.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `23`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The word 'with' or 'in' should be interpreted to mean 'associated with' or 'due to' when it appears in a code title, the Alphabetic Index (either under a main term or subterm), or an instructional note in the Tabular List. The classification presumes a causal relationship between the two conditions linked by these terms in the Alphabetic Index or Tabular List. These conditions should be coded as related even in the absence of provider documentation explicitly linking them, unless the documentation clearly states the conditions are unrelated or when another guideline exists that specifically requires a documented linkage between two conditions (e.g., sepsis guideline for 'acute organ dysfunction that is not clearly associated with the sepsis').
>
> For conditions not specifically linked by these relational terms in the classification or when a guideline requires that a linkage between two conditions be explicitly documented, provider documentation must link the conditions in order to code them as related.
>
> The word 'with' in the Alphabetic Index is sequenced immediately following the main term or subterm, not in alphabetical order.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Causal Relationship Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Interpret 'with' or 'in' in code titles or instructional notes as 'associated with' or 'due to', presuming a causal relationship between linked conditions.
- Code conditions as related even without explicit provider documentation if linked by relational terms, unless documentation clearly states they are unrelated.
- Require explicit provider documentation linking conditions to code them as related when conditions are not specifically linked by relational terms in the classification.
- Sequence the word 'with' immediately following the main term or subterm in the Alphabetic Index, disregarding standard alphabetical order.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider documentation must explicitly link conditions to code them as related when no specific relational term ('with', 'in') exists in the classification.
- Documentation must clearly state conditions are unrelated to override the presumption of causality when relational terms are present.
- Conditions linked by 'with' or 'in' must be coded as related even without explicit provider documentation, unless documentation clearly states they are unrelated.
- Provider documentation must explicitly link conditions to code them as related when conditions are not specifically linked by relational terms in the classification or when a specific guideline requires documented linkage.

### 164. ICD-10-CM Tabular List Structure
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical definition of the ICD-10-CM Tabular List hierarchy and character length rules.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `8`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The ICD-10-CM Tabular List contains categories, subcategories and codes. Characters for categories, subcategories and codes may be either a letter or a number. All categories are 3 characters. A three-character category that has no further subdivision is equivalent to a code. Subcategories are either 4 or 5 characters. Codes may be 3, 4, 5, 6 or 7 characters. That is, each level of subdivision after a category is a subcategory. The final level of subdivision is a code. Codes that have applicable 7 th characters are still referred to as codes, not subcategories. A code that has an applicable 7 th character is considered invalid without the 7 th character.
>
> The ICD-10-CM uses an indented format for ease in reference.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Tabular List Structure

**Key Behaviors:**
- Categories are strictly 3 characters and may have no further subdivision.
- Subcategories are either 4 or 5 characters.
- Final codes may be 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7 characters.
- Codes with applicable 7th characters are still referred to as codes, not subcategories.
- A code with an applicable 7th character is considered invalid without it.
- Characters for categories, subcategories, and codes may be either a letter or a number.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- All categories must be exactly 3 characters.
- Subcategories must be exactly 4 or 5 characters.
- Final codes must include applicable 7th characters if they exist for that code.
- A code with an applicable 7th character is invalid if the 7th character is missing.
- The list uses an indented format for reference.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `441`

> **Actual Text**

### 167. ICD-10-CM Default Code Assignment

*Both profiles describe the identical feature and behavior regarding ICD-10-CM default code assignment with no substantive changes.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `26`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A code listed next to a main term in the ICD-10-CM Alphabetic Index is referred to as a default code. The default code represents that condition that is most commonly associated with the main term or is the unspecified code for the condition. If a condition is documented in a medical record (for example, appendicitis) without any additional information, such as acute or chronic, the default code should be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Default Code Assignment

**Key Behaviors:**
- A code listed next to a main term in the ICD-10-CM Alphabetic Index is referred to as a default code.
- The default code represents the condition most commonly associated with the main term or the unspecified code for the condition.
- If a condition is documented without additional information (e.g., acute or chronic), the default code should be assigned.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be documented in the medical record.
- No additional specific information (such as acute or chronic) should be present for the default code to be the appropriate choice.

### 169. ICD-10-CM Principal Diagnosis Sequencing
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical ICD-10-CM sequencing rules for etiology/manifestation pairs and interrelated conditions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `21_384`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Certain conditions have both an underlying etiology and multiple body system manifestations due to the underlying etiology. For such conditions, the ICD-10-CM has a coding convention that requires the underlying condition be sequenced first, if applicable, followed by the manifestation. Wherever such a combination exists, there is a 'use additional code' note at the etiology code, and a 'code first' note at the manifestation code. These instructional notes indicate the proper sequencing order of the codes, etiology followed by manifestation.
>
> In most cases the manifestation codes will have in the code title, 'in diseases classified elsewhere.' Codes with this title are a component of the etiology/ manifestation convention. The code title indicates that it is a manifestation code. 'In diseases classified elsewhere' codes are never permitted to be used as first listed or principal diagnosis codes. They must be used in conjunction with an underlying condition code and they must be listed following the underlying condition. See category F02, Dementia in other diseases classified elsewhere, for an example of this convention.
>
> There are manifestation codes that do not have 'in diseases classified elsewhere' in the title. For such codes, there is a 'use additional code' note at the etiology code and a 'code first' note at the manifestation code, and the rules for sequencing apply.
>
> In addition to the notes in the Tabular List, these conditions also have a specific Alphabetic Index entry structure. In the Alphabetic Index both conditions are listed together with the etiology code first followed by the manifestation codes in brackets. The code in brackets is always to be sequenced second.
>
> An example of the etiology/manifestation convention is dementia with Parkinson's disease. In the Alphabetic Index, a code from category G20 is listed first, followed by code F02.80 or F02.81 -in brackets. A code from category G20 -represents the underlying etiology, Parkinson's disease, and must be sequenced first, whereas codes F02.80 and F02.81 -represent the manifestation of dementia in diseases classified elsewhere, with or without behavioral disturbance.
>
> 'Code first' and 'Use additional code' notes are also used as sequencing rules in the classification for certain codes that are not part of an etiology/ manifestation combination.
>
> See Section I.B.7. Multiple coding for a single condition.
>
> When there are two or more interrelated conditions (such as diseases in the same ICD-10-CM chapter or manifestations characteristically associated with a certain disease) potentially meeting the definition of principal diagnosis, either condition may be sequenced first, unless the circumstances of the admission, the therapy provided, the Tabular List, or the Alphabetic Index indicate otherwise.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Etiology/Manifestation Sequencing and Interrelated Conditions

**Key Behaviors:**
- Underlying etiology conditions must be sequenced first, followed by manifestation codes.
- Manifestation codes titled 'in diseases classified elsewhere' are never permitted as first-listed or principal diagnosis codes.
- Alphabetic Index entries listing etiology followed by manifestation codes in brackets indicate the code in brackets must be sequenced second.
- For interrelated conditions potentially meeting principal diagnosis definition, either may be sequenced first unless specific circumstances indicate otherwise.
- 'Code first' and 'Use additional code' notes serve as sequencing rules for both etiology/manifestation combinations and other specific codes.
- Codes with 'in diseases classified elsewhere' in the title are never permitted to be used as first-listed or principal diagnosis codes.
- The code in brackets in the Alphabetic Index entry must always be sequenced second.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Manifestation codes must be used in conjunction with an underlying condition code.
- Manifestation codes must be listed following the underlying condition code.
- The code in brackets in the Alphabetic Index must always be sequenced second.
- Sequencing must follow the Tabular List, Alphabetic Index, circumstances of admission, or therapy provided.
- Codes with 'in diseases classified elsewhere' in the title must not be used as principal diagnosis.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `453_817`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Certain conditions have both an underlying etiology and multiple body system manifestations due to the underlying etiology. For such conditions, the ICD-10-CM has a coding convention that requires the underlying condition be sequenced first, if applicable, followed by the manifestation. Wherever such a combination exists, there is a 'use additional code' note at the etiology code, and a 'code first' note at the manifestation code. These instructional notes indicate the proper sequencing order of the codes, etiology followed by manifestation.
>
> In most cases the manifestation codes will have in the code title, 'in diseases classified elsewhere.' Codes with this title are a component of the etiology/ manifestation convention. The code title indicates that it is a manifestation code. 'In diseases classified elsewhere' codes are never permitted to be used as first listed or principal diagnosis codes. They must be used in conjunction with an underlying condition code and they must be listed following the underlying condition. See category F02, Dementia in other diseases classified elsewhere, for an example of this convention.
>
> There are manifestation codes that do not have 'in diseases classified elsewhere' in the title. For such codes, there is a 'use additional code' note at the etiology code and a 'code first' note at the manifestation code, and the rules for sequencing apply.
>
> In addition to the notes in the Tabular List, these conditions also have a specific Alphabetic Index entry structure. In the Alphabetic Index both conditions are listed together with the etiology code first followed by the manifestation codes in brackets. The code in brackets is always to be sequenced second.
>
> An example of the etiology/manifestation convention is dementia with Parkinson's disease. In the Alphabetic Index, a code from category G20 is listed first, followed by code F02.80 or F02.81 -in brackets. A code from category G20 -represents the underlying etiology, Parkinson's disease, and must be sequenced first, whereas codes F02.80 and F02.81 -represent the manifestation of dementia in diseases classified elsewhere, with or without behavioral disturbance.
>
> 'Code first' and 'Use additional code' notes are also used as sequencing rules in the classification for certain codes that are not part of an etiology/ manifestation combination.
>
> See Section I.B.7. Multiple coding for a single condition.
>
> When there are two or more interrelated conditions (such as diseases in the same ICD-10-CM chapter or manifestations characteristically associated with a certain disease) potentially meeting the definition of principal diagnosis, either condition may be sequenced first, unless the circumstances of the admission, the therapy provided, the Tabular List, or the Alphabetic Index indicate otherwise.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Etiology/Manifestation Sequencing and Interrelated Conditions

**Key Behaviors:**
- Underlying etiology codes must be sequenced first, followed by manifestation codes when a combination exists.
- Manifestation codes titled 'in diseases classified elsewhere' are never permitted as first-listed or principal diagnosis codes.
- Alphabetic Index entries listing etiology followed by manifestation codes in brackets indicate the bracketed code must be sequenced second.
- For interrelated conditions potentially meeting principal diagnosis definition, either may be sequenced first unless specific circumstances indicate otherwise.
- 'Code first' and 'Use additional code' notes serve as sequencing rules for both etiology/manifestation combinations and other specific codes.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Manifestation codes must be listed following the underlying condition code.
- Codes in brackets within Alphabetic Index entries must always be sequenced second.
- Principal diagnosis sequencing must align with circumstances of admission, therapy provided, Tabular List, or Alphabetic Index instructions.
- Etiology codes must be used in conjunction with manifestation codes for 'in diseases classified elsewhere' entries.
- Instructional notes ('code first', 'use additional code') must be followed to determine proper sequencing order.
- Codes with 'in diseases classified elsewhere' in the title must be used in conjunction with an underlying condition code.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present the exact same clinical logic regarding the sequencing of underlying etiology codes before manifestation codes. Both versions explicitly state that codes titled 'in diseases classified elsewhere' cannot be used as principal diagnoses and must follow the etiology code. The raw text excerpts are identical, indicating no substantive update to the coding convention.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Underlying etiology conditions must be sequenced first, followed by manifestation codes. -> October 2025 (New Version): Underlying etiology codes must be sequenced first, followed by manifestation codes when a combination exists.

*Traceability: Chunk `21_384` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `453_817` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 170. FY 2025 ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles describe the FY 2025 ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines feature, comparing the October 2024 release against the updated October 2025 version.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `0_2_97`

> **Actual Text**
>
> FY 2025 -- UPDATED October 1, 2024 (October 1, 2024 - September 30, 2025)
>
> Narrative changes appear in bold text
>
> Items underlined have been moved within the guidelines since the April 2024, FY 2024 version Italics are used to indicate revisions to heading changes
>
> The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) and the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS), two departments within the U.S. Federal Government's Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS) provide the following guidelines for coding and reporting using the International Classification of Diseases, 10 th Revision, Clinical Modification (ICD-10-CM). These guidelines should be used as a companion document to the official version of the ICD-10-CM as published on the NCHS website. The ICD-10-CM is a morbidity classification published by the United States for classifying diagnoses and reason for visits in all health care settings. The ICD10-CM is based on the ICD-10, the statistical classification of disease published by the World Health Organization (WHO).
>
> These guidelines have been approved by the four organizations that make up the Cooperating Parties for the ICD-10-CM: the American Hospital Association (AHA), the American Health Information Management Association (AHIMA), CMS, and NCHS.
>
> These guidelines are a set of rules that have been developed to accompany and complement the official conventions and instructions provided within the ICD-10-CM itself. The instructions and conventions of the classification take precedence over guidelines. These guidelines are based on the coding and sequencing instructions in the Tabular List and Alphabetic Index of ICD-10-CM, but provide additional instruction. Adherence to these guidelines when assigning ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes is required under the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA). The diagnosis codes (Tabular List and Alphabetic Index) have been adopted under HIPAA for all healthcare settings. A joint effort between the healthcare provider and the coder is essential to achieve complete and accurate documentation, code assignment, and reporting of diagnoses and procedures. These guidelines have been developed to assist both the healthcare provider and the coder in identifying those diagnoses that are to be reported. The importance of consistent, complete documentation in the medical record cannot be overemphasized. Without such documentation accurate coding cannot be achieved. The entire record should be reviewed to determine the specific reason for the encounter and the conditions treated.
>
> The term encounter is used for all settings, including hospital admissions. In the context of these guidelines, the term provider is used throughout the guidelines to mean physician or any qualified health care practitioner who is legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis. Only this set of guidelines, approved by the Cooperating Parties, is official.
>
> The guidelines are organized into sections. Section I includes the structure and conventions of the classification and general guidelines that apply to the entire classification, and chapter-specific guidelines that correspond to the chapters as they are arranged in the classification. Section II includes guidelines for selection of principal diagnosis for non-outpatient settings. Section III includes guidelines for reporting additional diagnoses in non-outpatient settings. Section IV is for outpatient coding and reporting. It is necessary to review all sections of the guidelines to fully understand all of the rules and instructions needed to code properly.
>
> {"b.": "c.", "Sequencing of External Causes of Morbidity": "Other External Causes of Morbidity Code Issues.................................................................", "Codes............................................................ 18": "18"}
> {"b.": "d.", "Sequencing of External Causes of Morbidity": "Use of Z codes ......................................................................................................................", "Codes............................................................ 18": "18"}
> {"b.": "C. Chapter-Specific Coding Guidelines ............................................................................................", "Sequencing of External Causes of Morbidity": "C. Chapter-Specific Coding Guidelines ............................................................................................", "Codes............................................................ 18": "19"}
> [... +43 more table rows omitted ...]
>
> Chapter 2 of the ICD-10-CM contains the codes for most benign and all malignant neoplasms. Certain benign neoplasms, such as prostatic adenomas, may be found in the specific body system chapters. To properly code a neoplasm, it is necessary to determine from the record if the neoplasm is benign, in-situ, malignant, or of uncertain histologic behavior. If malignant, any secondary (metastatic) sites should also be determined.
>
> Primary malignant neoplasms overlapping site boundaries
>
> A primary malignant neoplasm that overlaps two or more contiguous (next to each other) sites should be classified to the subcategory/code .8 ('overlapping lesion'), unless the combination is specifically indexed elsewhere. For multiple neoplasms of the same site that are not contiguous such as tumors in different quadrants of the same breast, codes for each site should be assigned.
>
> Malignant neoplasm of ectopic tissue
>
> Malignant neoplasms of ectopic tissue are to be coded to the site of origin mentioned, e.g., ectopic pancreatic malignant neoplasms involving the stomach are coded to malignant neoplasm of pancreas, unspecified (C25.9).
>
> The neoplasm table in the Alphabetic Index should be referenced first. However, if the histological term is documented, that term should be referenced first, rather than going immediately to the Neoplasm Table, in order to determine which column in the Neoplasm Table is appropriate. For example, if the documentation indicates 'adenoma,' refer to the term in the Alphabetic Index to review the entries under this term and the instructional note to 'see also neoplasm, by site, benign.' The table provides the proper code based on the type of neoplasm and the site. It is important to select the proper column in the table that corresponds to the type of neoplasm. The Tabular List should then be referenced to verify that the correct code has been selected from the table and that a more specific site code does not exist.
>
> See Section I.C.21. Factors influencing health status and contact with health services, Status, for information regarding Z15.0, codes for genetic susceptibility to cancer.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Neoplasm Coding and Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Primary malignant neoplasms overlapping two or more contiguous sites must be classified to the subcategory/code .8 ('overlapping lesion') unless specifically indexed elsewhere.
- Malignant neoplasms of ectopic tissue must be coded to the site of origin mentioned in the documentation.
- The neoplasm table in the Alphabetic Index should be referenced first, but if the histological term is documented, that term must be referenced first to determine the appropriate column in the Neoplasm Table.
- Multiple neoplasms of the same site that are not contiguous (e.g., tumors in different quadrants of the same breast) require codes for each site to be assigned.
- The Tabular List must be referenced to verify the correct code and ensure a more specific site code does not exist.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- It is necessary to determine from the record if the neoplasm is benign, in-situ, malignant, or of uncertain histologic behavior.
- If malignant, any secondary (metastatic) sites should also be determined.
- The histological term must be referenced in the Alphabetic Index before the Neoplasm Table if documented.
- Verification in the Tabular List is required to confirm the correct code and rule out more specific site codes.

> Chapter 2 of the ICD-10-CM contains the codes for most benign and all malignant neoplasms. Certain benign neoplasms, such as prostatic adenomas, may be found in the specific body system chapters. **Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** FY 2026 ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Guidelines are organized into sections covering structure, principal diagnosis selection, additional diagnoses, and outpatient coding.
- Instructions and conventions within the ICD-10-CM classification take precedence over general guidelines.
- Primary malignant neoplasms overlapping two or more contiguous sites should be classified to the subcategory/code .8 ('overlapping lesion').
- For multiple neoplasms of the same site that are not contiguous, codes for each site should be assigned.
- Adherence to these guidelines is required under HIPAA for assigning ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes.
- The term 'encounter' encompasses all healthcare settings, including hospital admissions.
- The term 'provider' refers specifically to a physician or any qualified health care practitioner legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The entire medical record should be reviewed to determine the specific reason for the encounter and conditions treated.
- A joint effort between the healthcare provider and the coder is essential for accurate documentation and code assignment.
- Documentation must indicate if a neoplasm is benign, in-situ, malignant, or of uncertain histologic behavior.
- If a neoplasm is malignant, any secondary (metastatic) sites must also be determined.
- The combination of overlapping sites must not be specifically indexed elsewhere before using the .8 code.
- The guidelines are organized into four specific sections: Section I (structure/conventions), Section II (principal diagnosis for non-outpatient), Section III (additional diagnoses for non-outpatient), and Section IV (outpatient coding).
- Chapter 16 contains specific rules for conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00-P96), including observation/evaluation of newborns and coding additional perinatal diagnoses.

### 171. ICD-10-CM Diagnosis Code Reporting
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature and verbatim behaviors regarding ICD-10-CM diagnosis code reporting limits and documentation requirements.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `39_403`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Each unique ICD-10-CM diagnosis code may be reported only once for an encounter. This applies to bilateral conditions when there are no distinct codes identifying laterality or two different conditions classified to the same ICD-10-CM diagnosis code.
>
> For accurate reporting of ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes, the documentation should describe the patient's condition, using terminology which includes specific diagnoses as well as symptoms, problems, or reasons for the encounter. There are ICD-10-CM codes to describe all of these.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Diagnosis Code Reporting

**Key Behaviors:**
- Each unique ICD-10-CM diagnosis code may be reported only once for an encounter.
- This restriction applies to bilateral conditions when there are no distinct codes identifying laterality.
- This restriction applies when two different conditions are classified to the same ICD-10-CM diagnosis code.
- Documentation should describe the patient's condition using terminology that includes specific diagnoses as well as symptoms, problems, or reasons for the encounter.
- ICD-10-CM codes are available to describe all documented conditions including symptoms and problems.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Ensure no duplicate reporting of the same unique ICD-10-CM diagnosis code within a single encounter.
- Verify whether distinct codes exist for laterality before applying the single-code rule to bilateral conditions.
- Confirm if multiple conditions map to the same ICD-10-CM code before reporting them separately.
- Documentation must include specific diagnoses alongside symptoms, problems, or reasons for the encounter.
- Select appropriate ICD-10-CM codes that cover the full scope of documented patient conditions.

---

### 172. ICD-10-CM Diagnosis Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 8/10  
*Both profiles address the same feature area of ICD-10-CM diagnosis coding, specifically focusing on principal diagnosis selection and the handling of borderline conditions, though the newer version omits specific borderline rules.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `44_409`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If the provider documents a "borderline" diagnosis at the time of discharge, the diagnosis is coded as confirmed, unless the classification provides a specific entry (e.g., borderline diabetes). If a borderline condition has a specific index entry in ICD-10-CM, it should be coded as such. Since borderline conditions are not uncertain diagnoses, no distinction is made between the care setting (inpatient versus outpatient). Whenever the documentation is unclear regarding a borderline condition, coders are encouraged to query for clarification.
>
> List first the ICD-10-CM code for the diagnosis, condition, problem, or other reason for encounter/visit shown in the medical record to be chiefly responsible for the services provided. List additional codes that describe any coexisting conditions. In some cases, the first-listed diagnosis may be a symptom when a diagnosis has not been established (confirmed) by the provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Coding of Borderline Diagnoses and Principal Diagnosis Selection

**Key Behaviors:**
- Borderline diagnoses are coded as confirmed unless a specific index entry exists (e.g., borderline diabetes).
- No distinction is made between care settings (inpatient vs. outpatient) for borderline conditions.
- Coders are encouraged to query for clarification when documentation regarding a borderline condition is unclear.
- The first-listed diagnosis must be the condition chiefly responsible for services provided.
- Additional codes should be listed to describe any coexisting conditions.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- A specific index entry must exist in ICD-10-CM to code a borderline condition as such.
- The diagnosis must be documented as chiefly responsible for the services provided to be listed first.
- If a diagnosis has not been established (confirmed) by the provider, the first-listed diagnosis may be a symptom.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `842`

> **Actual Text**
>
> List first the ICD-10-CM code for the diagnosis, condition, problem, or other reason for encounter/visit shown in the medical record to be chiefly responsible for the services provided. List additional codes that describe any coexisting conditions. In some cases, the first-listed diagnosis may be a symptom when a diagnosis has not been established (confirmed) by the provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Chief and Coexisting Diagnosis Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- List the ICD-10-CM code for the diagnosis chiefly responsible for services provided first.
- List additional codes to describe any coexisting conditions.
- The first-listed diagnosis may be a symptom if a confirmed diagnosis has not been established by the provider.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The first-listed diagnosis must be the condition chiefly responsible for the services provided.
- Additional codes must describe coexisting conditions.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version explicitly detailed rules for coding borderline diagnoses (treating them as confirmed unless a specific index entry exists) and mandated queries for unclear documentation. The newer version removes these specific borderline instructions entirely, focusing solely on the general rule of listing the chief reason for encounter first. While the core logic for principal diagnosis selection remains identical in both texts, the removal of the specific borderline guidance represents a reduction in scope rather than a logical contradiction or expansion.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Borderline diagnoses are coded as confirmed unless a specific index entry exists, and coders are encouraged to query for clarification. -> October 2025 (New Version): Specific instructions regarding borderline diagnoses and the requirement to query for clarification are omitted.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicitly states no distinction is made between inpatient and outpatient settings for borderline conditions. -> October 2025 (New Version): Does not mention care setting distinctions or borderline condition specifics.

### 173. Z02 Encounter Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Z02 Encounter Coding Guidelines with verbatim identical text in the raw excerpts.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `369_375_376`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The Z codes allow for the description of encounters for routine examinations, such as, a general check-up, or, examinations for administrative purposes, such as, a pre-employment physical. The codes are not to be used if the examination is for diagnosis of a suspected condition or for treatment purposes. In such cases the diagnosis code is used. During a routine exam, should a diagnosis or condition be discovered, it should be coded as an additional code. Pre-existing and chronic conditions and history codes may also be included as additional codes as long as the examination is for administrative purposes and not focused on any particular condition.
>
> Some of the codes for routine health examinations distinguish between 'with' and 'without' abnormal findings. Code assignment depends on the information that is known at the time the encounter is being coded. For example, if no abnormal findings were found during the examination, but the encounter is being coded before test results are back, it is acceptable to assign the code for 'without abnormal findings.' When assigning a code for 'with abnormal findings,' additional code(s) should be assigned to identify the specific abnormal finding(s).
>
> Pre-operative examination and pre-procedural laboratory examination Z codes are for use only in those situations when a patient is being cleared for a procedure or surgery and no treatment is given.
>
> The Z codes/categories for routine and administrative examinations:
>
> Z00
>
> Encounter for general examination without complaint, suspected or reported diagnosis
>
> Z01
>
> Encounter for other special examination without complaint, suspected or reported diagnosis
>
> Z02
>
> Encounter for administrative examination
>
> Except: Z02.9, Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified
>
> Z32.0-
>
> Encounter for pregnancy test
>
> Z02.9
>
> Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
>
> Z04.9
>
> {"Z13.9": "Z41.9", "Encounter for screening, unspecified": "Encounter for procedure for purposes other than remedying health state, unspecified"}
> {"Z13.9": "Z52.9", "Encounter for screening, unspecified": "Donor of unspecified organ or tissue"}
> {"Z13.9": "Z86.59", "Encounter for screening, unspecified": "Personal history of other mental and behavioral disorders"}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Z02 Encounter Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes are not to be used if the examination is for diagnosis of a suspected condition or for treatment purposes.
- In such cases the diagnosis code is used.
- During a routine exam, should a diagnosis or condition be discovered, it should be coded as an additional code.
- Pre-existing and chronic conditions and history codes may also be included as additional codes as long as the examination is for administrative purposes and not focused on any particular condition.
- Code assignment depends on the information that is known at the time the encounter is being coded.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Examination must be for administrative purposes (e.g., pre-employment physical).
- Examination must not be for diagnosis of a suspected condition.
- Examination must not be for treatment purposes.
- If abnormal findings are present, additional code(s) must be assigned to identify the specific abnormal finding(s).
- Pre-operative examination Z codes are for use only when no treatment is given.
- Code assignment depends on the information known at the time the encounter is being coded.
- If no abnormal findings were found but test results are pending, the code for 'without abnormal findings' is acceptable.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `799`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The Z codes allow for the description of encounters for routine examinations, such as, a general check-up, or, examinations for administrative purposes, such as, a pre-employment physical. The codes are not to be used if the examination is for diagnosis of a suspected condition or for treatment purposes. In such cases the diagnosis code is used. During a routine exam, should a diagnosis or condition be discovered, it should be coded as an additional code. Pre-existing and chronic conditions and history codes may also be included as additional codes as long as the examination is for administrative purposes and not focused on any particular condition.
>
> Some of the codes for routine health examinations distinguish between 'with' and 'without' abnormal findings. Code assignment depends on the information that is known at the time the encounter is being coded. For example, if no abnormal findings were found during the examination, but the encounter is being coded before test results are back, it is acceptable to assign the code for 'without abnormal findings.' When assigning a code for 'with abnormal findings,' additional code(s) should be assigned to identify the specific abnormal finding(s).
>
> Pre-operative examination and pre-procedural laboratory examination Z codes are for use only in those situations when a patient is being cleared for a procedure or surgery and no treatment is given.
>
> The Z codes/categories for routine and administrative examinations:
>
> Z00
>
> Encounter for general examination without complaint, suspected or reported diagnosis
>
> - Z01
>
> Encounter for other special examination without complaint, suspected or reported diagnosis
>
> - Z02
>
> Encounter for administrative examination Except: Z02.9, Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified
>
> - Z32.0- Encounter for pregnancy test

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Z02 Encounter Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Z codes are used for routine examinations like general check-ups or administrative purposes such as pre-employment physicals.
- Z codes must not be used if the examination is for diagnosing a suspected condition or for treatment purposes; in those cases, the diagnosis code is used.
- If a diagnosis or condition is discovered during a routine exam, it should be coded as an additional code.
- Pre-existing and chronic conditions and history codes may be included as additional codes if the exam is for administrative purposes and not focused on a particular condition.
- Code assignment for 'with' vs 'without' abnormal findings depends on information known at the time of coding; if results are pending, 'without abnormal findings' is acceptable.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The examination must be for routine or administrative purposes, not for diagnosis of a suspected condition.
- The examination must not be for treatment purposes.
- If abnormal findings are present, additional codes must be assigned to identify the specific findings.
- Pre-operative and pre-procedural laboratory Z codes require that no treatment be given and the patient is being cleared for a procedure.
- History codes may only be included if the examination is not focused on any particular condition.
- Code assignment for 'with' vs 'without' abnormal findings depends on information known at the time of coding; if results are pending, 'without abnormal findings' is acceptable.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions contain the exact same raw text regarding Z02 guidelines, including rules for administrative exams, suspected conditions, and abnormal findings. The extracted profiles and requirements lists are semantically identical, indicating no actual change in coding logic occurred between these versions. The only difference is the metadata label (Taxonomy description) which was slightly updated to be more concise in the new version.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Taxonomy description mentions specific ICD-10-CM code mappings for Z02.9 and Z13.9 -> October 2025 (New Version): Taxonomy description is generalized to define rules for Z02 routine examinations without specific code examples.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Raw text excerpt ends abruptly mid-sentence ('specific abnormal find') -> October 2025 (New Version): Raw text excerpt ends abruptly mid-sentence ('specific abnormal find').

*Traceability: Chunk `369_375_376` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `799` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 174. ICD-10 Follow-Up Code Implementation
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding ICD-10 follow-up code implementation, sequencing, and usage criteria.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `359_360`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The follow-up codes are used to explain continuing surveillance following completed treatment of a disease, condition, or injury. They imply that the condition has been fully treated and no longer exists. They should not be confused with aftercare codes, or injury codes with a 7 th character for subsequent encounter, that explain ongoing care of a healing condition or its sequelae. Follow-up codes may be used in conjunction with history codes to provide the full picture of the healed condition and its treatment. The follow-up code is sequenced first, followed by the history code.
>
> A follow-up code may be used to explain multiple visits. Should a condition be found to have recurred on the follow-up visit, then the diagnosis code for the condition should be assigned in place of the follow-up code.
>
> {"The follow-up Z code s/ categories:": "Z08 Encounter for follow-up examination after completed treatment for malignant neoplasm"}
> {"The follow-up Z code s/ categories:": "Z09 Encounter for follow-up examination after completed treatment for conditions other than malignant neoplasm"}

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Follow-Up Code Implementation

**Key Behaviors:**
- Follow-up codes imply the condition has been fully treated and no longer exists.
- Follow-up codes should not be confused with aftercare codes or subsequent encounter codes.
- Follow-up codes may be used in conjunction with history codes to provide the full picture of the healed condition.
- The follow-up code is sequenced first, followed by the history code.
- If a condition recurs on a follow-up visit, the diagnosis code for the condition replaces the follow-up code.
- Follow-up codes may be used to explain multiple visits.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be documented as fully treated and no longer existing.
- Follow-up code must be sequenced before the history code.
- History code must be assigned to describe the healed condition and its treatment.
- If recurrence is found, the specific diagnosis code for the condition must replace the follow-up code.
- Follow-up codes are distinct from aftercare codes and subsequent encounter codes.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `790_791`

> **Actual Text**

Follow-up codes may be used in conjunction with history codes to provide the full picture of the healed condition and its treatment. The follow-up code is sequenced first, followed by the history code.
>
> A follow-up code may be used to explain multiple visits. Should a condition be found to have recurred on the follow-up visit, then the diagnosis code for the condition should be assigned in place of the follow-up code.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Follow-Up Code Implementation

**Key Behaviors:**
- Follow-up codes imply the condition has been fully treated and no longer exists.
- Follow-up codes should not be confused with aftercare codes or subsequent encounter codes.
- Follow-up codes may be used in conjunction with history codes to provide the full picture of the healed condition.
- The follow-up code is sequenced first, followed by the history code.
- If a condition recurs on a follow-up visit, the diagnosis code for the condition replaces the follow-up code.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be documented as fully treated and no longer existing.
- Documentation must distinguish between completed treatment (follow-up) and ongoing healing (aftercare/subsequent encounter).
- When used with history codes, the follow-up code must be sequenced first.

### 175. ICD-10 POA Reporting Logic
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical ICD-10 POA Reporting Logic feature with nearly identical behavioral rules and requirements.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `425`

> **Actual Text**
>
> - Condition is on the 'Exempt from Reporting' list
>
> Leave the 'present on admission' field blank if the condition is on the list of ICD10-CM codes for which this field is not applicable. This is the only circumstance in which the field may be left blank.
>
> - POA Explicitly Documented Assign Y for any condition the provider explicitly documents as being present on admission.
>
> Assign N for any condition the provider explicitly documents as not present at the time of admission.
>
> - Conditions diagnosed prior to inpatient admission Assign 'Y' for conditions that were diagnosed prior to admission (example:
>
> hypertension, diabetes mellitus, asthma)
>
> - Conditions diagnosed during the admission but clearly present before admission Assign 'Y' for conditions diagnosed during the admission that were clearly present but not diagnosed until after admission occurred.
>
> Diagnoses subsequently confirmed after admission are considered present on admission if at the time of admission they are documented as suspected, possible, rule out, differential diagnosis, or constitute an underlying cause of a symptom that is present at the time of admission.
>
> - Condition develops during outpatient encounter prior to inpatient admission Assign Y for any condition that develops during an outpatient encounter prior to a written order for inpatient admission.
> - Documentation does not indicate whether condition was present on admission Assign 'U' when the medical record documentation is unclear as to whether the condition was present on admission. 'U' should not be routinely assigned and used only in very limited circumstances. Coders are encouraged to query the providers when the documentation is unclear.
>
> Documentation states that it cannot be determined whether the condition was or was not present on admission
>
> - Assign 'W' when the medical record documentation indicates that it cannot be
>
> clinically determined whether or not the condition was present on admission.
>
> - Chronic condition with acute exacerbation during the admission If a single code identifies both the chronic condition and the acute exacerbation, see POA guidelines pertaining to codes that contain multiple clinical concepts.
>
> If a single code only identifies the chronic condition and not the acute exacerbation (e.g., acute exacerbation of chronic leukemia), assign 'Y.'
>
> Conditions documented as possible, probable, suspected, or rule out at the time of discharge
>
> If the final diagnosis contains a possible, probable, suspected, or rule out diagnosis, and this diagnosis was based on signs, symptoms or clinical findings suspected at the time of inpatient admission, assign 'Y.'
>
> If the final diagnosis contains a possible, probable, suspected, or rule out diagnosis, and this diagnosis was based on signs, symptoms or clinical findings that were not present on admission, assign 'N'.
>
> - Conditions documented as impending or threatened at the time of discharge If the final diagnosis contains an impending or threatened diagnosis, and this diagnosis is based on symptoms or clinical findings that were present on admission, assign 'Y'.
>
> If the final diagnosis contains an impending or threatened diagnosis, and this diagnosis is based on symptoms or clinical findings that were not present on admission, assign 'N'.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 POA Reporting Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Leave the 'present on admission' field blank if the condition is on the exempt list.
- Assign 'Y' for conditions explicitly documented as present on admission or diagnosed prior to admission.
- Assign 'N' for conditions explicitly documented as not present at the time of admission.
- Assign 'U' when documentation is unclear regarding presence on admission.
- Assign 'W' when it cannot be clinically determined whether the condition was present on admission.
- Assign 'Y' for conditions diagnosed during the admission that were clearly present but not diagnosed until after admission occurred.
- Assign 'Y' for conditions that develop during an outpatient encounter prior to a written order for inpatient admission.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must not be on the 'Exempt from Reporting' list to require a POA indicator.
- Provider must explicitly document presence or absence of the condition for 'Y' or 'N' assignment.
- Diagnosis must be based on signs, symptoms, or clinical findings present at the time of inpatient admission to assign 'Y' for uncertain diagnoses.
- Query the provider when documentation is unclear to avoid routine assignment of 'U'.
- For single codes identifying both chronic condition and acute exacerbation, follow specific POA guidelines for multiple clinical concepts.
- Condition must be based on signs, symptoms, or clinical findings present at the time of inpatient admission to assign 'Y' for uncertain diagnoses.
- Coders are encouraged to query the provider when documentation is unclear regarding presence on admission.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 POA Reporting Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Leave the 'present on admission' field blank if the condition is on the exempt list.
- Assign 'Y' for conditions explicitly documented as present on admission or diagnosed prior to admission.
- Assign 'N' for conditions explicitly documented as not present at the time of admission.
- Assign 'U' when documentation is unclear regarding presence on admission.
- Assign 'W' when it cannot be clinically determined whether the condition was present on admission.
- Assign 'Y' for conditions diagnosed during admission that were clearly present before admission but not diagnosed until after admission occurred.
- Assign 'Y' for conditions that develop during an outpatient encounter prior to a written order for inpatient admission.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**

- Condition must be based on signs, symptoms, or clinical findings present at the time of inpatient admission to assign 'Y' for uncertain cases.
- Final diagnosis must be based on signs, symptoms, or clinical findings suspected at the time of inpatient admission to assign 'Y' for 'possible/probable/suspected/rule out' diagnoses.

**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Section 21 Factors and Screening

- Screening must be performed for a specific condition and documented as such

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Codes must be assigned based on documentation of factors influencing health status.
- Screening codes require documentation of the specific test or procedure performed.

### 177. ICD-10-CM Diagnosis Code Assignment Logic
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical logic for assigning 'Y' or 'N' flags based on admission status for multiple conditions represented by a single code.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `428`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When the same ICD-10-CM diagnosis code applies to two or more conditions during the same encounter (e.g. two separate conditions classified to the same ICD-10-CM diagnosis code):
>
> Assign 'Y' if all conditions represented by the single ICD-10-CM code were present on admission (e.g. bilateral unspecified age-related cataracts).
>
> Assign 'N' if any of the conditions represented by the single ICD-10-CM code was not present on admission (e.g. traumatic secondary and recurrent hemorrhage and seroma is assigned to a single code T79.2, but only one of the conditions was present on admission).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Diagnosis Code Assignment Logic for Multiple Conditions

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign 'Y' if all conditions represented by the single ICD-10-CM code were present on admission.
- Assign 'N' if any of the conditions represented by the single ICD-10-CM code was not present on admission.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The same ICD-10-CM diagnosis code must apply to two or more conditions during the same encounter.
- The presence or absence of conditions on admission must be determined for each condition represented by the code.

---

### 178. ICD-10-CM Dementia Classification Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both chunks describe the identical ICD-10-CM Dementia Classification guidelines with verbatim matching text and behaviors.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `146`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The ICD-10-CM classifies dementia (categories F01, F02, and F03) on the basis of the etiology and severity (unspecified, mild, moderate or severe). Selection of the appropriate severity level requires the provider's clinical judgment and codes should be assigned only on the basis of provider documentation (as defined in the Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting ), unless otherwise instructed by the classification. If the documentation does not provide information about the severity of the dementia, assign the appropriate code for unspecified severity.
>
> If a patient is admitted to an inpatient acute care hospital or other inpatient facility setting with dementia at one severity level and it progresses to a higher severity level, assign one code for the highest severity level reported during the stay.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Dementia Classification

**Key Behaviors:**
- Classify dementia (categories F01, F02, and F03) based on etiology and severity.
- Assign codes only on the basis of provider documentation unless otherwise instructed.
- Assign unspecified severity code if documentation lacks severity information.
- Assign one code for the highest severity level if dementia progresses during an inpatient stay.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider must document the specific severity level (unspecified, mild, moderate, or severe).
- Provider must document the etiology of the dementia.
- If severity is not documented, assign the code for unspecified severity.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `576`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The ICD-10-CM classifies dementia (categories F01, F02, and F03) on the basis of the etiology and severity (unspecified, mild, moderate or severe). Selection of the appropriate severity level requires the provider's clinical judgment and codes should be assigned only on the basis of provider documentation (as defined in the Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting ), unless otherwise instructed by the classification. If the documentation does not provide information about the severity of the dementia, assign the appropriate code for unspecified severity.
>
> If a patient is admitted to an inpatient acute care hospital or other inpatient facility setting with dementia at one severity level and it progresses to a higher severity level, assign one code for the highest severity level reported during the stay.

**Extracted Profile:**

- Assign codes only on the basis of provider documentation unless otherwise instructed.
- Assign unspecified severity code if documentation lacks severity information.
- Assign one code for the highest severity level if dementia progresses during an inpatient stay.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider must document the specific severity level (unspecified, mild, moderate, or severe).
- If severity is not documented, assign the code for unspecified severity.
- For inpatient stays with progression, code the highest severity level reported during the stay.

### 179. Outpatient ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature regarding outpatient ICD-10-CM coding guidelines with identical behavioral constraints.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `398`

> **Actual Text**
>
> These coding guidelines for outpatient diagnoses have been approved for use by hospitals/ providers in coding and reporting hospital-based outpatient services and provider-based office visits. Guidelines in Section I, Conventions, general coding guidelines and chapter-specific guidelines, should also be applied for outpatient services and office visits.
>
> Information about the use of certain abbreviations, punctuation, symbols, and other conventions used in the ICD-10-CM Tabular List (code numbers and titles), can be found in Section IA of these guidelines, under 'Conventions Used in the Tabular List.' Section I.B. contains general guidelines that apply to the entire classification. Section I.C. contains chapter-specific guidelines that correspond to the chapters as they are arranged in the classification. Information about the correct sequence to use in finding a code is also described in Section I.
>
> The terms encounter and visit are often used interchangeably in describing outpatient service contacts and, therefore, appear together in these guidelines without distinguishing one from the other.
>
> Though the conventions and general guidelines apply to all settings, coding guidelines for outpatient and provider reporting of diagnoses will vary in a number of instances from those for inpatient diagnoses, recognizing that:
>
> The Uniform Hospital Discharge Data Set (UHDDS) definition of principal diagnosis does not apply to hospital-based outpatient services and provider-based office visits.
>
> Coding guidelines for inconclusive diagnoses (probable, suspected, rule out, etc.) were developed for inpatient reporting and do not apply to outpatients.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Outpatient ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Guidelines in Section I (Conventions, general, and chapter-specific) must be applied to outpatient services and office visits.
- The terms 'encounter' and 'visit' are used interchangeably to describe outpatient service contacts.
- Coding guidelines for outpatient and provider reporting of diagnoses vary from those for inpatient diagnoses.
- The Uniform Hospital Discharge Data Set (UHDDS) definition of principal diagnosis does not apply to hospital-based outpatient services and provider-based office visits.
- Coding guidelines for inconclusive diagnoses (probable, suspected, rule out, etc.) do not apply to outpatients.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Apply Section I conventions, general guidelines, and chapter-specific guidelines to outpatient services.
- Do not apply the UHDDS definition of principal diagnosis to hospital-based outpatient services or provider-based office visits.
- Do not code inconclusive diagnoses (probable, suspected, rule out) for outpatients.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `831`

> **Actual Text**
>
> These coding guidelines for outpatient diagnoses have been approved for use by hospitals/ providers in coding and reporting hospital-based outpatient services and provider-based office visits. Guidelines in Section I, Conventions, general coding guidelines and chapter-specific guidelines, should also be applied for outpatient services and office visits.
>
> Information about the use of certain abbreviations, punctuation, symbols, and other conventions used in the ICD-10-CM Tabular List (code numbers and titles), can be found in Section IA of these guidelines, under 'Conventions Used in the Tabular List.' Section I.B. contains general guidelines that apply to the entire classification. Section I.C. contains chapter-specific guidelines that correspond to the chapters as they are arranged in the classification. Information about the correct sequence to use in finding a code is also described in Section I.
>
> The terms encounter and visit are often used interchangeably in describing outpatient service contacts and, therefore, appear together in these guidelines without distinguishing one from the other.
>
> Though the conventions and general guidelines apply to all settings, coding guidelines for outpatient and provider reporting of diagnoses will vary in a number of instances from those for inpatient diagnoses, recognizing that:
>
> The Uniform Hospital Discharge Data Set (UHDDS) definition of principal diagnosis does not apply to hospital-based outpatient services and provider-based office visits.
>
> Coding guidelines for inconclusive diagnoses (probable, suspected, rule out, etc.) were developed for inpatient reporting and do not apply to outpatients.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Outpatient ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines

- The terms 'encounter' and 'visit' are used interchangeably to describe outpatient service contacts.
- Coding guidelines for outpatient and provider reporting vary from inpatient diagnoses in specific instances.
- The UHDDS definition of principal diagnosis does not apply to hospital-based outpatient services or provider-based office visits.
- Coding guidelines for inconclusive diagnoses (probable, suspected, rule out) do not apply to outpatients.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**

- Do not apply the UHDDS definition of principal diagnosis to outpatient settings.

### 180. COVID-19 Acute Respiratory Failure Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines and Implementation  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for assigning U07.1 and J96.0- for COVID-19 acute respiratory failure.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `82_87`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When the reason for the encounter/admission is a respiratory manifestation of COVID-19, assign code U07.1, COVID-19, as the principal/first-listed diagnosis and assign code(s) for the respiratory manifestation(s) as additional diagnoses.
>
> The following conditions are examples of common respiratory manifestations of COVID-19.
>
> For acute respiratory failure due to COVID-19, assign code U07.1, and code J96.0-, Acute respiratory failure.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Acute Respiratory Failure Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code U07.1 as the principal/first-listed diagnosis when the encounter reason is a respiratory manifestation of COVID-19.
- Assign code(s) for the specific respiratory manifestation(s) as additional diagnoses.
- Assign code J96.0- for acute respiratory failure due to COVID-19 alongside U07.1.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The reason for the encounter or admission must be a respiratory manifestation of COVID-19.
- Code U07.1 must be assigned as the principal diagnosis for respiratory manifestations of COVID-19.
- Code J96.0- must be used specifically for acute respiratory failure due to COVID-19.

- For acute respiratory failure due to COVID-19, assign code U07.1 alongside code J96.0-.
