### 181. COVID-19 Diagnosis Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines and Implementation  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles describe the same feature regarding COVID-19 coding logic, specifically the criteria for assigning U07.1 versus symptom codes.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `91`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For patients presenting with any signs/symptoms associated with COVID-19 (such as fever, etc.) but a definitive diagnosis has not been established, assign the appropriate code(s) for each of the presenting signs and symptoms such as:
>
> - R05.1, Acute cough, or R05.9, Cough, unspecified
> - R06.02 Shortness of breath
> - R50.9 Fever, unspecified
>
> If a patient with signs/symptoms associated with COVID-19 also has an actual or suspected contact with or exposure to COVID-19, assign Z20.822, Contact with and (suspected) exposure to COVID-19, as an additional code.
>
> - (h) Asymptomatic individuals who test positive for COVID-19
>
> For asymptomatic individuals who test positive for COVID-19, see guideline I.C.1.g.1.a. Although the individual is asymptomatic, the individual has tested positive and is considered to have the COVID-19 infection.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Symptom and Exposure Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes for specific signs and symptoms (e.g., acute cough, shortness of breath, fever) when a definitive COVID-19 diagnosis is not established.
- Assign Z20.822 as an additional code for patients with signs/symptoms who have an actual or suspected contact with or exposure to COVID-19.
- Consider asymptomatic individuals who test positive for COVID-19 as having the infection and assign the appropriate code.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Definitive diagnosis must not be established to use symptom codes (R05.1, R05.9, R06.02, R50.9).
- Contact with or exposure to COVID-19 must be documented to assign Z20.822.
- Positive test result must be documented for asymptomatic individuals to confirm infection status.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `506_517`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code only a confirmed diagnosis of the 2019 novel coronavirus disease (COVID-19) as documented by the provider. For a confirmed diagnosis, assign code U07.1, COVID-19. This is an exception to the hospital inpatient guideline Section II, H. In this context, 'confirmation' does not require documentation of a positive test result for COVID-19; the provider's documentation that the individual has COVID-19 is sufficient.
>
> If the provider documents "suspected," "possible," "probable," or 'inconclusive' COVID-19, do not assign code U07.1. Instead, code the signs and symptoms reported. See guideline I.C.1.g.1.g.
>
> For patients presenting with any signs/symptoms associated with COVID-19 (such as fever, etc.) but a definitive diagnosis has not been established, assign the appropriate code(s) for each of the presenting signs and symptoms such as:
>
> - R05.1, Acute cough, or R05.9, Cough, unspecified
> - R06.02 Shortness of breath
> - R50.9 Fever, unspecified
>
> If a patient with signs/symptoms associated with COVID-19 also has an actual or suspected contact with or exposure to COVID-19, assign Z20.822, Contact with and (suspected) exposure to COVID-19, as an additional code.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Diagnosis Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code U07.1 only for confirmed diagnoses documented by the provider, without requiring a positive test result.
- Do not assign code U07.1 for suspected, possible, probable, or inconclusive diagnoses; instead, code signs and symptoms.
- Code specific signs and symptoms (e.g., acute cough, shortness of breath, fever) for patients without a definitive diagnosis.
- Assign additional code Z20.822 for patients with signs/symptoms who have actual or suspected contact with or exposure to COVID-19.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider must document a confirmed diagnosis of COVID-19 to assign code U07.1.
- Documentation of uncertainty (suspected, possible, probable, inconclusive) precludes assignment of U07.1.
- Code U07.1 is an exception to hospital inpatient guideline Section II, H.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Definition Revision   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version required a positive test result to confirm an asymptomatic infection or establish a definitive diagnosis for coding purposes. The newer version revises this definition by stating that a provider's documentation of a confirmed diagnosis is sufficient for code U07.1, explicitly removing the requirement for a positive test result. Conversely, the newer version clarifies that terms like 'suspected' or 'possible' strictly preclude the use of U07.1, mandating symptom codes instead, whereas the older version focused more on the positive test confirmation for asymptomatic cases.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Asymptomatic individuals must have a positive test result to be considered infected and assigned the appropriate code. -> October 2025 (New Version): A confirmed diagnosis is assigned based on provider documentation alone, without requiring a positive test result.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Did not explicitly list 'suspected' or 'possible' as exclusions for U07.1 in the main text. -> October 2025 (New Version): Explicitly states that 'suspected,' 'possible,' 'probable,' or 'inconclusive' diagnoses preclude assignment of U07.1 and require symptom coding.

*Traceability: Chunk `91` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `506_517` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 182. COVID-19 Follow-Up Evaluation Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines and Implementation  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 8/10  
*Both profiles address the same feature area (COVID-19 coding) but the older version includes specific follow-up evaluation logic while the newer version focuses exclusively on screening.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `90_92`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For screening for COVID-19, including preoperative testing, assign code Z11.52, Encounter for screening for COVID-19.
>
> - Personal history of COVID-19 For patients with a history of COVID-19, assign code Z86.16,
>
> Personal history of COVID-19.
>
> - (j) Follow-up visits after COVID-19 infection has resolved For individuals who previously had COVID-19, without residual symptom(s) or condition(s), and are being seen for follow-up evaluation, and COVID-19 test results are negative, assign codes Z09, Encounter for follow-up examination after completed treatment for conditions other than malignant neoplasm, and Z86.16, Personal history of COVID-19.
>
> For follow-up visits for individuals with symptom(s) or condition(s) related to a previous COVID-19 infection, see guideline I.C.1.g.1.m.
>
> See Section I.C.21.c.8, Factors influencing health states and contact with health services, Follow-up

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Follow-Up Evaluation Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code Z11.52 for screening for COVID-19, including preoperative testing.
- Assign code Z86.16 for patients with a history of COVID-19.
- Assign codes Z09 and Z86.16 for follow-up visits after COVID-19 infection has resolved with no residual symptoms and negative test results.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- For follow-up visits after resolution, the patient must have no residual symptoms or conditions.
- For follow-up visits after resolution, COVID-19 test results must be negative.
- For follow-up visits with residual symptoms or conditions, refer to guideline I.C.1.g.1.m.

**Key Behaviors:**

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The encounter must be for the purpose of screening for COVID-19.

---

The October 2025 version narrows the scope significantly, removing the detailed instructions for post-resolution follow-up evaluations and focusing solely on the assignment of Z11.52 for screening and preoperative testing. This represents a reduction in the specific behavioral rules for the 'Follow-Up Evaluation' aspect of the feature within the provided text.

**Key Differences:**

- October 1, 2024 (Older): Directs cases with related symptoms to specific guidelines for malignant neoplasm or other conditions. -> October 2025 (New Version): Omits any reference to symptom management or related condition guidelines.

### 183. Post-COVID-19 Condition Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines and Implementation  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Post-COVID-19 Condition Coding Guidelines with matching behavioral rules and text excerpts.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `86_95`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) due to COVID-19, assign codes U07.1, and J80, Acute respiratory distress syndrome.
>
> For sequela of COVID-19, or associated symptoms or conditions that develop following a previous COVID-19 infection, assign a code(s) for the specific symptom(s) or condition(s) related to the previous COVID-19 infection, if known, and code U09.9, Post COVID-19 condition, unspecified.
>
> Code U09.9 should not be assigned for manifestations of an active (current) COVID-19 infection.
>
> If a patient has a condition(s) associated with a previous COVID-19 infection and develops a new active (current) COVID-19 infection, code U09.9 may be assigned in conjunction with code U07.1, COVID-19, to identify that the patient also has a condition(s) associated with a previous COVID-19 infection. Code(s) for the specific condition(s) associated with the previous COVID-19 infection and code(s) for manifestation(s) of the new active (current) COVID-19 infection should also be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Post-COVID-19 Condition Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code U07.1 and J80 for acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) due to COVID-19.
- Assign specific symptom/condition codes plus U09.9 for sequela of COVID-19 or associated conditions developing after a previous infection.
- Do not assign U09.9 for manifestations of an active (current) COVID-19 infection.
- Assign U09.9 in conjunction with U07.1 if a patient has conditions from a previous infection and develops a new active infection.
- Assign codes for specific conditions associated with the previous infection and codes for manifestations of the new active infection when both are present.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be documented as sequela or associated symptoms developing following a previous COVID-19 infection.
- Specific symptom(s) or condition(s) related to the previous infection must be known.
- Active (current) COVID-19 infection must be ruled out for U09.9 assignment.
- Both previous infection-related conditions and new active infection must be documented to assign U09.9 with U07.1.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `523`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For sequela of COVID-19, or associated symptoms or conditions that develop following a previous COVID-19 infection, assign a code(s) for the specific symptom(s) or condition(s) related to the previous COVID-19 infection, if known, and code U09.9, Post COVID-19 condition, unspecified.
>
> Code U09.9 should not be assigned for manifestations of an active (current) COVID-19 infection.
>
> If a patient has a condition(s) associated with a previous COVID-19 infection and develops a new active (current) COVID-19 infection, code U09.9 may be assigned in conjunction with code U07.1, COVID-19, to identify that the patient also has a condition(s) associated with a previous COVID-19 infection. Code(s) for the specific condition(s) associated with the previous COVID-19 infection and code(s) for manifestation(s) of the new active (current) COVID-19 infection should also be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Post-COVID-19 Condition Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign specific symptom or condition codes related to a previous COVID-19 infection along with code U09.9 for unspecified post-COVID-19 condition.
- Do not assign code U09.9 for manifestations of an active (current) COVID-19 infection.
- When a patient has conditions from a previous infection and develops a new active infection, assign U09.9 in conjunction with U07.1 to identify both the sequela and the active infection.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The condition must be sequela of COVID-19 or associated symptoms/conditions developing following a previous infection.
- The specific symptom(s) or condition(s) related to the previous infection must be known.
- Code U09.9 must not be used if the manifestations are of an active (current) infection only.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions present the exact same coding logic for assigning U09.9 for sequelae and prohibiting its use for active infections. The raw text excerpts are identical in both versions, indicating no substantive update to the clinical criteria or coding requirements. The only variation is the inclusion of a separate rule for ARDS (U07.1 and J80) in the older version's taxonomy description, which is not present in the newer version's specific excerpt, but the core feature regarding post-COVID-19 conditions remains unchanged.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Includes specific instruction to assign U07.1 and J80 for ARDS due to COVID-19 in the taxonomy description -> October 2025 (New Version): Omits the ARDS-specific instruction in the provided excerpt, focusing solely on post-COVID-19 sequelae rules.

*Traceability: Chunk `86_95` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `523` (October 2025 (New Version))*

- Assign code J20.8 for acute bronchitis due to other specified organisms

- Distinguish between acute bronchitis and bronchitis NOS

> **Actual Text**

> Code Z23 is for encounters for inoculations and vaccinations. It indicates that a patient is being seen to receive a prophylactic inoculation against a disease. Procedure codes are required to identify the actual administration of the injection and the type(s) of immunizations given. Code Z23 may be used as a secondary code if the inoculation is given as a routine part of preventive health care, such as a well-baby visit.

**Feature:** ICD-10 Code Z23 Implementation

- Code Z23 is used for encounters for inoculations and vaccinations indicating a patient is being seen to receive a prophylactic inoculation against a disease.

- Code Z23 may be used as a secondary code if the inoculation is given as a routine part of preventive health care, such as a well-baby visit.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Procedure codes must be assigned to identify the actual administration of the injection.
- Procedure codes must specify the type(s) of immunizations given.
- Code Z23 is appropriate only when the encounter is for a prophylactic inoculation or vaccination.

### 190. MIS-C Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines and Implementation  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical MIS-C coding logic with only minor wording adjustments in the requirements section.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `94`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For individuals with multisystem inflammatory syndrome (MIS) and COVID-19, assign code U07.1, COVID-19, as the principal/first-listed diagnosis and assign code M35.81, Multisystem inflammatory syndrome, as an additional diagnosis.
>
> If an individual with a history of COVID-19 develops MIS, assign codes M35.81, Multisystem inflammatory syndrome, and U09.9, Post COVID-19 condition, unspecified.
>
> If an individual with a known or suspected exposure to COVID19, and no current COVID-19 infection or history of COVID-19, develops MIS, assign codes M35.81, Multisystem inflammatory syndrome, and Z20.822, Contact with and (suspected) exposure to COVID-19.
>
> Additional codes should be assigned for any associated complications of MIS.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** MIS-C Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code U07.1 as principal diagnosis for individuals with MIS and current COVID-19
- Assign code M35.81 as additional diagnosis for Multisystem inflammatory syndrome
- Assign code U09.9 for individuals with history of COVID-19 who develop MIS
- Assign code Z20.822 for individuals with suspected exposure to COVID-19 who develop MIS without current infection or history
- Assign additional codes for any associated complications of MIS

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Current COVID-19 infection must be present to assign U07.1 as principal diagnosis
- History of COVID-19 must be documented to assign U09.9
- Known or suspected exposure to COVID-19 without current infection or history must be documented to assign Z20.822
- MIS diagnosis must be confirmed or documented as suspected to assign M35.81
- Associated complications must be explicitly documented to assign additional codes

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `522`

> **Actual Text**

### 191. SIRS Coding Guidelines for Noninfectious Origins
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines and Implementation  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical SIRS coding logic for noninfectious origins with no substantive changes in behavior or requirements.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `279`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) can develop as a result of certain non-infectious disease processes, such as trauma, malignant neoplasm, or pancreatitis. When SIRS is documented with a noninfectious condition, and no subsequent infection is documented, the code for the underlying condition, such as an injury, should be assigned, followed by code R65.10, Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) of non-infectious origin without acute organ dysfunction, or code R65.11, Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) of non-infectious origin with acute organ dysfunction. If an associated acute organ dysfunction is documented, the appropriate code(s) for the specific type of organ dysfunction(s) should be assigned in addition to code R65.11. If acute organ dysfunction is documented, but it cannot be determined if the acute organ dysfunction is associated with SIRS or due to another condition (e.g., directly due to the trauma), the provider should be queried.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** SIRS Coding for Noninfectious Origins

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code for underlying noninfectious condition followed by R65.10 or R65.11 when SIRS is documented with no subsequent infection.
- Assign specific organ dysfunction codes in addition to R65.11 if acute organ dysfunction is documented.
- Query the provider if acute organ dysfunction cannot be determined to be associated with SIRS or due to another condition.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- SIRS must be documented with a noninfectious condition (e.g., trauma, malignant neoplasm, pancreatitis).
- No subsequent infection must be documented to use R65.10 or R65.11.
- Acute organ dysfunction must be explicitly documented to assign R65.11 and additional organ codes.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `706`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) can develop as a result of certain non-infectious disease processes, such as trauma, malignant neoplasm, or pancreatitis. When SIRS is documented with a noninfectious condition, and no subsequent infection is documented, the code for the underlying condition, such as an injury, should be assigned, followed by code R65.10, Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) of non-infectious origin without acute organ dysfunction, or code R65.11, Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) of non-infectious origin with acute organ dysfunction. If an associated acute organ dysfunction is documented, the appropriate code(s) for the specific type of organ dysfunction(s) should be assigned in addition to code R65.11. If acute organ dysfunction is documented, but it cannot be determined if the acute organ dysfunction is associated with SIRS or due to another condition (e.g., directly due to the trauma), the provider should be queried.

- Assign specific acute organ dysfunction codes in addition to R65.11 if acute organ dysfunction is documented alongside SIRS of noninfectious origin.
- Query the provider if acute organ dysfunction is documented but its association with SIRS versus another condition cannot be determined.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- SIRS must be documented with a noninfectious condition (e.g., trauma, malignant neoplasm, pancreatitis).

- Acute organ dysfunction must be explicitly documented to assign R65.11 and additional dysfunction codes.

>
> If COVID-19 is confirmed, see guideline I.C.1.g.1.a.

**Feature:** COVID-19 Exposure Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code Z20.822 for asymptomatic individuals with actual or suspected exposure to COVID-19.
- Assign code Z20.822 for symptomatic individuals with actual or suspected exposure where infection is ruled out or test results are inconclusive/unknown.
- Refer to guideline I.C.21.c.1 for additional guidance on category Z20 codes.
- Refer to guideline I.C.1.g.1.a if COVID-19 is confirmed.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be asymptomatic with actual or suspected exposure.
- Condition must be symptomatic with actual or suspected exposure AND infection ruled out OR test results inconclusive/unknown.
- Confirmation of COVID-19 infection requires different coding per guideline I.C.1.g.1.a.

- Vaccination status must align with CDC recommendations current at the time of the encounter.

### 195. COVID-19 Inpatient Coding Exception
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines and Implementation  
**LLM Relevance:** 🔴 4/10 — possibly mismatched pair  
*Both profiles reference COVID-19 coding but describe distinct features: the older version covers the exception for active confirmed diagnoses, while the newer version covers the requirement for history codes.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `80`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code only a confirmed diagnosis of the 2019 novel coronavirus disease (COVID-19) as documented by the provider, or documentation of a positive COVID-19 test result. For a confirmed diagnosis, assign code U07.1, COVID-19. This is an exception to the hospital inpatient guideline Section II, H. In this context, 'confirmation' does not require documentation of a positive test result for COVID-19; the provider's documentation that the individual has COVID-19 is sufficient.
>
> If the provider documents "suspected," "possible," "probable," or 'inconclusive' COVID-19, do not assign code U07.1. Instead, code the signs and symptoms reported. See guideline I.C.1.g.1.g.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Inpatient Coding Exception

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code U07.1 for confirmed COVID-19 diagnoses based on provider documentation alone, without requiring a positive test result.
- Do not assign code U07.1 if the provider documents 'suspected', 'possible', 'probable', or 'inconclusive' COVID-19.
- Code signs and symptoms instead of U07.1 for uncertain COVID-19 diagnoses per guideline I.C.1.g.1.g.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider must document a confirmed diagnosis of COVID-19.
- Provider documentation of a positive COVID-19 test result is sufficient but not strictly required for confirmation.
- Uncertain diagnoses (suspected, possible, probable, inconclusive) must be coded as signs and symptoms.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `519`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For patients with a history of COVID-19, assign code Z86.16, Personal history of COVID-19.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** COVID-19 Inpatient Coding Exception

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code Z86.16 for patients with a history of COVID-19

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Patient must have a documented history of COVID-19

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version (October 1, 2024) defines an exception allowing code U07.1 for active confirmed COVID-19 diagnoses based on provider documentation alone, without a positive test. The newer version (October 2025) introduces a separate requirement to assign code Z86.16 for patients with a documented history of the disease. These are two different coding rules for different clinical states (active vs. history) rather than a modification of the same rule.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Establishes an exception to allow code U07.1 for confirmed active COVID-19 diagnoses based on provider documentation without a positive test result. -> October 2025 (New Version): Establishes a requirement to assign code Z86.16 for patients with a documented history of COVID-19.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicitly prohibits using U07.1 for 'suspected', 'possible', 'probable', or 'inconclusive' diagnoses, directing coders to use signs and symptoms. -> October 2025 (New Version): Does not address the coding of uncertain active diagnoses, focusing solely on the history code assignment.

*Traceability: Chunk `80` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `519` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 196. Bone Site Designation Rules
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → Anatomic Site Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding rule for designating bone sites over joints for specific conditions like avascular necrosis and osteoporosis.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `205`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For certain conditions, the bone may be affected at the upper or lower end, (e.g., avascular necrosis of bone, M87, Osteoporosis, M80, M81). Though the portion of the bone affected may be at the joint, the site designation will be the bone, not the joint.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Bone Site Designation Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Conditions affecting the upper or lower end of a bone must be coded to the bone site, not the joint.
- Even if the affected portion is at the joint, the site designation remains the bone.
- Examples include avascular necrosis (M87), Osteoporosis (M80), and Osteoporosis (M81).

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The condition must be documented as affecting the bone (e.g., avascular necrosis, osteoporosis).
- The specific location (upper or lower end) must be identifiable or implied by the condition type.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `633`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For certain conditions, the bone may be affected at the upper or lower end, (e.g., avascular necrosis of bone, M87, Osteoporosis, M80, M81).
> Though the portion of the bone affected may be at the joint, the site designation will be the bone, not the joint.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Bone Site Designation Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- For conditions affecting the upper or lower end of a bone (e.g., avascular necrosis, osteoporosis), the bone site must be designated.
- If the affected portion is at the joint, the site designation must be the bone, not the joint.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must affect the upper or lower end of the bone.
- Condition must be documented as affecting the bone rather than the joint.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 guideline explicitly states that conditions affecting the upper or lower end of a bone must be coded to the bone site, not the joint, even if the portion is at the joint. The October 2025 version contains the exact same logic and wording, reiterating that the site designation remains the bone regardless of joint involvement. There are no substantive differences in the coding logic, examples, or requirements between the two versions.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Conditions affecting the upper or lower end of a bone must be coded to the bone site, not the joint. -> October 2025 (New Version): Conditions affecting the upper or lower end of a bone must be coded to the bone site, not the joint.

*Traceability: Chunk `205` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `633` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 197. Chapter 13 Site and Laterality Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → Anatomic Site Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding Chapter 13 site and laterality coding rules with no substantive behavioral changes.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `204`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Most of the codes within Chapter 13 have site and laterality designations. The site represents the bone, joint or the muscle involved. For some conditions where more than one bone, joint or muscle is usually involved, such as osteoarthritis, there is a 'multiple sites' code available. For categories where no multiple site code is provided and more than one bone, joint or muscle is involved, multiple codes should be used to indicate the different sites involved.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Chapter 13 Site and Laterality Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes within Chapter 13 generally include site and laterality designations.
- The site designation represents the specific bone, joint, or muscle involved.
- A 'multiple sites' code is used when more than one bone, joint, or muscle is usually involved (e.g., osteoarthritis).
- Multiple codes must be used to indicate different sites involved when no multiple site code is provided for a category.
- Coding must distinguish between the bone, joint, or muscle component of the condition.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The diagnosis must specify the bone, joint, or muscle involved to assign the correct site code.
- If a condition typically involves multiple sites and a multiple site code exists, that single code must be used instead of multiple codes.
- If a condition involves multiple sites and no multiple site code is available, multiple codes must be assigned to cover all involved sites.
- Laterality designations must be applied where available in the code set to distinguish left, right, or bilateral involvement.
- Site specificity is required for accurate classification within Chapter 13 categories.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `632`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Most of the codes within Chapter 13 have site and laterality designations. The site represents the bone, joint or the muscle involved.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Chapter 13 Site and Laterality Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Codes within Chapter 13 include site and laterality designations
- Site designations identify the specific bone, joint, or muscle involved

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Code must specify the anatomical site (bone, joint, or muscle)
- Laterality must be assigned if designated in the code set

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same logic for assigning site and laterality codes in Chapter 13. Both versions mandate that codes represent specific bones, joints, or muscles and provide identical instructions for handling multiple sites versus single multiple-site codes. The only differences are minor phrasing adjustments in the requirements section, such as changing 'must specify' to 'Code must specify', without altering the underlying coding logic.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): The diagnosis must specify the bone, joint, or muscle involved to assign the correct site code. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code must specify the anatomical site (bone, joint, or muscle)
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Multiple codes must be used to indicate different sites involved when no multiple site code is provided. -> October 2025 (New Version): Laterality must be assigned if designated in the code set

*Traceability: Chunk `204` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `632` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 199. ICD-10-CM Bilateral Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → Edge Cases & Standalone Topics  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical ICD-10-CM Bilateral Coding Guidelines feature with identical behavioral rules and text excerpts.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `40_126`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Some ICD-10-CM codes indicate laterality, specifying whether the condition occurs on the left, right or is bilateral. If no bilateral code is provided and the condition is bilateral, assign separate codes for both the left and right side. If the side is not identified in the medical record, assign the code for the unspecified side.
>
> When a patient has a bilateral condition and each side is treated during separate encounters, assign the "bilateral" code (as the condition still exists on both sides), including for the encounter to treat the first side. For the second encounter for treatment after one side has previously been treated and the condition no longer exists on that side, assign the appropriate unilateral code for the side where the condition still exists (e.g., cataract surgery performed on each eye in separate encounters). The bilateral code would not be assigned for the subsequent encounter, as the patient no longer has the condition in the previously-treated site. If the treatment on the first side did not completely resolve the condition, then the bilateral code would still be appropriate.
>
> When laterality is not documented by the patient's provider, code assignment for the affected side may be based on medical record documentation from other clinicians. If there is conflicting medical record documentation regarding the affected side, the patient's provider should be queried for clarification. Codes for 'unspecified' side should rarely be used, such as when the documentation in the record is insufficient to determine the affected side and it is not possible to obtain clarification.
>
> Breast implant associated anaplastic large cell lymphoma (BIA-ALCL) is a type of lymphoma that can develop around breast implants. Assign code C84.7A, Anaplastic large cell lymphoma, ALK-negative, breast, for BIA-ALCL or C84.7B, Anaplastic large cell lymphoma, ALK-negative, in remission, for BIA-ALCL in remission . Do not assign a complication code from chapter 19.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Bilateral Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign separate left and right codes if no bilateral code exists and the condition is bilateral.
- Assign unspecified side codes only when documentation is insufficient and clarification is impossible.
- Use bilateral codes for encounters treating the first side of a bilateral condition, even if the second side was treated separately.
- Switch to unilateral codes for subsequent encounters if the condition no longer exists on the previously treated side.
- Do not assign complication codes from Chapter 19 for Breast implant associated anaplastic large cell lymphoma (BIA-ALCL).

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Bilateral codes must be assigned only when the condition exists on both sides at the time of the encounter.
- Unilateral codes are required for subsequent encounters where the condition has resolved on one side.
- Provider query is mandatory when medical records contain conflicting documentation regarding the affected side.
- Unspecified side codes should be used rarely, strictly when documentation is insufficient and clarification cannot be obtained.
- For BIA-ALCL, assign specific lymphoma codes (C84.7A or C84.7B) instead of complication codes from Chapter 19.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `472_553`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Some ICD-10-CM codes indicate laterality, specifying whether the condition occurs on the left, right or is bilateral. If no bilateral code is provided and the condition is bilateral, assign separate codes for both the left and right side. If the side is not identified in the medical record, assign the code for the unspecified side.
>
> When a patient has a bilateral condition and each side is treated during separate encounters, assign the "bilateral" code (as the condition still exists on both sides), including for the encounter to treat the first side. For the second encounter for treatment after one side has previously been treated and the condition no longer exists on that side, assign the appropriate unilateral code for the side where the condition still exists (e.g., cataract surgery performed on each eye in separate encounters). The bilateral code would not be assigned for the subsequent encounter, as the patient no longer has the condition in the previously-treated site. If the treatment on the first side did not completely resolve the condition, then the bilateral code would still be appropriate.
>
> When laterality is not documented by the patient's provider, code assignment for the affected side may be based on medical record documentation from other clinicians. If there is conflicting medical record documentation regarding the affected side, the patient's provider should be queried for clarification. Codes for 'unspecified' side should rarely be used, such as when the documentation in the record is insufficient to determine the affected side and it is not possible to obtain clarification.
>
> Breast implant associated anaplastic large cell lymphoma (BIA-ALCL) is a type of lymphoma that can develop around breast implants. Assign code C84.7A, Anaplastic large cell lymphoma, ALK-negative, breast, for BIA-ALCL or C84.7B, Anaplastic large cell lymphoma, ALK-negative, in remission, for BIAALCL in remission. Do not assign a complication code from chapter 19.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10-CM Bilateral Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign separate left and right codes if no bilateral code exists and the condition is bilateral.
- Assign unspecified side codes only when documentation is insufficient and clarification is impossible.
- Use bilateral codes for separate encounters treating both sides if the condition persists on both sides.
- Switch to unilateral codes for subsequent encounters if the condition no longer exists on the previously treated side.
- Do not assign complication codes from chapter 19 for Breast implant associated anaplastic large cell lymphoma (BIA-ALCL).

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Medical record must explicitly identify the side or indicate bilateral involvement.
- Provider must be queried for clarification if conflicting documentation exists regarding the affected side.
- Bilateral code assignment requires the condition to still exist on both sides at the time of the encounter.
- Unilateral codes are required for subsequent encounters where the condition has resolved on one side.
- BIA-ALCL must be coded using specific lymphoma codes (C84.7A or C84.7B) rather than complication codes.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 versions contain identical text excerpts and behavioral definitions for assigning bilateral versus unilateral codes. The guidelines regarding BIA-ALCL coding (C84.7A/C84.7B) and the prohibition of Chapter 19 complication codes are unchanged between the two versions. The only variation is a minor capitalization difference in the phrase 'chapter 19' versus 'Chapter 19', which does not alter the coding logic.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Prohibits complication codes from Chapter 19 for BIA-ALCL -> October 2025 (New Version): Prohibits complication codes from chapter 19 for BIA-ALCL (minor capitalization change only)
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Requires bilateral codes if condition exists on both sides -> October 2025 (New Version): Requires bilateral codes if condition exists on both sides (identical logic)

*Traceability: Chunk `40_126` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `472_553` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 200. ICD-10 Code Assignment for MSSA/MRSA Colonization
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Compliance → Edge Cases & Standalone Topics  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding ICD-10 code assignment for MSSA/MRSA colonization with matching definitions and code mappings.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `77`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The condition or state of being colonized or carrying MSSA or MRSA is called colonization or carriage, while an individual person is described as being colonized or being a carrier.
>
> Colonization means that MSSA or MSRA is present on or in the body without necessarily causing illness. A positive MRSA colonization test might be documented by the provider as 'MRSA screen positive' or 'MRSA nasal swab positive'.
>
> Assign code Z22.322, Carrier or suspected carrier of Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus, for patients documented as having MRSA colonization. Assign code Z22.321, Carrier or suspected carrier of Methicillin susceptible Staphylococcus aureus, for patients documented as having MSSA colonization. Colonization is not necessarily indicative of a disease process or as the cause of a specific condition the patient may have unless documented as such by the provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Code Assignment for MSSA/MRSA Colonization

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code Z22.322 for patients documented as having MRSA colonization.
- Assign code Z22.321 for patients documented as having MSSA colonization.
- Recognize colonization as the presence of bacteria without necessarily causing illness or a specific disease process.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must explicitly state the patient is a carrier or suspected carrier of Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus.
- Documentation must explicitly state the patient is a carrier or suspected carrier of Methicillin susceptible Staphylococcus aureus.
- Provider must document colonization status (e.g., 'MRSA screen positive' or 'MRSA nasal swab positive') to support code assignment.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `503`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The condition or state of being colonized or carrying MSSA or MRSA is called colonization or carriage, while an individual person is described as being colonized or being a carrier.
>
> Colonization means that MSSA or MSRA is present on or in the body without necessarily causing illness. A positive MRSA colonization test might be documented by the provider as 'MRSA screen positive' or 'MRSA nasal swab positive'.
>
> Assign code Z22.322, Carrier or suspected carrier of Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus, for patients documented as having MRSA colonization. Assign code Z22.321, Carrier or suspected carrier of Methicillin susceptible Staphylococcus aureus, for patients documented as having MSSA colonization. Colonization is not necessarily indicative of a disease process or as the cause of a specific condition the patient may have unless documented as such by the provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** ICD-10 Code Assignment for MSSA/MRSA Colonization

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code Z22.322 for patients documented as having MRSA colonization.
- Assign code Z22.321 for patients documented as having MSSA colonization.
- Recognize colonization as the presence of bacteria without necessarily causing illness.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must explicitly state the patient is a carrier or suspected carrier of the specific organism.
- Provider must document the condition as colonization or carriage rather than an active disease process unless specified otherwise.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older and newer guidelines present identical logic for assigning codes Z22.322 and Z22.321 based on explicit documentation of carrier status. The core requirement that colonization represents the presence of bacteria without necessarily causing illness remains unchanged in both versions. While the newer version slightly condenses the 'Requirements' section by removing the specific examples of 'Methicillin susceptible' in the requirement list, the behavioral instruction and clinical criteria are functionally identical.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Requirements explicitly listed 'Methicillin susceptible' and 'Methicillin resistant' as separate documentation examples -> October 2025 (New Version): Requirements generalized to 'the specific organism' while retaining the same code assignment logic.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Behaviors listed three distinct points including 'Recognize colonization as the presence of bacteria without necessarily causing illness or a specific disease process' -> October 2025 (New Version): Behaviors listed three points with the third point shortened to 'Recognize colonization as the presence of bacteria without necessarily causing illness'.

*Traceability: Chunk `77` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `503` (October 2025 (New Version))*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `277`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code R40.20, Unspecified coma, should be assigned when the underlying cause of the coma is not known, or the cause is a traumatic brain injury and the coma scale is not documented in the medical record.
>
> Do not report codes for unspecified coma, individual or total Glasgow coma scale scores for a patient with a medically induced coma or a sedated patient.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Unspecified Coma Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code R40.20 when the underlying cause of the coma is not known.
- Assign code R40.20 when the cause is a traumatic brain injury and the coma scale is not documented.
- Do not report unspecified coma codes for patients with medically induced coma.
- Do not report unspecified coma codes for sedated patients.
- Do not report individual or total Glasgow coma scale scores for patients with medically induced coma or sedated patients.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Underlying cause of coma must be unknown to assign R40.20.
- Coma scale must be documented if the cause is a traumatic brain injury to avoid assigning R40.20.
- Patient must not be in a medically induced coma or sedated state to assign R40.20.

### 203. Alphabetic Index Instruction Logic

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `24`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The 'see' instruction following a main term in the Alphabetic Index indicates that another term should be referenced. It is necessary to go to the main term referenced with the 'see' note to locate the correct code.
>
> A 'see also' instruction following a main term in the Alphabetic Index instructs that there is another main term that may also be referenced that may provide additional Alphabetic Index entries that may be useful. It is not necessary to follow the 'see also' note when the original main term provides the necessary code.

**Extracted Profile:**

- 'see also' instruction indicates an additional main term may provide useful entries but is not mandatory if the original term provides the necessary code.

- When a 'see also' instruction is present, the coder may skip it if the original main term yields the required code.

### 208. External Cause Code Reporting Logic
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Reporting Standards → External Cause Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical logic for assigning 7th characters and prioritizing external cause codes based on the provided text excerpts.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `319_321_329_330`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assign the external cause code, with the appropriate 7 th character (initial encounter, subsequent encounter or sequela) for each encounter for which the injury or condition is being treated.
>
> Most categories in chapter 20 have a 7 th character requirement for each applicable code. Most categories in this chapter have three 7 th character values: A, initial encounter, D, subsequent encounter and S, sequela. While the patient may be seen by a new or different provider over the course of treatment for an injury or condition, assignment of the 7 th character for external cause should match the 7 th character of the code assigned for the associated injury or condition for the encounter.
>
> Assign as many external cause codes as necessary to fully explain each cause. If only one external code can be recorded, assign the code most related to the principal diagnosis.
>
> If the reporting format limits the number of external cause codes that can be used in reporting clinical data, report the code for the cause/intent most related to the principal diagnosis. If the format permits capture of additional external cause codes, the cause/intent, including medical misadventures, of the additional events should be reported rather than the codes for place, activity, or external status.
>
> More than one external cause code is required to fully describe the external cause of an illness or injury. The assignment of external cause codes should be sequenced in the following priority:
>
> If two or more events cause separate injuries, an external cause code should be assigned for each cause. The first-listed external cause code will be selected in the following order:
>
> External codes for child and adult abuse take priority over all other external cause codes .
>
> See Section I.C.19., Child and Adult abuse guidelines.
>
> External cause codes for terrorism events take priority over all other external cause codes except child and adult abuse.
>
> External cause codes for cataclysmic events take priority over all other external cause codes except child and adult abuse and terrorism.
>
> External cause codes for transport accidents take priority over all other external cause codes except cataclysmic events, child and adult abuse and terrorism.
>
> Activity and external cause status codes are assigned following all causal (intent) external cause codes.
>
> The first-listed external cause code should correspond to the cause of the most serious diagnosis due to an assault, accident, or self-harm, following the order of hierarchy listed above.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** External Cause Code Reporting Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign the external cause code with the appropriate 7th character (initial, subsequent, or sequela) for each encounter where the injury or condition is treated.
- The 7th character for the external cause code must match the 7th character of the code assigned for the associated injury or condition for the encounter.
- Assign as many external cause codes as necessary to fully explain each cause, prioritizing cause/intent codes over place, activity, or external status codes.
- When multiple events cause separate injuries, assign an external cause code for each cause, sequencing them by priority: abuse, terrorism, cataclysmic events, transport accidents, then activity/status.
- The first-listed external cause code must correspond to the cause of the most serious diagnosis due to assault, accident, or self-harm, following the established hierarchy.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The 7th character assignment must align with the associated injury or condition code for the specific encounter.
- If reporting format limits the number of codes, the code for the cause/intent most related to the principal diagnosis must be reported.
- External cause codes must be sequenced according to the priority hierarchy: abuse > terrorism > cataclysmic > transport accidents > activity/status.
- The first-listed external cause code must reflect the cause of the most serious diagnosis.
- Additional external cause codes should report cause/intent (including medical misadventures) rather than place, activity, or external status if permitted.
- If two or more events cause separate injuries, an external cause code must be assigned for each cause.
- The first-listed external cause code must be selected based on a specific hierarchy: child/adult abuse > terrorism > cataclysmic events > transport accidents > activity/status.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `747_749_757`

### 209. External Cause Code Application
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Reporting Standards → External Cause Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the application of external cause codes with the exact same behavioral rules and text excerpts.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `318_320`

> **Actual Text**
>
> An external cause code may be used with any code in the range of A00.0-T88.9, Z00-Z99, classification that represents a health condition due to an external cause. Though they are most applicable to injuries, they are also valid for use with such things as infections or diseases due to an external source, and other health conditions, such as a heart attack that occurs during strenuous physical activity.
>
> Use the full range of external cause codes to completely describe the cause, the intent, the place of occurrence, and if applicable, the activity of the patient at the time of the event, and the patient's status, for all injuries, and other health conditions due to an external cause.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** External Cause Code Application

**Key Behaviors:**
- External cause codes may be used with any code in the range of A00.0-T88.9, Z00-Z99 representing a health condition due to an external cause.
- Codes are valid for injuries, infections, diseases due to external sources, and other conditions like heart attacks during strenuous activity.
- Use the full range of codes to completely describe the cause, intent, place of occurrence, activity, and patient status.
- Application is required for all injuries and other health conditions due to an external cause.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The primary diagnosis code must represent a health condition due to an external cause.
- Documentation must support the cause, intent, place of occurrence, activity, and patient status.

### 210. External Cause Code Classification Logic
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Reporting Standards → External Cause Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical logic for assigning 'Y' or 'N' to external cause codes based on timing relative to inpatient admission.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `322_432`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The selection of the appropriate external cause code is guided by the Alphabetic Index of External Causes and by Inclusion and Exclusion notes in the Tabular List.
>
> Assign 'Y' for any external cause code representing an external cause of morbidity that occurred prior to inpatient admission (e.g., patient fell out of bed at home, patient fell out of bed in emergency room prior to admission)
>
> Assign 'N' for any external cause code representing an external cause of morbidity that occurred during inpatient hospitalization (e.g., patient fell out of hospital bed during hospital stay, patient experienced an adverse reaction to a medication administered after inpatient admission).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** External Cause Code Classification Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Selection of appropriate external cause code is guided by the Alphabetic Index of External Causes and Inclusion/Exclusion notes in the Tabular List.
- Assign 'Y' for external cause codes representing morbidity occurring prior to inpatient admission.
- Assign 'N' for external cause codes representing morbidity occurring during inpatient hospitalization.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- External cause must be linked to a specific morbidity event.
- Timing of the event relative to inpatient admission must be determined to assign 'Y' or 'N'.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `750_865`

### 211. External Cause Code Sequencing Logic
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Reporting Standards → External Cause Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding External Cause Code Sequencing Logic with consistent behavioral rules across versions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `324_328`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Certain of the external cause codes are combination codes that identify sequential events that result in an injury, such as a fall which results in striking against an object. The injury may be due to either event or both. The combination external cause code used should correspond to the sequence of events regardless of which caused the most serious injury.
>
> When applicable, place of occurrence , activity, and external cause status codes are sequenced after the main external cause code(s). Regardless of the number of external cause codes assigned, generally there should be only one place of occurrence code, one activity code, and one external cause status code assigned to an encounter. However, in the rare instance that a new injury occurs during hospitalization, an additional place of occurrence code may be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** External Cause Code Sequencing Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Combination codes for sequential events must correspond to the sequence of events regardless of which event caused the most serious injury.
- Place of occurrence, activity, and external cause status codes are sequenced after the main external cause code(s).
- Generally, only one place of occurrence code, one activity code, and one external cause status code are assigned per encounter.
- An additional place of occurrence code may be assigned if a new injury occurs during hospitalization.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The combination external cause code must reflect the chronological sequence of events resulting in the injury.
- The assignment of place of occurrence, activity, and external cause status codes is conditional on their applicability to the encounter.

---

### 213. External Cause Code Reporting Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Reporting Standards → External Cause Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding the sequencing and mandatory reporting status of external cause codes with verbatim identical text.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `317`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The external causes of morbidity codes should never be sequenced as the first-listed or principal diagnosis.
>
> External cause codes are intended to provide data for injury research and evaluation of injury prevention strategies. These codes capture how the injury or health condition happened (cause), the intent (unintentional or accidental; or intentional, such as suicide or assault), the place where the event occurred the activity of the patient at the time of the event, and the person's status (e.g., civilian, military).
>
> There is no national requirement for mandatory ICD-10-CM external cause code reporting. Unless a provider is subject to a state-based external cause code reporting mandate or these codes are required by a particular payer, reporting of ICD-10-CM codes in Chapter 20, External Causes of Morbidity, is not required. In the absence of a mandatory reporting requirement, providers are encouraged to voluntarily report external cause codes, as they provide valuable data for injury research and evaluation of injury prevention strategies.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** External Cause Code Reporting Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- External cause codes should never be sequenced as the first-listed or principal diagnosis.
- External cause codes capture the cause, intent, place, activity, and status of the patient regarding an injury or health condition.
- Reporting of ICD-10-CM external cause codes is not nationally mandatory unless required by state mandate or specific payer.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- External cause codes must not be assigned as the principal diagnosis.
- External cause codes are only mandatory if a state-based mandate or specific payer requirement exists.

---

### 217. Y38 Terrorism Secondary Effects Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Reporting Standards → Terrorism Injury Coding  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles describe the Y38 Terrorism Secondary Effects Coding feature, with the newer version adding specific FBI identification requirements and sequencing rules.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `337`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Assign code Y38.9, Terrorism, secondary effects, for conditions occurring subsequent to the terrorist event. This code should not be assigned for conditions that are due to the initial terrorist act.
>
> It is acceptable to assign code Y38.9 with another code from Y38 if there is an injury due to the initial terrorist event and an injury that is a subsequent result of the terrorist event.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Y38 Terrorism Secondary Effects Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code Y38.9 for conditions occurring subsequent to the terrorist event.
- Do not assign Y38.9 for conditions directly due to the initial terrorist act.
- Combine Y38.9 with another Y38 code if an initial injury and a subsequent result both exist.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be documented as occurring subsequent to the terrorist event.
- Condition must not be directly caused by the initial terrorist act.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `765_767`

> **Actual Text**
>
> - 1) Cause of injury identified by the Federal Government (FBI) as terrorism
>
> When the cause of an injury is identified by the Federal Government (FBI) as terrorism, the first-listed external cause code should be a code from category Y38, Terrorism. The definition of terrorism employed by the FBI is found at the inclusion note at the beginning of category Y38. Use additional code for place of occurrence (Y92.-). More than one Y38 code may be assigned if the injury is the result of more than one mechanism of terrorism.
>
> Assign code Y38.9, Terrorism, secondary effects, for conditions occurring subsequent to the terrorist event. This code should not be assigned for conditions that are due to the initial terrorist act.
>
> It is acceptable to assign code Y38.9 with another code from Y38 if there is an injury due to the initial terrorist event and an injury that is a subsequent result of the terrorist event.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Y38 Terrorism Secondary Effects Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign first-listed external cause code from category Y38 when the Federal Government (FBI) identifies the cause of injury as terrorism.
- Use additional code Y92.- for place of occurrence.
- Assign multiple Y38 codes if the injury results from more than one mechanism of terrorism.
- Assign code Y38.9 for conditions occurring subsequent to the terrorist event.
- Do not assign Y38.9 for conditions due to the initial terrorist act.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Cause of injury must be identified by the Federal Government (FBI) as terrorism.
- Code Y38.9 must not be used for conditions resulting from the initial terrorist act.
- Code Y38.9 may be assigned with another Y38 code if there is an injury from the initial event and a subsequent result.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Rule Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version provided general instructions to assign Y38.9 for subsequent effects while excluding initial acts, without specifying the authority for the terrorism classification. The newer version clarifies that the cause must be explicitly identified by the Federal Government (FBI) as terrorism to trigger the use of category Y38 codes. Additionally, the new version introduces specific sequencing rules, mandating Y38 as the first-listed code and requiring an additional Y92.- code for the place of occurrence, which were not detailed in the older text.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): General instruction to assign Y38.9 for subsequent effects without specifying the authority for classification -> October 2025 (New Version): Explicit requirement that the cause of injury must be identified by the Federal Government (FBI) as terrorism.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): No mention of sequencing or place of occurrence codes -> October 2025 (New Version): Mandates Y38 as the first-listed external cause and requires an additional code Y92.- for place of occurrence.

*Traceability: Chunk `337` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `765_767` (October 2025 (New Version))*

- October 1, 2024 (Older): For confirmed cases where the perpetrator is known, a code from Y07 should accompany any other assault codes. -> October 2025 (New Version): For confirmed cases where the perpetrator is known, a code from Y07 should accompany any other assault codes.

*Traceability: Chunk `331` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `759` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 223. Y99 External Cause Status Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Reporting Standards → Edge Cases & Standalone Topics  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Y99 External Cause Status Coding rules with no substantive changes in logic or requirements between versions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `338`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A code from category Y99, External cause status, should be assigned whenever any other external cause code is assigned for an encounter, including an Activity code, except for the events noted below. Assign a code from category Y99, External cause status, to indicate the work status of the person at the time the event occurred. The status code indicates whether the event occurred during military activity, whether a non-military person was at work, whether an individual including a student or volunteer was involved in a non-work activity at the time of the causal event.
>
> A code from Y99, External cause status, should be assigned, when applicable, with other external cause codes, such as transport accidents and falls. The external cause status codes are not applicable to poisonings, adverse effects, misadventures or late effects.
>
> Do not assign a code from category Y99 if no other external cause codes (cause, activity) are applicable for the encounter.
>
> An external cause status code is used only once, at the initial encounter for treatment. Only one code from Y99 should be recorded on a medical record.
> Do not assign code Y99.9, Unspecified external cause status, if the status is not stated.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Y99 External Cause Status Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign a Y99 code whenever any other external cause code is assigned for an encounter, including activity codes.
- Use Y99 codes to indicate work status (military activity, non-military person at work, or non-work activity) at the time of the event.
- Combine Y99 status codes with other external cause codes such as transport accidents and falls.
- Do not assign Y99 codes if no other external cause codes (cause or activity) are applicable for the encounter.
- Record only one Y99 code per medical record, used exclusively at the initial encounter for treatment.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Other external cause codes (cause or activity) must be applicable for the encounter.
- The status must be stated; do not assign Y99.9 if the status is not stated.
- The code is only applicable to events other than poisonings, adverse effects, misadventures, or late effects.
- The code must be assigned at the initial encounter for treatment.
- Only one Y99 code should be recorded on the medical record.

---

The status code indicates whether the event occurred during military activity, whether a non-military person was at work, whether an individual including a student or volunteer was involved in a non-work activity at the time of the causal event.

>

- Combine Y99 status codes with other external cause codes such as transport accidents and falls when applicable.
- Restrict Y99 usage to the initial encounter for treatment, recording only one code per medical record.
- Exclude Y99 codes for poisonings, adverse effects, misadventures, or late effects.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**

Both versions strictly mandate recording only one Y99 code per medical record at the initial encounter for treatment.

### 224. Hurricane-Related External Cause Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding & Reporting Standards → Edge Cases & Standalone Topics  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature regarding Hurricane-Related External Cause Coding with identical core logic and specific code assignments.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `46_47`

> **Actual Text**
>
> An external cause of morbidity code should be assigned to identify the cause of the injury(ies) incurred as a result of the hurricane. The use of external cause of morbidity codes is supplemental to the application of ICD-10-CM codes. External cause of morbidity codes are never to be recorded as a principal diagnosis (firstlisted in non-inpatient settings). The appropriate injury code should be sequenced before any external cause codes. The external cause of morbidity codes capture how the injury or health condition happened (cause), the intent (unintentional or accidental; or intentional, such as suicide or assault), the place where the event occurred, the activity of the patient at the time of the event, and the person's status
>
> (e.g., civilian, military). They should not be assigned for encounters to treat hurricane victims' medical conditions when no injury, adverse effect or poisoning is involved. External cause of morbidity codes should be assigned for each encounter for care and treatment of the injury. External cause of morbidity codes may be assigned in all health care settings. For the purpose of capturing complete and accurate ICD-10-CM data in the aftermath of the hurricane, a healthcare setting should be considered as any location where medical care is provided by licensed healthcare professionals.
>
> Codes for cataclysmic events, such as a hurricane, take priority over all other external cause codes except child and adult abuse and terrorism and should be sequenced before other external cause of injury codes. Assign as many external cause of morbidity codes as necessary to fully explain each cause. For example, if an injury occurs as a result of a building collapse during the hurricane, external cause codes for both the hurricane and the building collapse should be assigned, with the external causes code for hurricane being sequenced as the first external cause code. For injuries incurred as a direct result of the hurricane, assign the appropriate code(s) for the injuries, followed by the code X37.0-, Hurricane (with the appropriate 7th character), and any other applicable external cause of injury codes. Code X37.0- also should be assigned when an injury is incurred as a result of flooding caused by a levee breaking related to the hurricane. Code X38.-, Flood (with the appropriate 7th character), should be assigned when an injury is from flooding resulting directly from the storm. Code X36.0.-, Collapse of dam or manmade structure, should not be assigned when the cause of the collapse is due to the hurricane. Use of code X36.0- is limited to collapses of man-made structures due to earth surface movements, not due to storm surges directly from a hurricane.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Hurricane-Related External Cause Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign external cause codes to identify the cause of injuries incurred as a result of the hurricane.
- Sequence the appropriate injury code before any external cause codes.
- Assign external cause codes for each encounter for care and treatment of the injury.
- Sequence codes for cataclysmic events (hurricane) before other external cause codes, except abuse and terrorism.
- Assign multiple external cause codes if necessary to fully explain the cause (e.g., hurricane and building collapse).
- External cause codes capture the intent of the event (unintentional, accidental, or intentional such as suicide or assault).
- External cause codes capture the place where the event occurred.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- External cause codes must not be recorded as a principal diagnosis in non-inpatient settings.
- Codes should not be assigned for encounters treating medical conditions when no injury, adverse effect, or poisoning is involved.
- Code X37.0- (Hurricane) is used for injuries from the hurricane or flooding caused by a levee breaking related to the hurricane.
- Code X38.- (Flood) is used for injuries from flooding resulting directly from the storm.
- Code X36.0.- (Collapse of dam or manmade structure) must not be assigned when the cause is due to the hurricane; it is limited to collapses due to earth surface movements.
- Code X37.0- must include the appropriate 7th character.
- Code X38.- must include the appropriate 7th character.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `478_479`

> **Actual Text**
>
> An external cause of morbidity code should be assigned to identify the cause of the injury(ies) incurred as a result of the hurricane. The use of external cause of morbidity codes is supplemental to the application of ICD-10-CM codes. External cause of morbidity codes are never to be recorded as a principal diagnosis (firstlisted in non-inpatient settings). The appropriate injury code should be sequenced before any external cause codes. The external cause of morbidity codes capture how the injury or health condition happened (cause), the intent (unintentional or accidental; or intentional, such as suicide or assault), the place where the event occurred, the activity of the patient at the time of the event, and the person's status (e.g., civilian, military). They should not be assigned for encounters to treat hurricane victims' medical conditions when no injury, adverse effect or poisoning is involved. External cause of morbidity codes should be assigned for each encounter for care and treatment of the injury. External cause of morbidity codes may be assigned in all health care settings. For the purpose of capturing complete and accurate ICD-10-CM data in the aftermath of the hurricane, a healthcare setting should be considered as any location where medical care is provided by licensed healthcare professionals.
>
> Codes for cataclysmic events, such as a hurricane, take priority over all other external cause codes except child and adult abuse and terrorism and should be sequenced before other external cause of injury codes. Assign as many external cause of morbidity codes as necessary to fully explain each cause. For example, if an injury occurs as a result of a building collapse during the hurricane, external cause codes for both the hurricane and the building collapse should be assigned, with the external causes code for hurricane being sequenced as the first external cause code. For injuries incurred as a direct result of the hurricane, assign the appropriate code(s) for the injuries, followed by the code X37.0-, Hurricane (with the appropriate 7th character), and any other applicable external cause of injury codes. Code X37.0- also should be assigned when an injury is incurred as a result of flooding caused by a levee breaking related to the hurricane. Code X38.-, Flood (with the appropriate 7th character), should be assigned when an injury is from flooding resulting directly from the storm. Code X36.0.-, Collapse of dam or manmade structure, should not be assigned when the cause of the collapse is due to the hurricane. Use of code X36.0- is limited to collapses of man-made structures due to earth surface movements, not due to storm surges directly from a hurricane.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Hurricane-Related External Cause Coding

- Assign external cause codes to identify the cause of injuries incurred as a result of the hurricane.
- Sequence the appropriate injury code before any external cause codes.

- External cause codes capture the place where the event occurred.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**

- Codes should only be assigned when an injury, adverse effect, or poisoning is involved.
- Assign codes X37.0- for injuries from the hurricane or flooding caused by a levee breaking related to the hurricane.
- Assign code X38.- for injuries from flooding resulting directly from the storm.
- Use code X36.0- only for collapses of man-made structures due to earth surface movements, not storm surges.

- Assign code X37.0- when an injury is incurred as a result of flooding caused by a levee breaking related to the hurricane.

The text in both the older and newer versions explicitly mandates the use of X37.0- for hurricane/flooding and prohibits the use of X36.0- for storm-related collapses, reserving it for earth surface movements. While the newer version includes minor phrasing adjustments like 'Do not assign collapse codes' versus 'must not be assigned', the clinical instruction and resulting coding behavior remain unchanged.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Code X36.0.- (Collapse of dam or manmade structure) must not be assigned when the cause is due to the hurricane; it is limited to collapses due to earth surface movements. -> October 2025 (New Version): Use code X36.0- only for collapses of man-made structures due to earth surface movements, not storm surges.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Code X37.0- (Hurricane) is used for injuries from the hurricane or flooding caused by a levee breaking related to the hurricane. -> October 2025 (New Version): Assign codes X37.0- for injuries from the hurricane or flooding caused by a levee breaking related to the hurricane.
