### 258. Primary Malignancy Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Neoplasm Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 9/10  
*Both profiles describe the Primary Malignancy Coding Guidelines, with the newer version expanding scope to include specific sequencing rules for metastasis and antineoplastic therapy.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `100_120`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Coding and sequencing of complications associated with the malignancies or with the therapy thereof are subject to the following guidelines:
>
> When a primary malignancy has been excised but further treatment, such as an additional surgery for the malignancy, radiation therapy or chemotherapy is directed to that site, the primary malignancy code should be used until treatment is completed.
>
> When a primary malignancy has been previously excised or eradicated from its site, there is no further treatment (of the malignancy) directed to that site, and there is no evidence of any existing primary malignancy at that site, a code from category Z85, Personal history of malignant neoplasm, should be used to indicate the former site of the malignancy.
>
> Codes from subcategories Z85.0 - Z85.85 should only be assigned for the former site of a primary malignancy, not the site of a secondary malignancy. Code Z85.89 may be assigned for the former site(s) of either a primary or secondary malignancy.
>
> See Section I.C.21. Factors influencing health status and contact with health services, History (of)

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Primary Malignancy Coding and Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Use primary malignancy code until treatment is completed if excised but further treatment is directed to that site.
- Use category Z85 code if malignancy is excised/eradicated with no further treatment and no evidence of existing malignancy.
- Assign Z85.0-Z85.85 codes only for the former site of a primary malignancy, not for secondary malignancy sites.
- Assign Z85.89 for the former site(s) of either a primary or secondary malignancy.
- Refer to Section I.C.21 for factors influencing health status and contact with health services regarding history.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Evidence of no further treatment directed to the site.
- No evidence of any existing primary malignancy at the site.
- Distinction between former site of primary vs. site of secondary malignancy for Z85 subcategories.
- Confirmation that malignancy has been excised or eradicated.
- Verification that treatment (surgery, radiation, chemotherapy) is completed or not directed to the site.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `529_534_547`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Coding and sequencing of complications associated with the malignancies or with the therapy thereof are subject to the following guidelines:
>
> When a primary malignancy has been previously excised or eradicated from its site and there is no further treatment directed to that site and there is no evidence of any existing primary malignancy at that site, a code from category Z85, Personal history of malignant neoplasm, should be used to indicate the former site of the malignancy. Any mention of extension, invasion, or metastasis to another site is coded as a secondary malignant neoplasm to that site. The secondary site may be the principal or first-listed diagnosis with the Z85 code used as a secondary code.
>
> See section I.C.2.t. Secondary malignant neoplasm of lymphoid tissue .
>
> - e. Admissions/Encounters involving antineoplastic chemotherapy, immunotherapy and radiation therapy
> - 1) Episode of care involves surgical removal of neoplasm
>
> When an episode of care involves the surgical removal of a neoplasm, primary or secondary site, followed by adjunct chemotherapy or radiation treatment during the same episode of care, the code for the neoplasm should be assigned as principal or first-listed diagnosis .
>
> - 2) Patient admission/encounter chiefly for administration of antineoplastic chemotherapy, immunotherapy and radiation therapy
>
> If a patient admission/encounter is chiefly for the administration of chemotherapy, immunotherapy or external beam radiation therapy for the treatment of a neoplasm , assign code Z51.0, Encounter for antineoplastic radiation therapy, or Z51.11, Encounter for antineoplastic chemotherapy, or Z51.12, Encounter for antineoplastic immunotherapy as the first-listed or principal diagnosis. If the reason for the encounter is more than one type of antineoplastic therapy, code Z51.0 and codes from subcategory Z51.1 may be assigned together, in which case one of these codes would be reported as a secondary diagnosis.
>
> The malignancy for which the therapy is being administered should be assigned as a secondary diagnosis.
>
> If a patient admission/encounter is for the insertion or implantation of radioactive elements (e.g., brachytherapy) the appropriate code for the malignancy is sequenced as the principal or first-listed diagnosis. Code Z51.0 should not be assigned.
>
> - 3) Patient admitted for radiation therapy, chemotherapy or immunotherapy and develops complications
>
> When a patient is admitted for the purpose of external beam radiotherapy, immunotherapy or chemotherapy and develops complications such as uncontrolled nausea and vomiting or dehydration, the principal or first-listed diagnosis is Z51.0, Encounter for antineoplastic radiation therapy, or Z51.11, Encounter for antineoplastic chemotherapy, or Z51.12, Encounter for antineoplastic immunotherapy followed by any codes for the complications.
>
> When a patient is admitted for the purpose of insertion or implantation of radioactive elements (e.g., brachytherapy) and develops complications such as uncontrolled nausea and vomiting or dehydration, the principal or first-listed diagnosis is the appropriate code for the malignancy followed by any codes for the complications.
>
> When a primary malignancy has been excised but further treatment, such as an additional surgery for the malignancy, radiation therapy or chemotherapy is directed to that site, the primary malignancy code should be used until treatment is completed.
>
> When a primary malignancy has been previously excised or eradicated from its site, there is no further treatment (of the malignancy) directed to that site, and there is no evidence of any existing primary malignancy at that site, a code from category Z85, Personal history of malignant neoplasm, should be used to indicate the former site of the malignancy.
>
> Codes from subcategories Z85.0 - Z85.85 should only be assigned for the former site of a primary malignancy, not the site of a secondary malignancy. Code Z85.89 may be assigned for the former site(s) of either a primary or secondary malignancy.
>
> See Section I.C.21. Factors influencing health status and contact with health services, History (of)

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Primary Malignancy Coding and Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign Z85 Personal history codes when a primary malignancy is excised/eradicated with no further treatment or evidence of existing malignancy at that site.
- Code secondary malignant neoplasms to the affected site as the principal diagnosis with Z85 as a secondary code.
- Assign the neoplasm code as principal diagnosis when surgical removal is followed by adjunct chemotherapy or radiation during the same episode.
- Assign Z51.0, Z51.11, or Z51.12 as principal diagnosis for encounters chiefly for antineoplastic therapy administration.
- Sequence the malignancy code as principal diagnosis for brachytherapy encounters, explicitly excluding Z51.0.
- Assign Z51.0, Z51.11, or Z51.12 as the first-listed diagnosis when multiple types of antineoplastic therapy are administered, with one reported as secondary.
- Sequence the malignancy code as principal diagnosis for brachytherapy complications, whereas Z51.0 is sequenced as principal for external beam therapy complications.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Verify no further treatment is directed to the site before assigning Z85 for excised malignancies.
- Ensure documentation confirms the malignancy is suspected or probable if coding borderline diagnoses.
- Assign Z85.0-Z85.85 only for the former site of a primary malignancy, not for secondary malignancy sites.
- Use Z85.89 for the former site of either a primary or secondary malignancy.
- Sequence the malignancy code as principal diagnosis when complications arise during brachytherapy insertion/implantation.
- Verify there is no further treatment directed to the site before assigning Z85 for excised malignancies.
- Verify there is no evidence of any existing primary malignancy at the site before assigning Z85.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Scope Expansion   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version focused strictly on the binary decision of using a primary malignancy code versus a Z85 history code based on treatment status. The newer version expands this scope by explicitly defining how to code secondary malignant neoplasms (allowing them as principal diagnoses) and detailing specific sequencing rules for encounters involving antineoplastic therapy and brachytherapy.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Focuses on using primary codes until treatment is completed or Z85 codes if excised with no further treatment. -> October 2025 (New Version): Adds explicit instructions to code secondary malignant neoplasms to the affected site as the principal diagnosis with Z85 as a secondary code.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Mentions Z85 usage for excised malignancies but lacks specific therapy sequencing. -> October 2025 (New Version): Specifies assigning Z51.0, Z51.11, or Z51.12 as principal diagnosis for encounters chiefly for antineoplastic therapy administration.

*Traceability: Chunk `100_120` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `529_534_547` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 259. Neoplasm and Anemia Encounter Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Neoplasm Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe identical sequencing rules for neoplasm and anemia encounters with no behavioral changes between versions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `101_117`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When admission/encounter is for management of an anemia associated with the malignancy, and the treatment is only for anemia, the appropriate code for the malignancy is sequenced as the principal or first-listed diagnosis followed by the appropriate code for the anemia (such as code D63.0, Anemia in neoplastic disease ).
>
> When an encounter is for management of a complication associated with a neoplasm, such as dehydration, and the treatment is only for the complication, the complication is coded first, followed by the appropriate code(s) for the neoplasm.
>
> The exception to this guideline is anemia. When the admission/encounter is for management of an anemia associated with the malignancy, and the treatment is only for anemia, the appropriate code for the malignancy is sequenced as the principal or first-listed diagnosis followed by code D63.0, Anemia in neoplastic disease.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Neoplasm and Anemia Encounter Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- When admission is for management of anemia associated with malignancy and treatment is only for anemia, sequence malignancy code first followed by anemia code D63.0.
- When admission is for management of a complication associated with neoplasm (e.g., dehydration) and treatment is only for the complication, sequence complication code first followed by neoplasm code.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Treatment must be exclusively for anemia to sequence malignancy as principal diagnosis.
- Treatment must be exclusively for the complication to sequence complication as principal diagnosis.
- Anemia must be documented as associated with the malignancy.
- Code D63.0 must be used for anemia in neoplastic disease.
- Provider must document the specific complication being managed.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `530_544`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When admission/encounter is for management of an anemia associated with the malignancy, and the treatment is only for anemia, the appropriate code for the malignancy is sequenced as the principal or first-listed diagnosis followed by the appropriate code for the anemia (such as code D63.0, Anemia in neoplastic disease ).
>
> When an encounter is for management of a complication associated with a neoplasm, such as dehydration, and the treatment is only for the complication, the complication is coded first, followed by the appropriate code(s) for the neoplasm.
>
> The exception to this guideline is anemia. When the admission/encounter is for management of an anemia associated with the malignancy, and the treatment is only for anemia, the appropriate code for the malignancy is sequenced as the principal or first-listed diagnosis followed by code D63.0, Anemia in neoplastic disease.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Neoplasm and Anemia Encounter Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- When admission is for management of anemia associated with malignancy and treatment is only for anemia, sequence malignancy code first followed by anemia code D63.0.
- When admission is for management of a complication associated with neoplasm (e.g., dehydration) and treatment is only for the complication, sequence complication code first followed by neoplasm code(s).
- Anemia associated with malignancy is treated as an exception to the general complication sequencing rule, requiring malignancy to be sequenced first.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Treatment must be limited to anemia management for the malignancy-first sequencing rule to apply.
- Treatment must be limited to the complication management for the complication-first sequencing rule to apply.
- The anemia code must be D63.0 (Anemia in neoplastic disease) when associated with malignancy.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version contain verbatim identical text regarding the sequencing of malignancy codes versus anemia code D63.0. Both versions explicitly state that when treatment is limited to anemia associated with malignancy, the malignancy must be sequenced first, followed by D63.0. Similarly, both versions mandate that neoplasm complications are coded first if treatment is limited to those complications. There are no differences in logic, requirements, or clinical criteria between the two texts.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): When admission is for management of anemia associated with malignancy, sequence malignancy first followed by D63.0 -> October 2025 (New Version): When admission is for management of anemia associated with malignancy, sequence malignancy first followed by D63.0
- October 1, 2024 (Older): When encounter is for management of neoplasm complication, sequence complication first followed by neoplasm -> October 2025 (New Version): When encounter is for management of neoplasm complication, sequence complication first followed by neoplasm

### 260. Tumor Coding Clarification Protocol

**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Tumor Coding Clarification Protocol with matching behaviors, requirements, and raw text excerpts.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `111`

> **Actual Text**
>
> A patient may have more than one malignant tumor in the same organ. These tumors may represent different primaries or metastatic disease, depending on the site. Should the documentation be unclear, the provider should be queried as to the status of each tumor so that the correct codes can be assigned.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Tumor Coding Clarification Protocol

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes for multiple malignant tumors in the same organ based on whether they represent different primaries or metastatic disease.
- Query the provider if documentation regarding tumor status is unclear to ensure correct code assignment.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must clearly distinguish between different primaries and metastatic disease for multiple tumors in the same organ.
- Provider query is required when the status of each tumor is unclear.

### 263. Extranodal Lymphoma Coding Rules
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Neoplasm Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for extranodal lymphoma metastasis with no substantive change in behavior.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `127`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When a malignant neoplasm of lymphoid tissue metastasizes beyond the lymph nodes, a code from categories C81-C85 with a final character identifying 'extranodal and solid organ sites' should be assigned rather than a code for the secondary neoplasm of the affected solid organ. For example, for metastasis of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma to the lung, brain and left adrenal gland, assign code C83.39 8 , Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of other extranodal and solid organ sites.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Extranodal Lymphoma Coding Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign codes from categories C81-C85 with a final character identifying 'extranodal and solid organ sites' when malignant neoplasm metastasizes beyond lymph nodes.
- Do not assign a code for the secondary neoplasm of the affected solid organ in cases of extranodal metastasis.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The condition must be a malignant neoplasm of lymphoid tissue.
- The neoplasm must metastasize beyond the lymph nodes to extranodal or solid organ sites.
- The assigned code must include the final character identifying 'extranodal and solid organ sites'.

### 270. Outpatient First-Listed Diagnosis Coding
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding outpatient first-listed diagnosis coding rules, including terminology, search order, and sequencing logic.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `399_413`

> **Actual Text**
>
> In the outpatient setting, the term first-listed diagnosis is used in lieu of principal diagnosis.
>
> In determining the first-listed diagnosis the coding conventions of ICD-10-CM, as well as the general and disease specific guidelines take precedence over the outpatient guidelines.
>
> Diagnoses often are not established at the time of the initial encounter/visit. It may take two or more visits before the diagnosis is confirmed.
>
> The most critical rule involves beginning the search for the correct code assignment through the Alphabetic Index. Never begin searching initially in the Tabular List as this will lead to coding errors.
>
> For patients receiving diagnostic services only during an encounter/visit, sequence first the diagnosis, condition, problem, or other reason for encounter/visit shown in the medical record to be chiefly responsible for the outpatient services provided during the encounter/visit. Codes for other diagnoses (e.g., chronic conditions) may be sequenced as additional diagnoses.
>
> For encounters for routine laboratory/radiology testing in the absence of any signs, symptoms, or associated diagnosis, assign Z01.89, Encounter for other specified special examinations. If routine testing is performed during the same encounter as a test to evaluate a sign, symptom, or diagnosis, it is appropriate to assign both the Z code and the code describing the reason for the non-routine test.
>
> For outpatient encounters for diagnostic tests that have been interpreted by a physician, and the final report is available at the time of coding, code any confirmed or definitive diagnosis(es) documented in the interpretation. Do not code related signs and symptoms as additional diagnoses.
>
> Please note: This differs from the coding practice in the hospital inpatient setting regarding abnormal findings on test results.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Outpatient First-Listed Diagnosis Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- The term first-listed diagnosis is used in lieu of principal diagnosis in the outpatient setting.
- ICD-10-CM coding conventions and general/disease-specific guidelines take precedence over outpatient guidelines.
- The search for correct code assignment must begin through the Alphabetic Index, never the Tabular List initially.
- For diagnostic services only, sequence the chiefly responsible diagnosis first, with chronic conditions as additional diagnoses.
- For interpreted diagnostic tests with available final reports, code confirmed or definitive diagnoses without coding related signs and symptoms.
- Diagnoses may not be established at the time of the initial encounter/visit.
- It may take two or more visits before the diagnosis is confirmed.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Diagnosis must be chiefly responsible for the outpatient services provided during the encounter/visit.
- Final report must be available at the time of coding for interpreted diagnostic tests.
- Related signs and symptoms must not be coded as additional diagnoses when a confirmed diagnosis exists.
- Routine laboratory/radiology testing without associated signs/symptoms requires assignment of Z01.89.
- If routine testing occurs with a non-routine test, both the Z code and the code for the non-routine test reason must be assigned.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `832_846`

> **Actual Text**
>
> In the outpatient setting, the term first-listed diagnosis is used in lieu of principal diagnosis.
>
> In determining the first-listed diagnosis the coding conventions of ICD-10-CM, as well as the general and disease specific guidelines take precedence over the outpatient guidelines.
>
> Diagnoses often are not established at the time of the initial encounter/visit. It may take two or more visits before the diagnosis is confirmed.
>
> The most critical rule involves beginning the search for the correct code assignment through the Alphabetic Index. Never begin searching initially in the Tabular List as this will lead to coding errors.
>
> For patients receiving diagnostic services only during an encounter/visit, sequence first the diagnosis, condition, problem, or other reason for encounter/visit shown in the medical record to be chiefly responsible for the outpatient services provided during the encounter/visit. Codes for other diagnoses (e.g., chronic conditions) may be sequenced as additional diagnoses.
>
> For encounters for routine laboratory/radiology testing in the absence of any signs, symptoms, or associated diagnosis, assign Z01.89, Encounter for other specified special examinations. If routine testing is performed during the same encounter as a test to evaluate a sign, symptom, or diagnosis, it is appropriate to assign both the Z code and the code describing the reason for the non-routine test.
>
> For outpatient encounters for diagnostic tests that have been interpreted by a physician, and the final report is available at the time of coding, code any confirmed or definitive diagnosis(es) documented in the interpretation. Do not code related signs and symptoms as additional diagnoses.
>
> Please note: This differs from the coding practice in the hospital inpatient setting regarding abnormal findings on test results.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Outpatient First-Listed Diagnosis Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Use 'first-listed diagnosis' terminology instead of 'principal diagnosis' in outpatient settings.
- ICD-10-CM coding conventions and disease-specific guidelines take precedence over outpatient guidelines.
- Begin code assignment search through the Alphabetic Index, never starting in the Tabular List.
- Sequence the diagnosis chiefly responsible for services first; code other diagnoses (e.g., chronic conditions) as additional diagnoses.
- Code confirmed or definitive diagnoses from physician-interpreted reports available at coding time; do not code related signs and symptoms as additional diagnoses.
- Recognize that diagnoses may not be established at the initial encounter and may require two or more visits for confirmation.
- Differentiate coding practice for outpatient encounters from the hospital inpatient setting regarding abnormal findings on test results.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Diagnosis must be documented as chiefly responsible for the outpatient services provided during the encounter.
- Final report for diagnostic tests must be available at the time of coding to assign confirmed diagnoses.
- Routine laboratory/radiology testing without associated signs, symptoms, or diagnosis requires assignment of Z01.89.
- If routine testing occurs with a non-routine test evaluating a sign/symptom, assign both the Z code and the code for the non-routine test reason.
- Do not code related signs and symptoms as additional diagnoses when a confirmed diagnosis is documented in an interpreted report.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 and October 2025 guidelines present the exact same coding logic for outpatient first-listed diagnoses, including the requirement to use the Alphabetic Index first and the precedence of ICD-10-CM conventions. The text excerpts are nearly identical, with the 2025 version only adding a minor clarification about differentiating outpatient practices from inpatient settings regarding abnormal findings. No substantive changes to the coding behavior or requirements were observed between the two versions.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Defines the use of first-listed diagnosis and mandates starting code assignment searches in the Alphabetic Index. -> October 2025 (New Version): Defines the same use of first-listed diagnosis and mandates the same search order, with an added note to differentiate outpatient coding from inpatient practices regarding abnormal findings.

*Traceability: Chunk `399_413` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `832_846` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 271. Kidney Transplant Complication Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding T86.1 coding rules for kidney transplant complications and the exclusion of uncomplicated CKD.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `314_349`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Patients who have undergone kidney transplant may still have some form of chronic kidney disease (CKD) because the kidney transplant may not fully restore kidney function. Code T86.1should be assigned for documented complications of a kidney transplant, such as transplant failure or rejection or other transplant complication. Code T86.1- should not be assigned for post kidney transplant patients who have chronic kidney (CKD) unless a transplant complication such as transplant failure or rejection is documented. If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a complication of the transplant, query the provider.
>
> Conditions that affect the function of the transplanted kidney, other than CKD, should be assigned a code from subcategory T86.1, Complications of transplanted organ, Kidney, and a secondary code that identifies the complication.
>
> For patients with CKD following a kidney transplant, but who do not have a complication such as failure or rejection, see section I.C.14. Chronic kidney disease and kidney transplant status .
>
> See I.C.1.d.4. for sequencing of sepsis due to infection in transplanted organ
>
> See section I.C.19. for information on the coding of organ transplant complications.
>
> - Z99
>
> Dependence on enabling machines and devices, not elsewhere classified
>
> Note: Categories Z89-Z90 and Z93-Z99 are for use only if there are no complications or malfunctions of the organ or tissue replaced, the amputation site or the equipment on which the patient is dependent.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Kidney Transplant Complication Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code T86.1 for documented complications of a kidney transplant such as transplant failure or rejection.
- Do not assign T86.1- for post-transplant patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD) unless a specific transplant complication like failure or rejection is documented.
- Query the provider if documentation is unclear regarding whether the patient has a transplant complication.
- Assign codes from subcategory T86.1 for conditions affecting transplanted kidney function other than CKD, paired with a secondary code identifying the complication.
- Use categories Z89-Z90 and Z93-Z99 only if there are no complications or malfunctions of the organ, amputation site, or dependent equipment.
- Assign a secondary code to identify the specific complication when using subcategory T86.1 for conditions affecting transplanted kidney function other than CKD.
- Refer to section I.C.1.d.4 for specific sequencing instructions regarding sepsis due to infection in a transplanted organ.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must explicitly state a transplant complication (e.g., failure, rejection) to assign T86.1.
- A secondary code must identify the specific complication when assigning codes from subcategory T86.1.
- Provider query is required if the documentation is unclear about the presence of a transplant complication.
- Categories Z89-Z90 and Z93-Z99 require confirmation that no complications or malfunctions exist.
- Refer to section I.C.14 for coding CKD following a kidney transplant without complications.
- Documentation must explicitly state a transplant complication (e.g., failure, rejection) to assign code T86.1.
- Categories Z89-Z90 and Z93-Z99 require confirmation that there are no complications or malfunctions of the organ, amputation site, or dependent equipment.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `742`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Patients who have undergone kidney transplant may still have some form of chronic kidney disease (CKD) because the kidney transplant may not fully restore kidney function. Code T86.1should be assigned for documented complications of a kidney transplant, such as transplant failure or rejection or other transplant complication. Code T86.1- should not be assigned for post kidney transplant patients who have chronic kidney (CKD) unless a transplant complication such as transplant failure or rejection is documented. If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a complication of the transplant, query the provider.
>
> Conditions that affect the function of the transplanted kidney, other than CKD, should be assigned a code from subcategory T86.1, Complications of transplanted organ, Kidney, and a secondary code that identifies the complication.
>
> For patients with CKD following a kidney transplant, but who do not have a complication such as failure or rejection, see section I.C.14. Chronic kidney disease and kidney transplant status .
>
> See I.C.1.d.4. for sequencing of sepsis due to infection in transplanted organ

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Kidney Transplant Complication Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code T86.1 for documented complications of a kidney transplant such as transplant failure or rejection.
- Do not assign T86.1- for post-transplant patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD) unless a specific transplant complication like failure or rejection is documented.
- Query the provider if documentation is unclear regarding whether the patient has a transplant complication.
- Assign codes from subcategory T86.1 for conditions affecting transplanted kidney function other than CKD, paired with a secondary code identifying the complication.

The newer version retains the exact same instructions for querying providers on unclear documentation and referencing section I.C.14 for non-complicated cases. While the newer version includes a specific reference to section I.C.19 for organ trauma in its taxonomy description, the extracted behavioral profiles and raw text excerpts are functionally equivalent with no substantive logic delta.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Refer to section I.C.1.d.4 for specific sequencing instructions regarding sepsis due to infection in a transplanted organ. -> October 2025 (New Version): Refer to section I.C.1.d.4 for specific sequencing instructions regarding sepsis due to infection in a transplanted organ.

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `60`

> **Actual Text**
>
> If a patient with documented HIV disease, HIV-related illness or AIDS is currently managed on antiretroviral medications, assign code B20, Human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] disease. Code Z79.899, Other long term (current) drug therapy, may be assigned as an additional code to identify the long-term (current) use of antiretroviral medications.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** HIV Medication Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code B20 for patients with documented HIV disease, HIV-related illness, or AIDS currently managed on antiretroviral medications.
- Assign code Z79.899 as an additional code to identify the long-term (current) use of antiretroviral medications.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**

- Patient must be currently managed on antiretroviral medications.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `487_488`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Patients with documentation of inconclusive HIV serology, may be assigned code R75 human immunodeficiency virus [HIV].
>
> - Inconclusive HIV serology , Inconclusive laboratory evidence of
> - (g) Previously diagnosed HIV-related illness Patients with documentation of a prior diagnosis of an HIV-related illness should be coded to B20. Once an HIV-related illness has developed, code B20 should always be assigned on every subsequent admission/encounter. Patients previously diagnosed with any HIV illness (B20) should never be assigned to R75, Inconclusive laboratory evidence of human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] or Z21, Asymptomatic human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] infection status.
> - (h) HIV Infection in Pregnancy, Childbirth and the Puerperium When a patient presents during pregnancy, childbirth or the puerperium with documented symptomatic HIV disease or an HIV related illness, assign a code from subcategory O98.7, Human immunodeficiency [HIV] disease complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, followed by code B20 and additional code(s) for any HIV-related illness(es). Codes from Chapter 15 always take sequencing priority.
>
> When a patient presents during pregnancy, childbirth or the puerperium with documented asymptomatic HIV infection status or is HIV-positive, assign a code from subcategory O98.7 followed by code Z21.
>
> - (i)
> - Encounters for HIV testing Use additional codes for any
>
> If a patient without signs or symptoms is tested for HIV, assign code Z11.4, Encounter for screening for human immunodeficiency virus [HIV]. associated high-risk behavior , if applicable.
>
> If a patient with signs or symptoms of HIV presents for HIV testing, code the signs and symptoms. An additional counseling code Z71.7, Human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] counseling, may be assigned if counseling is provided during the encounter for the test. Code Z11.4, Encounter for screening for human immunodeficiency virus [HIV], should not be assigned if HIV signs or symptoms are present.
>
> When a patient presents for follow up regarding their HIV test results and the test result is negative, assign code Z71.7, Human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] counseling.
>
> If the results are positive, see previous guidelines and assign codes as appropriate.
>
> If a patient with documented HIV disease, HIV-related illness or AIDS is currently managed on antiretroviral medications, assign code B20, Human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] disease.
>
> If a patient with documented HIV positive status is currently managed on antiretroviral medication, assign code Z21, Asymptomatic human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] infection status, in the absence of any additional documentation of HIV disease, HIV-related illness or AIDS.
>
> Code Z79.899, Other long term (current) drug therapy, may be assigned as an additional code to identify the long-term (current) use of antiretroviral medications.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** HIV Medication and Status Coding

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code B20 for patients with documented HIV disease, HIV-related illness, or AIDS managed on antiretroviral medications.
- Assign code Z21 for patients with documented HIV positive status managed on antiretroviral medication only if there is no documentation of HIV disease, illness, or AIDS.
- Assign code Z79.899 as an additional code to identify the long-term use of antiretroviral medications.
- Assign code Z11.4 for encounters where a patient without signs or symptoms is tested for HIV.
- Assign code Z71.7 for encounters regarding follow-up of negative HIV test results or for counseling during testing.
- Assign code R75 for patients with documentation of inconclusive HIV serology.
- Assign code Z11.4 with an additional code for associated high-risk behavior if applicable during HIV screening encounters.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Code B20 must be assigned on every subsequent admission/encounter once an HIV-related illness has developed.
- Patients previously diagnosed with any HIV illness (B20) must never be assigned to R75 or Z21.
- Code Z11.4 should not be assigned if HIV signs or symptoms are present.
- Codes from Chapter 15 (O98.7) always take sequencing priority for pregnancy, childbirth, or puerperium cases.
- Code Z71.7 may be assigned if counseling is provided during the encounter for the test.
- For pregnancy, childbirth, or puerperium cases with symptomatic HIV disease, assign code O98.7 followed by code B20 and additional codes for HIV-related illnesses.
- For pregnancy, childbirth, or puerperium cases with asymptomatic HIV infection status, assign code O98.7 followed by code Z21.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Scope Expansion   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older guideline focused primarily on assigning B20 for documented HIV disease and Z79.899 for medication use. The newer version expands the scope by explicitly defining rules for inconclusive serology (R75), asymptomatic status (Z21), and establishing strict sequencing priorities for pregnancy cases (O98.7) to prevent incorrect code assignment.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Focuses on assigning B20 for documented HIV disease and Z79.899 for long-term drug therapy without specific sequencing rules for pregnancy. -> October 2025 (New Version): Expands to include specific sequencing priorities where O98.7 takes priority over B20 or Z21 in pregnancy cases and defines rules for inconclusive serology (R75).
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Does not mention coding for inconclusive HIV serology or asymptomatic status distinctions. -> October 2025 (New Version): Explicitly assigns R75 for inconclusive serology and Z21 for asymptomatic status only if no HIV disease is documented.

*Traceability: Chunk `60` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `487_488` (October 2025 (New Version))*

#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `770_805_806`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Z codes are for use in any healthcare setting. Z codes may be used as either a first-listed (principal diagnosis code in the inpatient setting) or secondary code, depending on the circumstances of the encounter. Certain Z codes may only be used as first-listed or principal diagnosis.
>
> Certain Z codes are so non-specific, or potentially redundant with other codes in the classification, that there can be little justification for their use in the inpatient setting. Their use in the outpatient setting should be limited to those instances when there is no further documentation to permit more precise coding. Otherwise, any sign or symptom or any other reason for visit that is captured in another code should be used.
>
> Nonspecific Z codes/categories:
>
> {"Z02.9": "Z04.9", "Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified": "Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason"}
> {"Z02.9": "Z13.9", "Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified": "Encounter for screening, unspecified"}
> {"Z02.9": "Z41.9", "Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified": "Encounter for procedure for purposes other than remedying health state, unspecified"}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Z Code Usage and Specificity

**Key Behaviors:**
- Z codes may be used as first-listed or secondary codes depending on the encounter circumstances.
- Certain Z codes are restricted to use only as first-listed or principal diagnosis codes.
- Use of non-specific Z codes in the inpatient setting is discouraged due to redundancy with other codes.
- Outpatient use of non-specific Z codes is limited to instances where no further documentation permits more precise coding.
- Any sign, symptom, or reason for visit captured in another code should be used instead of a non-specific Z code.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Z codes must be appropriate for the specific healthcare setting (inpatient or outpatient).
- More precise codes must be used if documentation supports them over non-specific Z codes.
- Non-specific Z codes should only be assigned when no other code exists to capture the reason for the visit.
- Z codes must be used as either a first-listed or secondary code depending on the circumstances of the encounter.
- Certain Z codes are restricted to use only as first-listed or principal diagnosis codes.

**Superseded Guidance:**
- Encounter for administrative examinations, unspecified (Z02.9) is deprecated in favor of more specific codes such as Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason (Z04.9), Encounter for screening, unspecified (Z13.9), or Encounter for procedure for purposes other than remedying health state, unspecified (Z41.9).
- The use of the non-specific code Z02.9 is no longer permitted when more specific documentation exists for the reason for the visit.

---
#### 📌 Delta: Coding Rule Clarification   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The older version provided general guidelines stating Z codes could be used as first-listed or secondary codes based on encounter circumstances. The newer version clarifies this by explicitly discouraging the use of non-specific Z codes in inpatient settings and limiting outpatient use to instances where no further documentation permits more precise coding. It further mandates that any sign, symptom, or reason for visit captured in another code must be used instead of a non-specific Z code, and provides specific deprecated mappings for administrative examinations.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Z codes may be used as either a first-listed or secondary code depending on the circumstances of the encounter. -> October 2025 (New Version): Use of non-specific Z codes in the inpatient setting is discouraged due to redundancy with other codes, and outpatient use is limited to instances where no further documentation permits more precise coding.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): No specific restrictions on non-specific codes mentioned. -> October 2025 (New Version): Any sign, symptom, or reason for visit captured in another code should be used instead of a non-specific Z code, with specific deprecation of Z02.9 in favor of Z04.9, Z13.9, or Z41.9.

*Traceability: Chunk `340` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `770_805_806` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 274. CKD Post-Transplant Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for CKD post-transplant status, including the distinction between residual disease and complications.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `212`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Patients who have undergone kidney transplant may still have some form of chronic kidney disease ( CKD ) because the kidney transplant may not
> fully restore kidney function. Therefore, the presence of CKD alone does not constitute a transplant complication. Assign the appropriate N18 code for the patient's stage of CKD and code Z94.0, Kidney transplant status. If a transplant complication such as failure or rejection or other transplant complication is documented, see section I.C.19.g for information on coding complications of a kidney transplant. If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a complication of the transplant, query the provider.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** CKD Post-Transplant Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign appropriate N18 code for patient's stage of CKD regardless of transplant status
- Code Z94.0 for kidney transplant status
- Do not code CKD as a transplant complication unless specific complication is documented
- Query provider if documentation is unclear regarding transplant complications
- Refer to section I.C.19.g for coding specific transplant complications like failure or rejection

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Presence of CKD alone does not constitute a transplant complication
- Specific documentation of transplant complication (failure, rejection, etc.) required to code as complication
- Provider query required if documentation is unclear regarding complication status

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `641`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Patients who have undergone kidney transplant may still have some form of chronic kidney disease ( CKD ) because the kidney transplant may not fully restore kidney function. If a transplant complication such as failure or rejection or other transplant complication is documented, see section I.C.19.g for information on coding complications of a kidney transplant. If the documentation is unclear as to whether the patient has a complication of the transplant, query the provider.

### 276. Z13 Screening Encounter Classification
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Z13 Screening Encounter Classification logic with no substantive behavioral changes between versions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `353_354`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Screening is the testing for disease or disease precursors in seemingly well individuals so that early detection and treatment can be provided for those who test positive for the disease (e.g., screening mammogram).
>
> The testing of a person to rule out or confirm a suspected diagnosis because the patient has some sign or symptom is a diagnostic examination, not a screening. In these cases, the sign or symptom is used to explain the reason for the test.
>
> A screening code may be a first-listed code if the reason for the visit is specifically the screening exam. It may also be used as an additional code if the screening is done during an office visit for other health problems. A screening code is not necessary if the screening is inherent to a routine examination, such as a pap smear done during a routine pelvic examination.
>
> Should a condition be discovered during the screening then the code for the condition may be assigned as an additional diagnosis.
>
> The Z code indicates that a screening exam is planned. A procedure code is required to confirm that the screening was performed.
>
> The screening Z codes/categories:
>
> {"Z11": "Z12", "Encounter for screening for infectious and parasitic diseases": "Encounter for screening for malignant neoplasms"}
> {"Z11": "Z13", "Encounter for screening for infectious and parasitic diseases": "Encounter for screening for other diseases and disorders Except: Z13.9, Encounter for screening, unspecified"}
> {"Z11": "Z36", "Encounter for screening for infectious and parasitic diseases": "Encounter for antenatal screening for mother"}

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Z13 Screening Encounter Classification

**Key Behaviors:**
- Screening is defined as testing for disease in seemingly well individuals for early detection, distinct from diagnostic examinations for suspected conditions.
- A screening code may be a first-listed code if the reason for the visit is specifically the screening exam.
- A screening code may be used as an additional code if the screening is performed during an office visit for other health problems.
- A screening code is not necessary if the screening is inherent to a routine examination (e.g., pap smear during a routine pelvic exam).
- If a condition is discovered during screening, the code for the condition is assigned as an additional diagnosis.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The Z code indicates that a screening exam is planned.
- A procedure code is required to confirm that the screening was performed.
- For diagnostic examinations (testing to rule out or confirm a suspected diagnosis), the sign or symptom must be used to explain the reason for the test.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `785_786`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Screening is the testing for disease or disease precursors in seemingly well individuals so that early detection and treatment can be provided for those who test positive for the disease (e.g., screening mammogram).

### 280. Encounter Codes for Abnormal Findings
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for Z00 encounters with abnormal findings, including the requirement for a first-listed diagnosis and secondary code.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `417`

> **Actual Text**
>
> The subcategories for encounters for general medical examinations, Z00.0- and encounter for routine child health examination, Z00.12-, provide codes for with and without abnormal findings. Should a general medical examination result in an abnormal finding, the code for general medical examination with abnormal finding should be assigned as the first-listed diagnosis. An examination with abnormal findings refers to a condition/diagnosis that is newly identified or a change in severity of a chronic condition (such as uncontrolled hypertension, or an acute exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) during a routine physical examination. A secondary code for the abnormal finding should also be coded.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Encounter Codes for Abnormal Findings

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign code for general medical examination with abnormal findings as the first-listed diagnosis when an abnormal finding is identified.
- An examination with abnormal findings refers to a newly identified condition or a change in severity of a chronic condition during a routine physical examination.
- A secondary code for the specific abnormal finding must also be coded.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The abnormal finding must be newly identified or represent a change in severity of a chronic condition.
- The finding must occur during a routine general medical examination or routine child health examination.
- The code for the examination with abnormal findings must be assigned as the first-listed diagnosis.

---

> **Actual Text**
>
> Counseling Z codes are used when a patient or family member receives assistance in the aftermath of an illness or injury, or when support is required in coping with family or social problems.
>
> The counseling Z codes/categories:
>
> {"Z30.0-": "Z31.5", "Encounter for general counseling and advice on contraception": "Encounter for procreative genetic counseling"}
> {"Z30.0-": "Z31.6-", "Encounter for general counseling and advice on contraception": "Encounter for general counseling and advice on procreation"}
> {"Z30.0-": "Z32.2", "Encounter for general counseling and advice on contraception": "Encounter for childbirth instruction"}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Counseling Z Code Mapping Updates

**Key Behaviors:**
- Counseling Z codes are used when a patient or family member receives assistance in the aftermath of an illness or injury, or when support is required in coping with family or social problems.
- Encounter for general counseling and advice on contraception is mapped to Encounter for procreative genetic counseling (Z31.5).
- Encounter for general counseling and advice on contraception is mapped to Encounter for general counseling and advice on procreation (Z31.6-).
- Encounter for general counseling and advice on contraception is mapped to Encounter for childbirth instruction (Z32.2).
- Additional mapping table rows exist for other counseling scenarios.
- Z71.84 and Z71.85 are excluded from the general counseling category because they represent specific high-risk scenarios (travel safety and vaccine immunization) requiring distinct clinical contexts.
- A single clinical encounter can legitimately serve multiple distinct purposes (e.g., genetic counseling, childbirth instruction) depending on the specific advice given.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Patient or family member must receive assistance in the aftermath of an illness or injury.
- Support must be required in coping with family or social problems.
- Documentation must specify the type of counseling (e.g., contraception, procreation, childbirth instruction).
- General counseling and advice on contraception must be mapped to the specific procreation-related Z code category.
- Encounter must be classified under the appropriate Z30-Z39 counseling category.
- Documentation must specify the type of counseling (e.g., contraception, procreation, childbirth instruction) to justify the mapping to specific Z30-Z39 categories.

- Encounter for general counseling and advice on contraception is mapped to Encounter for general counseling and advice on procreation (Z31.6-).
- Encounter for general counseling and advice on contraception is mapped to Encounter for childbirth instruction (Z32.2).

### 284. Chemotherapy Encounter Sequencing Rules
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Chemotherapy Encounter Sequencing rule with no substantive changes in logic or behavior between versions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `414`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For patients receiving therapeutic services only during an encounter/visit, sequence first the diagnosis, condition, problem, or other reason for encounter/visit shown in the medical record to be chiefly responsible for the outpatient services provided during the encounter/visit. Codes for other diagnoses (e.g., chronic conditions) may be sequenced as additional diagnoses.
>
> The only exception to this rule is that when the primary reason for the admission/encounter is chemotherapy or radiation therapy, the appropriate Z code for the service is listed first, and the diagnosis or problem for which the service is being performed listed second.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Chemotherapy Encounter Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Sequence the diagnosis chiefly responsible for outpatient therapeutic services first.
- Code other diagnoses (e.g., chronic conditions) as additional diagnoses.
- List the appropriate Z code for chemotherapy or radiation therapy first if it is the primary reason for admission/encounter.
- List the diagnosis or problem for which the service is being performed second if chemotherapy/radiation is the primary reason.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The diagnosis must be shown in the medical record to be chiefly responsible for the outpatient services provided.
- The primary reason for the admission/encounter must be chemotherapy or radiation therapy to trigger the Z code sequencing exception.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `847`

> **Actual Text**
>

### 285. Inpatient Z-Code Classification Guidelines

*Both profiles address the classification and reporting restrictions of Z codes in the inpatient setting, though they focus on different specific constraints (general non-specificity vs. principal diagnosis eligibility).*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `374`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Certain Z codes are so non-specific, or potentially redundant with other codes in the classification, that there can be little justification for their use in the inpatient setting. Their use in the outpatient setting should be limited to those instances when there is no further documentation to permit more precise coding. Otherwise, any sign or symptom or any other reason for visit that is captured in another code should be used.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Inpatient Z-Code Classification Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Certain non-specific Z codes are discouraged in the inpatient setting due to redundancy with other codes.
- Use of Z codes in the outpatient setting is limited to instances where no further documentation permits more precise coding.
- Any sign, symptom, or reason for visit captured in another code should be used instead of a non-specific Z code.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must permit more precise coding before a Z code can be used in the outpatient setting.
- The condition must not be better described by another specific code in the classification.

> **Actual Text**

>

>
> Except: Z00.6
>
> Z01
>
> Encounter for other special examination without complaint,
>
> suspected or reported diagnosis
>
> Z02
>
> Encounter for administrative examination
>
> Z04
>
> Encounter for examination and observation for other reasons
>
> Z33.2
>
> Encounter for elective termination of pregnancy
>
> {"Z31.81": "Z31.83", "Encounter for male factor infertility in female patient": "Encounter for assisted reproductive fertility procedure cycle"}
> {"Z31.81": "Z31.84", "Encounter for male factor infertility in female patient": "Encounter for fertility preservation procedure"}
> {"Z31.81": "Z34", "Encounter for male factor infertility in female patient": "Encounter for supervision of normal pregnancy"}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Inpatient Z-Code Principal Diagnosis Restrictions

**Key Behaviors:**
- Z codes may only be reported as the principal/first-listed diagnosis in specific scenarios.
- The exception applies when there are multiple encounters on the same day and medical records are combined.
- Z00.6 is excluded from the general Z00 principal diagnosis rule.
- Specific Z code mappings exist for infertility and pregnancy supervision scenarios.
- Administrative and special examinations without complaints are restricted to principal diagnosis status.
- Z01 codes (Encounter for other special examination) are restricted from being the principal diagnosis.
- Z02 codes (Encounter for administrative examination) are restricted from being the principal diagnosis.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Multiple encounters must occur on the same day.
- Medical records for the encounters must be combined.
- The condition must not fall under the Z00.6 exception category.
- The diagnosis must be the primary reason for the combined encounter.

---

The older guideline broadly discouraged the use of non-specific Z codes in the inpatient setting to prevent redundancy with more precise codes. The newer version expands the scope by explicitly defining which specific Z code categories (Z00, Z01, Z02, Z04) are restricted from being reported as the principal diagnosis, while introducing specific exceptions for combined encounters on the same day and mapping specific infertility codes.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Certain non-specific Z codes are generally discouraged in the inpatient setting due to redundancy with other codes. -> October 2025 (New Version): Specific Z code categories (Z00, Z01, Z02, Z04) are restricted from being the principal diagnosis, except when multiple encounters on the same day are combined.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Focuses on avoiding non-specific codes when more precise documentation exists. -> October 2025 (New Version): Introduces explicit mappings for infertility scenarios (e.g., Z31.81 to Z31.83 or Z31.84) and pregnancy supervision exceptions.

*Traceability: Chunk `374` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `807_808` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 287. Secondary Diabetes Medication Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for assigning Z79 codes to secondary diabetes patients using specific medications.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `136`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For patients with secondary diabetes mellitus who routinely use insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs, or injectable non-insulin drugs, additional code(s) from category Z79 should be assigned to identify the long-term (current) use of insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs , or non-injectable non-insulin drugs as follows:
>
> If the patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and insulin, both code Z79.4, Long term (current) use of insulin, and code Z79.84, Long term (current) use of oral hypoglycemic drugs, should be assigned.
>
> If the patient is treated with both insulin and an injectable non- insulin antidiabetic drug, assign codes Z79.4, Longterm (current) use of insulin, and Z79.85, Long-term (current) use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
>
> If the patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and an injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drug, assign codes Z79.84, Long-term (current) use of oral hypoglycemic drugs, and Z79.85, Long-term (current) use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
>
> Code Z79.4 should not be assigned if insulin is given temporarily to bring a secondary diabetic patient's blood sugar under control during an encounter .

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Secondary Diabetes Medication Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign additional Z79 codes to identify long-term use of insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs, or injectable non-insulin drugs for patients with secondary diabetes mellitus.
- Assign both Z79.4 and Z79.84 when a patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and insulin.
- Assign both Z79.4 and Z79.85 when a patient is treated with both insulin and an injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drug.
- Assign both Z79.84 and Z79.85 when a patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and an injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drug.
- Do not assign code Z79.4 if insulin is given temporarily to control blood sugar during an encounter.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Patient must have secondary diabetes mellitus.
- Patient must routinely use the specified medication (insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs, or injectable non-insulin drugs).
- Usage must be long-term (current) rather than temporary.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `563`

> **Actual Text**
>
> For patients with secondary diabetes mellitus who routinely use insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs, or injectable non-insulin drugs, additional code(s) from category Z79 should be assigned to identify the long-term (current) use of insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs , or non-injectable non-insulin drugs as follows:
>
> If the patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and insulin, both code Z79.4, Long term (current) use of insulin, and code Z79.84, Long term (current) use of oral hypoglycemic drugs, should be assigned.
>
> If the patient is treated with both insulin and an injectable non- insulin antidiabetic drug, assign codes Z79.4, Longterm (current) use of insulin, and Z79.85, Long-term (current) use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.
>
> If the patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and an injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drug, assign codes Z79.84, Long-term (current) use of oral hypoglycemic drugs, and Z79.85, Long-term (current) use of injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drugs.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Secondary Diabetes Medication Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign additional codes from category Z79 for patients with secondary diabetes mellitus who routinely use insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs, or injectable non-insulin drugs.
- Assign both Z79.4 and Z79.84 when a patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and insulin.
- Assign both Z79.4 and Z79.85 when a patient is treated with both insulin and an injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drug.
- Assign both Z79.84 and Z79.85 when a patient is treated with both oral hypoglycemic drugs and an injectable non-insulin antidiabetic drug.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Patient must have secondary diabetes mellitus.
- Patient must routinely use insulin, oral hypoglycemic drugs, or injectable non-insulin drugs.
- Codes must identify the long-term (current) use of the specified medications.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same instructions for assigning Z79.4, Z79.84, and Z79.85 codes based on medication combinations. The fundamental requirement that patients must routinely use the medications for long-term care remains identical in both texts.

**Key Differences:**

*Traceability: Chunk `136` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `563` (October 2025 (New Version))*

### 290. Observation Z Code Assignment Rules
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical feature regarding Observation Z Code Assignment Rules with identical behavioral logic and requirements.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `355`

> **Actual Text**
>
> There are three observation Z code categories. They are for use in very limited circumstances when a person is being observed for a suspected condition that is ruled out. The observation codes are not for use if an injury or illness or any signs or symptoms related to the suspected condition are present. In such cases the diagnosis/symptom code is used with the corresponding external cause code.
>
> The observation codes are primarily to be used as a principal / first-listed diagnosis. An observation code may be assigned as a secondary diagnosis code when the patient is being observed for a condition that is ruled out and is unrelated to the principal/first-listed diagnosis. Also, when the principal diagnosis is required to be a code from category Z38, Liveborn infants according to place of birth and type of delivery, then a code from category Z05, Encounter for observation and evaluation of newborn for suspected diseases and conditions ruled out, is sequenced after the Z38 code. Additional codes may be used in addition to the observation code, but only if they are unrelated to the suspected condition being observed.
>
> Codes from subcategory Z03.7 , Encounter for suspected maternal and fetal conditions ruled out, may either be used as a first-listed or as an additional code assignment depending on the case. They are for use in very limited circumstances on a maternal record when an encounter is for a suspected maternal or fetal condition that is ruled out during that encounter (for example, a maternal or fetal condition may be suspected due to an abnormal test result). These codes should not be used when the condition is confirmed. In those cases, the confirmed condition should be coded. In addition, these codes are not for use if an illness or any signs or symptoms related to the suspected condition or problem are present. In such cases the diagnosis/symptom code is used.
>
> Additional codes may be used in addition to the code from subcategory Z03.7, but only if they are unrelated to the suspected condition being evaluated.
>
> Codes from subcategory Z03.7 may not be used for encounters for antenatal screening of mother. See Section I.C.21. Screening.
>
> For encounters for suspected fetal condition that are inconclusive following testing and evaluation, assign the appropriate code from category O35, O36, O40 or O41.
>
> The observation Z code categories:
>
> Z03
>
> Encounter for medical observation for suspected diseases and conditions ruled out
>
> Z04
>
> Encounter for examination and observation for other reasons Except: Z04.9, Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
>
> Z05
>
> Encounter for observation and evaluation of newborn for suspected diseases and conditions ruled out

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Observation Z Code Assignment Rules

**Key Behaviors:**
- Observation Z codes are used in very limited circumstances when a suspected condition is ruled out.
- Observation codes are not used if an injury, illness, or signs/symptoms related to the suspected condition are present.
- Observation codes are primarily used as a principal/first-listed diagnosis.
- Observation codes may be assigned as secondary diagnoses when unrelated to the principal diagnosis.
- Codes from subcategory Z03.7 may be used as first-listed or additional codes for suspected maternal/fetal conditions ruled out during an encounter.
- Codes from subcategory Z03.7 may be used as a first-listed diagnosis depending on the case.
- When the principal diagnosis must be from category Z38, a code from category Z05 is sequenced immediately after the Z38 code.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The suspected condition must be ruled out during the encounter.
- No injury, illness, or signs/symptoms related to the suspected condition must be present.
- Additional codes may only be used if they are unrelated to the suspected condition being observed.
- For suspected fetal conditions that are inconclusive, assign codes from categories O35, O36, O40, or O41 instead of Z codes.
- Codes from subcategory Z03.7 are not for use in encounters for antenatal screening of mother.
- Additional codes may only be used in addition to observation codes if they are unrelated to the suspected condition being observed or evaluated.

#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `787`

> **Actual Text**
>
> There are three observation Z code categories. They are for use in very limited circumstances when a person is being observed for a suspected condition that is ruled out. The observation codes are not for use if an injury or illness or any signs or symptoms related to the suspected condition are present. In such cases the diagnosis/symptom code is used with the corresponding external cause code.
>
> The observation codes are primarily to be used as a principal / first-listed diagnosis. An observation code may be assigned as a secondary diagnosis code when the patient is being observed for a condition that is ruled out and is unrelated to the principal/first-listed diagnosis. Also, when the principal diagnosis is required to be a code from category Z38, Liveborn infants according to place of birth and type of delivery, then a code from category Z05, Encounter for observation and evaluation of newborn for suspected diseases and conditions ruled out, is sequenced after the Z38 code. Additional codes may be used in addition to the observation code, but only if they are unrelated to the suspected condition being observed.
>
> Codes from subcategory Z03.7 , Encounter for suspected maternal and fetal conditions ruled out, may either be used as a first-listed or as an additional code assignment depending on the case. They are for use in very limited circumstances on a maternal record when an encounter is for a suspected maternal or fetal condition that is ruled out during that encounter (for example, a maternal or fetal condition may be suspected due to an abnormal test result). These codes should not be used when the condition is confirmed. In those cases, the confirmed condition should be coded. In addition, these codes are not for use if an illness or any signs or symptoms related to the suspected condition or problem are present. In such cases the diagnosis/symptom code is used.
>
> Additional codes may be used in addition to the code from subcategory Z03.7, but only if they are unrelated to the suspected condition being evaluated.
>
> Codes from subcategory Z03.7 may not be used for encounters for antenatal screening of mother. See Section I.C.21. Screening.
>
> For encounters for suspected fetal condition that are inconclusive following testing and evaluation, assign the appropriate code from category O35, O36, O40 or O41.
>
> The observation Z code categories:
>
> Z03
>
> Encounter for medical observation for suspected diseases and conditions ruled out
>
> Z04
>
> Encounter for examination and observation for other reasons Except: Z04.9, Encounter for examination and observation for unspecified reason
>
> Z05
>
> Encounter for observation and evaluation of newborn for suspected diseases and conditions ruled out

- Observation Z codes are used in very limited circumstances when a suspected condition is ruled out.
- Observation codes are not used if an injury, illness, or signs/symptoms related to the suspected condition are present.
- Observation codes are primarily used as a principal/first-listed diagnosis.
- Observation codes may be assigned as secondary diagnoses when unrelated to the principal diagnosis.
- Codes from subcategory Z03.7 may be used as first-listed or additional codes for suspected maternal/fetal conditions ruled out during an encounter.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The suspected condition must be ruled out during the encounter.
- No injury, illness, or signs/symptoms related to the suspected condition must be present.
- Additional codes may only be used if they are unrelated to the suspected condition being observed.
- For suspected fetal conditions that are inconclusive, assign codes from categories O35, O36, O40, or O41 instead of Z codes.
- Codes from subcategory Z03.7 are not for use in encounters for antenatal screening of mother.
- Codes from subcategory Z03.7 must not be used for encounters for antenatal screening of mother.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- The encounter must be for preoperative evaluations only.

- The reason for surgery must be documented as an additional diagnosis.

### 293. Zika Virus Diagnosis Coding Exception
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical Zika Virus Diagnosis Coding Exception logic with no behavioral differences between versions.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `79`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code only a confirmed diagnosis of Zika virus (A92.5, Zika virus disease) as documented by the provider. This is an exception to the hospital inpatient guideline Section II, H. In this context, 'confirmation' does not require documentation of the type of test performed; the provider's diagnostic statement that the condition is confirmed is sufficient. This code should be assigned regardless of the stated mode of transmission.
>
> If the provider documents "suspected", "possible" or "probable" Zika, do not assign code A92.5. Assign a code(s) explaining the reason for encounter (such as fever, rash, or joint pain) or Z20.821, Contact with and (suspected) exposure to Zika virus.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Zika Virus Diagnosis Coding Exception

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code only a confirmed diagnosis of Zika virus (A92.5) as documented by the provider.
- Confirmation does not require documentation of the type of test performed; the provider's diagnostic statement is sufficient.
- Assign code A92.5 regardless of the stated mode of transmission.
- Do not assign code A92.5 if the provider documents 'suspected', 'possible', or 'probable' Zika.
- For non-confirmed cases, assign a code explaining the reason for encounter (e.g., fever, rash) or Z20.821.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider must explicitly document the condition as 'confirmed'.
- If documentation indicates 'suspected', 'possible', or 'probable', code A92.5 is prohibited.
- Non-confirmed cases require an alternative code for symptoms or exposure (Z20.821).

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `505`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Code only a confirmed diagnosis of Zika virus (A92.5, Zika virus disease) as documented by the provider. This is an exception to the hospital inpatient guideline Section II, H. In this context, 'confirmation' does not require documentation of the type of test performed; the provider's diagnostic statement that the condition is confirmed is sufficient. This code should be assigned regardless of the stated mode of transmission.
>
> If the provider documents "suspected", "possible" or "probable" Zika, do not assign code A92.5. Assign a code(s) explaining the reason for encounter (such as fever, rash, or joint pain) or Z20.821, Contact with and (suspected) exposure to Zika virus.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Zika Virus Diagnosis Coding Exception

**Key Behaviors:**
- Code only a confirmed diagnosis of Zika virus (A92.5) as documented by the provider.
- Confirmation does not require documentation of the type of test performed; the provider's diagnostic statement is sufficient.
- Assign code A92.5 regardless of the stated mode of transmission.
- Do not assign code A92.5 if the provider documents 'suspected', 'possible', or 'probable' Zika.
- For non-confirmed cases, assign a code explaining the reason for encounter (e.g., fever, rash) or Z20.821.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Provider must explicitly document the condition as 'confirmed'.
- Documentation of the specific test type is not required for confirmation.
- Diagnosis must be documented as suspected, possible, or probable to trigger the exception (no A92.5).
- Alternative codes must explain the reason for encounter if Zika is not confirmed.
- Code Z20.821 is used for contact with or suspected exposure to Zika virus.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version contain identical text regarding the assignment of code A92.5 based solely on provider confirmation without test documentation. Both versions explicitly prohibit using A92.5 for 'suspected', 'possible', or 'probable' cases and mandate the use of symptom codes or Z20.821 for non-confirmed diagnoses. The raw text excerpts are verbatim matches, indicating that the coding logic has remained static between these two releases.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Code only a confirmed diagnosis of Zika virus (A92.5) as documented by the provider. -> October 2025 (New Version): Code only a confirmed diagnosis of Zika virus (A92.5) as documented by the provider.
