**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Z79 Code Assignment and tPA Transfer Reporting

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign Z79 codes for medications used prophylactically, for chronic conditions, or requiring lengthy treatment courses.
- Do not assign Z79 codes for medications administered for brief periods to treat acute illnesses or injuries.
- Assign code Z92.82 as a secondary diagnosis for patients transferred to a facility who received tPA within the last 24 hours prior to admission.
- Code Z92.82 applies to the receiving facility record only, not the transferring facility record.
- The condition for which tPA was administered must be assigned as the primary diagnosis before Z92.82.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Documentation must indicate the medication is for prophylaxis, chronic treatment, or a lengthy course.
- Documentation must explicitly exclude brief treatment of acute illness or injury for Z79 assignment.
- Documentation must confirm tPA administration occurred within 24 hours prior to admission to the current facility.
- The primary diagnosis must be the condition treated by the tPA administration.
- Z92.82 is restricted to the receiving facility's claim record.
- The guideline applies even if the patient is still receiving the tPA at the time they are received into the current facility.

---

### 297. Aftercare Encounter Coding Guidelines
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → ICD-10 Coding Guidelines  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the exact same feature regarding Aftercare Encounter Coding Guidelines with identical core logic and specific examples.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `356_357_358`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Aftercare visit codes cover situations when the initial treatment of a disease has been performed and the patient requires continued care during the healing or recovery phase, or for the long-term consequences of the disease. The aftercare Z code should not be used if treatment is directed at a current, acute disease. The diagnosis code is to be used in these cases. Exceptions to this rule are codes Z51.0, Encounter for antineoplastic radiation therapy, and codes from subcategory Z51.1, Encounter for antineoplastic chemotherapy and immunotherapy. These codes are to be first listed, followed by the diagnosis code when a patient's encounter is chiefly to receive radiation therapy, chemotherapy, or immunotherapy for the treatment of a neoplasm. If the reason for the encounter is more than one type of antineoplastic therapy, code Z51.0 and a code from subcategory Z51.1 may be assigned together, in which case one of these codes would be reported as a secondary diagnosis.
>
> The aftercare Z codes should also not be used for aftercare for injuries. For aftercare of an injury, assign the acute injury code with the appropriate 7 th character (for subsequent encounter).
>
> The aftercare codes are generally first listed to explain the specific reason for the encounter. An aftercare code may be used as an additional code when some type of aftercare is provided in addition to the reason for admission and no diagnosis code is applicable. An example of this would be the closure of a colostomy during an encounter for treatment of another condition.
>
> Aftercare codes should be used in conjunction with other aftercare codes or diagnosis codes to provide better detail on the specifics of an aftercare encounter visit, unless otherwise directed by the classification. The sequencing of multiple aftercare codes depends on the circumstances of the encounter.
>
> Certain aftercare Z code categories need a secondary diagnosis code to describe the resolving condition or sequelae. For others, the condition is included in the code title.
>
> Additional Z code aftercare category terms include fitting and adjustment, and attention to artificial openings.
>
> Status Z codes may be used with aftercare Z codes to indicate the nature of the aftercare. For example, code Z95.1, Presence of aortocoronary bypass graft, may be used with code Z48.812, Encounter for surgical aftercare following surgery on the circulatory system, to indicate the surgery for which the aftercare is being performed. A status code should not be used when the aftercare code indicates the type of status, such as using Z43.0, Encounter for attention to tracheostomy, with Z93.0, Tracheostomy status.
>
> {"The aftercare Z category/codes:": "Z42 Encounter for plastic and reconstructive surgery following medical procedure or healed injury"}
> {"The aftercare Z category/codes:": "Z43 Encounter for attention to artificial openings"}
> {"The aftercare Z category/codes:": "Z44 Encounter for fitting and adjustment of external prosthetic device"}
> [... +4 more table rows omitted ...]
>
> {"Z49": "Z51", "Encounter for care involving renal dialysis": "Encounter for other aftercare and medical care"}

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Aftercare Encounter Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Aftercare Z codes are used when initial treatment is complete and the patient requires continued care during healing or recovery, or for long-term consequences.
- Aftercare Z codes should not be used if treatment is directed at a current, acute disease; instead, the diagnosis code is used.
- Aftercare Z codes should not be used for aftercare of injuries; assign the acute injury code with the appropriate 7th character for subsequent encounter.
- Aftercare codes are generally first listed to explain the specific reason for the encounter, unless a diagnosis code is applicable.
- Status Z codes may be used with aftercare Z codes to indicate the nature of the aftercare, except when the aftercare code already indicates the type of status.
- An aftercare code may be used as an additional code when some type of aftercare is provided in addition to the reason for admission and no diagnosis code is applicable.
- The sequencing of multiple aftercare codes depends on the circumstances of the encounter.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- For encounters chiefly to receive radiation therapy, chemotherapy, or immunotherapy for a neoplasm, code Z51.0 or Z51.1 must be listed first, followed by the diagnosis code.
- If the reason for the encounter is more than one type of antineoplastic therapy, code Z51.0 and a code from subcategory Z51.1 may be assigned together, with one reported as a secondary diagnosis.
- Certain aftercare Z code categories require a secondary diagnosis code to describe the resolving condition or sequelae.
- Status codes should not be used when the aftercare code indicates the type of status (e.g., do not use Z43.0 with Z93.0).

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `788_789`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Aftercare visit codes cover situations when the initial treatment of a disease has been performed and the patient requires continued care during the healing or recovery phase, or for the long-term consequences of the disease. The aftercare Z code should not be used if treatment is directed at a current, acute disease. The diagnosis code is to be used in these cases.
>
> The aftercare Z codes should also not be used for aftercare for injuries. For aftercare of an injury, assign the acute injury code with the appropriate 7 th character (for subsequent encounter).
>
> The aftercare codes are generally first listed to explain the specific reason for the encounter. An aftercare code may be reported as an additional code when a specific type of aftercare is provided in addition to the reason for the encounter. An example of this would be the closure of a colostomy during an encounter for treatment of another condition.
>
> Aftercare codes should be used in conjunction with other aftercare codes or diagnosis codes to provide better detail on the specifics of an aftercare encounter visit, unless otherwise directed by the classification. The sequencing of multiple aftercare codes depends on the circumstances of the encounter.
>
> Certain aftercare Z code categories need a secondary diagnosis code to describe the resolving condition or sequelae. For others, the condition is included in the code title.
>
> Additional Z code aftercare category terms include fitting and adjustment, and attention to artificial openings.
>
> Status Z codes may be used with aftercare Z codes to indicate the nature of the aftercare. For example, code Z95.1, Presence of aortocoronary bypass graft, may be used with code Z48.812, Encounter for surgical aftercare following surgery on the circulatory system, to indicate the surgery for which the aftercare is being performed. A status code should not be used when the aftercare code indicates the type of status, such as using Z43.0, Encounter for attention to tracheostomy, with Z93.0, Tracheostomy status.
>
> {"The aftercare codes are generally found in the following categories :": "Z42 Encounter for plastic and reconstructive surgery following medical procedure or healed injury"}
> {"The aftercare codes are generally found in the following categories :": "Z43 Encounter for attention to artificial openings"}
> {"The aftercare codes are generally found in the following categories :": "Z44 Encounter for fitting and adjustment of external prosthetic device"}
> [... +1 more table rows omitted ...]

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Aftercare Encounter Coding Guidelines

**Key Behaviors:**
- Use aftercare Z codes for continued care during healing/recovery or long-term consequences after initial treatment.
- Do not use aftercare Z codes if treatment is directed at a current, acute disease; use the diagnosis code instead.
- Do not use aftercare Z codes for aftercare of injuries; assign the acute injury code with the appropriate 7th character for subsequent encounter.
- Generally list the aftercare Z code first to explain the reason for the encounter, unless a specific type of aftercare is provided in addition to another reason.
- Use status Z codes with aftercare Z codes to indicate the nature of the aftercare, unless the aftercare code already indicates the type of status.
- An aftercare code may be reported as an additional code when a specific type of aftercare is provided in addition to the reason for the encounter.
- The sequencing of multiple aftercare codes depends on the specific circumstances of the encounter.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Initial treatment of the disease must have been performed before assigning aftercare codes.
- Patient must require continued care during the healing or recovery phase, or for long-term consequences.
- For injury aftercare, the acute injury code must be assigned with the appropriate 7th character for subsequent encounter.
- Certain aftercare Z code categories require a secondary diagnosis code to describe the resolving condition or sequelae.
- Status codes should not be used when the aftercare code already indicates the type of status (e.g., Z43.0 with Z93.0).

### 298. Pathological Fracture Coding Sequencing
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Edge Cases & Standalone Topics  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for pathological fracture sequencing in neoplastic disease with no behavioral changes.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `119_289`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When an encounter is for a pathological fracture due to a neoplasm, and the focus of treatment is the fracture, a code from subcategory M84.5, Pathological fracture in neoplastic disease, should be sequenced first, followed by the code for the neoplasm.
>
> If the focus of treatment is the neoplasm with an associated pathological fracture, the neoplasm code should be sequenced first, followed by a code from M84.5 for the pathological fracture.
>
> Multiple fractures are sequenced in accordance with the severity of the fracture.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Pathological Fracture Coding Sequencing

**Key Behaviors:**
- Sequence M84.5 code first when the focus of treatment is the fracture due to neoplasm.
- Sequence neoplasm code first when the focus of treatment is the neoplasm with an associated pathological fracture.
- Sequence multiple fractures based on the severity of the fracture.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Determine the primary focus of treatment (fracture vs. neoplasm) to establish sequencing order.
- Assign code from subcategory M84.5 for pathological fracture in neoplastic disease.
- Assign appropriate code for the underlying neoplasm.

---

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Congenital Anomaly Exemption Logic

**Key Behaviors:**
- Assign 'Y' for congenital conditions and anomalies except for categories Q00-Q99
- Categories Q00-Q99 are on the exempt list and do not receive 'Y' assignment
- Congenital conditions are always considered present on admission

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be a congenital anomaly or condition
- Condition must not fall within categories Q00-Q99
- Condition must be documented as present on admission

### 300. Intrauterine and Delivery Condition Adjudication
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Edge Cases & Standalone Topics  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical adjudication logic for assigning POA status to intrauterine and delivery conditions in newborns.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `430`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Newborns are not considered to be admitted until after birth. Therefore, any condition present at birth or that developed in utero is considered present at admission and should be assigned 'Y'. This includes conditions that occur during delivery (e.g., injury during delivery, meconium aspiration, exposure to streptococcus B in the vaginal canal).

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Intrauterine and Delivery Condition Adjudication

**Key Behaviors:**
- Newborns are not considered admitted until after birth.
- Conditions present at birth or developed in utero are considered present at admission.
- Conditions occurring during delivery (e.g., injury, meconium aspiration, exposure) are assigned 'Y'.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be present at birth or have developed in utero.
- Condition must be present at admission for the newborn.

---

### 301. POA Assignment Logic for Obstetrical Conditions
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Edge Cases & Standalone Topics  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical POA assignment logic for obstetrical conditions with verbatim matching behaviors and examples.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `429`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Whether or not the patient delivers during the current hospitalization does not affect assignment of the POA indicator. The determining factor for POA assignment is whether the pregnancy complication or obstetrical condition described by the code was present at the time of admission or not.
>
> If the pregnancy complication or obstetrical condition was present on admission (e.g., patient admitted in preterm labor), assign 'Y'.
>
> If the pregnancy complication or obstetrical condition was not present on admission (e.g., 2 nd degree laceration during delivery, postpartum hemorrhage that occurred during current hospitalization, fetal distress develops after admission), assign 'N'.
>
> If the obstetrical code includes more than one diagnosis and any of the diagnoses identified by the code were not present on admission assign 'N'.
>
> (e.g., Category O11, Pre-existing hypertension with pre-eclampsia)

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** POA Assignment Logic for Obstetrical Conditions

**Key Behaviors:**
- Whether or not the patient delivers during the current hospitalization does not affect assignment of the POA indicator.
- The determining factor for POA assignment is whether the pregnancy complication or obstetrical condition described by the code was present at the time of admission or not.
- If the pregnancy complication or obstetrical condition was present on admission, assign 'Y'.
- If the pregnancy complication or obstetrical condition was not present on admission, assign 'N'.
- If the obstetrical code includes more than one diagnosis and any of the diagnoses identified by the code were not present on admission, assign 'N'.
- Examples of conditions assigned 'N' include 2nd degree laceration during delivery, postpartum hemorrhage occurring during the current hospitalization, and fetal distress developing after admission.
- Examples of conditions assigned 'Y' include patients admitted in preterm labor.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Assign 'Y' if the pregnancy complication or obstetrical condition was present on admission.
- Assign 'N' if the pregnancy complication or obstetrical condition was not present on admission.
- Assign 'N' if any diagnosis within a multi-diagnosis obstetrical code was not present on admission.

---
#### October 2025 (New Version) — Chunk `862`

> **Actual Text**
>
> Whether or not the patient delivers during the current hospitalization does not affect assignment of the POA indicator. The determining factor for POA assignment is whether the pregnancy complication or obstetrical condition described by the code was present at the time of admission or not.

> If the pregnancy complication or obstetrical condition was present on admission (e.g., patient admitted in preterm labor), assign 'Y'.
>

**Feature:** O9A Sequencing for Toxic Effects

- Then include additional code(s) specifying the condition caused by the poisoning, toxic effect, adverse effect, or underdosing

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Condition must be classified under subcategory O9A.2 (Injury, poisoning and certain other consequences of external causes complicating pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium)
- Documentation must support the causal link between the toxic effect and the resulting condition

### 305. Anemia Sequencing for Chemotherapy Adverse Effects
**Location:** Clinical Coding Standards → Edge Cases & Standalone Topics  
**LLM Relevance:** 🟢 10/10  
*Both profiles describe the identical coding logic for sequencing anemia codes resulting from chemotherapy and radiotherapy adverse effects.*

#### October 1, 2024 (Older) — Chunk `102`

> **Actual Text**
>
> When the admission/encounter is for management of an anemia associated with an adverse effect of the administration of chemotherapy or immunotherapy and the only treatment is for the anemia, the anemia code is sequenced first followed by the appropriate codes for the neoplasm and the adverse effect (T45.1X5 -, Adverse effect of antineoplastic and immunosuppressive drugs).
>
> When the admission/encounter is for management of an anemia associated with an adverse effect of radiotherapy, the anemia code should be sequenced first, followed by the appropriate neoplasm code and code Y84.2, Radiological procedure and radiotherapy as the cause of abnormal reaction of the patient, or of later complication, without mention of misadventure at the time of the procedure.

**Extracted Profile:**
**Feature:** Anemia Sequencing for Chemotherapy and Radiotherapy Adverse Effects

**Key Behaviors:**
- Sequence anemia code first when admission is for management of anemia associated with chemotherapy/immunotherapy adverse effects and only treatment is for anemia.
- Sequence anemia code first when admission is for management of anemia associated with radiotherapy adverse effects.
- Follow anemia code with appropriate neoplasm code and adverse effect code (T45.1X5-) for chemotherapy/immunotherapy cases.
- Follow anemia code with appropriate neoplasm code and code Y84.2 for radiotherapy cases.
- Use code Y84.2 specifically for radiotherapy as the cause of abnormal reaction without mention of misadventure.

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Admission or encounter must be for management of anemia associated with the adverse effect.
- For chemotherapy/immunotherapy, the only treatment provided must be for the anemia.
- For radiotherapy, the documentation must not mention misadventure at the time of the procedure.
- Appropriate neoplasm code must be assigned in both scenarios.
- Code Y84.2 must be used for radiotherapy adverse effects.

**Feature:** Anemia Sequencing for Chemotherapy and Radiotherapy Adverse Effects

**Key Behaviors:**
- Sequence anemia code first when admission is for management of anemia associated with chemotherapy adverse effect and only treatment is for anemia.
- Sequence anemia code first when admission is for management of anemia associated with radiotherapy adverse effect.
- Follow anemia code with appropriate neoplasm code and adverse effect code (T45.1X5- for chemotherapy) or cause code (Y84.2 for radiotherapy).
- Use Y84.2 specifically for radiotherapy as the cause of abnormal reaction without mention of misadventure.
- Ensure adverse effect codes are linked to the specific administration type (chemotherapy vs radiotherapy).

**Mandatory Coding Criteria:**
- Admission or encounter must be specifically for management of anemia.
- Anemia must be documented as associated with an adverse effect of chemotherapy or immunotherapy.
- Only treatment provided must be for the anemia (no other concurrent treatments for the underlying condition).
- For radiotherapy, code Y84.2 must be used to indicate the cause of the abnormal reaction.
- Neoplasm code must be included to identify the underlying condition associated with the adverse effect.
- The encounter must be for management of anemia associated with an adverse effect of immunotherapy.
- The encounter must be for management of anemia associated with an adverse effect of radiotherapy.

---
#### 📌 Delta: No Meaningful Change   |   Confidence: 🟢 High

The October 1, 2024 guideline and the October 2025 version present the exact same sequencing rules, requiring the anemia code to be listed first followed by neoplasm and adverse effect codes. The raw text excerpts are identical in both versions, indicating no substantive update to the clinical logic or coding requirements. While the extracted profile lists slightly more granular requirements in the new version, the core behavioral instructions and raw source text remain unchanged.

**Key Differences:**
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Sequence anemia code first when admission is for management of anemia associated with chemotherapy/immunotherapy adverse effects and only treatment is for anemia. -> October 2025 (New Version): Sequence anemia code first when admission is for management of anemia associated with chemotherapy adverse effect and only treatment is for anemia.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Follow anemia code with appropriate neoplasm code and adverse effect code (T45.1X5-) for chemotherapy/immunotherapy cases. -> October 2025 (New Version): Follow anemia code with appropriate neoplasm code and adverse effect code (T45.1X5- for chemotherapy) or cause code (Y84.2 for radiotherapy).
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Use code Y84.2 specifically for radiotherapy as the cause of abnormal reaction without mention of misadventure. -> October 2025 (New Version): Use Y84.2 specifically for radiotherapy as the cause of abnormal reaction without mention of misadventure.
- October 1, 2024 (Older): Raw text excerpt specifies 'chemotherapy or immunotherapy' and 'radiotherapy'. -> October 2025 (New Version): Raw text excerpt specifies 'chemotherapy or immunotherapy' and 'radiotherapy' (identical text).

*Traceability: Chunk `102` (October 1, 2024 (Older)) vs Chunk `531` (October 2025 (New Version))*

- Age is not the sole determining factor for classifying diabetes type.
